The Agentic Ecom Playbook

The Agentic Ecom Playbook

From Manual Marketing to Agentic Workflows with Claude Code

A comprehensive technical guide for media buyers and ecom store owners. Learn how to connect Claude Code to your Shopify store and Meta ad account, build reusable SOPs, convert them into one-command Skills, and orchestrate fully autonomous agentic workflows.

CRITICAL DISCLAIMER
This playbook teaches you to automate real operations on live platforms — Shopify stores with real products and prices, Meta ad accounts with real budgets and real money. Always test on a development store or with a minimal budget first. Never run untested automations against production data. The authors are not responsible for accidental price changes, runaway ad spend, deleted products, or any other unintended consequences. Start small, verify every output, and scale only after you trust the process.

Part 1: Foundation
Sections 1 -- 4

Install Claude Code, connect it to Shopify and Meta, and lay the groundwork for everything that follows.

Section 1: What You're Building & Why

IN THIS SECTION YOU WILL
  • Understand why manual marketing doesn't scale
  • See the five-layer automation stack you're building
  • Learn what Claude Code actually does and how it thinks
  • Map the journey from Section 1 to fully agentic workflows

The Problem: You're Doing Everything by Hand

Let's be honest about what your typical day looks like.

You wake up, grab your coffee, and open Meta Ads Manager. You need to launch a campaign for a new product drop. So you click "Create," pick your objective, name the campaign, set the budget, define your audience, choose placements, upload your creative, write the copy, add the URL, double-check the tracking pixel, and finally hit Publish. That's thirty minutes gone — for one campaign. If you're running separate campaigns for men and women, or testing different creatives, multiply that by three or four.

Then you switch to Shopify. You need to update prices on twenty products for a weekend sale. So you open each product page, scroll down to the variants, change the compare-at price, change the actual price, click Save, and move on to the next one. That's another hour of your life — clicking, typing, clicking, saving, clicking, typing, clicking, saving.

Need to check how your ads are performing? Open Ads Manager, set the date range, scroll through the data, export a CSV, open Google Sheets, paste it in, build a pivot table, create a chart. Another thirty minutes before you even start thinking about what to do with the numbers.

Want to create a new collection and sort it by best sellers? Open Shopify admin, go to Collections, click Create, define the rules, add products, arrange the order. Want to tag products for a seasonal campaign? One by one. Want to set up discount codes? One by one. Want to check inventory levels across your catalog? You guessed it — manually.

Here's the real problem, though. It's not just about time. It's about what lives in your head.

You know that your retargeting campaigns work best when you exclude purchasers from the last 14 days. You know that your winter boots audience should target ages 25 to 45. You know that your budget scaling rule is never more than 20 percent per day. You know that your image specs need to be 1080 by 1080 for feed placements.

But none of that is written down anywhere. It's tribal knowledge — locked inside your brain. If you get sick, go on vacation, or hire someone new, all of that expertise vanishes. Your replacement starts from zero, making the same mistakes you made two years ago.

This is the cycle that every media buyer and ecom store owner is stuck in: manual work, undocumented processes, no automation, no way to scale your knowledge.

The Solution: Tell the Computer What You Want, in Plain English

What if, instead of all that clicking, you could just say what you want?

Imagine opening a terminal on your computer and typing:

"Create a conversion campaign for my new winter boots collection. Set the daily budget to 500 EGP. Target men aged 25 to 45 in Egypt. Use the product images from my catalog. Set it to PAUSED so I can review before it goes live."

And then watching as the system creates the campaign, configures the ad set with the right targeting, links the creative to your product catalog, adds UTM tracking parameters to the URL, and pauses everything for your review — all in under two minutes.

That's not a fantasy. That's what you're building in this playbook.

The tool that makes this possible is called Claude Code. It's an AI assistant that runs on your computer, connects to the same APIs (Application Programming Interfaces — the behind-the-scenes connections) that Meta Ads Manager and Shopify Admin use, and executes your instructions step by step.

When you click "Create Campaign" in Ads Manager, your browser sends a request to Meta's servers through their API. Claude Code sends the exact same type of request — it just skips the clicking. When you edit a product in Shopify Admin, the browser sends a request to Shopify's API. Claude Code does the same thing — without the browser, without the clicks, without the waiting.

The difference is that you describe what you want in words, and the system handles the details.

But it goes further than just saving time. Every task you automate gets saved as a Standard Operating Procedure — a written set of steps that anyone can follow or the system can repeat. Your knowledge stops living in your head and starts living in documented, repeatable, improvable instructions. When something goes wrong, the system records the error and the fix, so it never makes the same mistake twice.

This is what "agentic" means: a system that doesn't just respond to commands one at a time, but can carry out multi-step tasks on its own, make decisions along the way, handle errors, and learn from the results.

Before vs After

Here's what the difference looks like in practice.

BEFORE: Creating a Campaign Manually

Step 1:  Open Meta Ads Manager                   [ 5 min]
Step 2:  Click Create, choose campaign objective  [ 2 min]
Step 3:  Name campaign, set budget strategy       [ 1 min]
Step 4:  Set daily or lifetime budget             [ 1 min]
Step 5:  Configure audience targeting             [10 min]
Step 6:  Choose placements                        [ 2 min]
Step 7:  Upload creative, write ad copy           [ 5 min]
Step 8:  Set destination URL with UTM parameters  [ 3 min]
Step 9:  Review everything, fix mistakes          [ 5 min]
Step 10: Publish                                  [ 1 min]
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Total: ~35 minutes per campaign
Total for 4 variations: ~2 hours 20 minutes

AFTER: Creating a Campaign with Claude Code

You: "Create a conversion campaign for winter boots,
      500 EGP/day budget, men 25-45, Egypt.
      Use catalog images. Set status to PAUSED."

Claude Code: Done. Here's what I created:

      Campaign created       — "Winter Boots - Men - Conversions"
      Ad set configured      — Men 25-45, Egypt, automatic placements
      Creative linked        — Catalog product images attached
      UTM tracking added     — source=meta, medium=paid, campaign=winter-boots
      Status set to PAUSED   — Ready for your review

      Campaign ID: 120212345678901234
      Review it here: https://adsmanager.facebook.com/...
─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Total: ~2 minutes
Total for 4 variations: ~8 minutes

And updating prices for a seasonal sale?

BEFORE: Updating 20 Products Manually

For each product:
  Open product page → scroll to variants → edit price →
  edit compare-at price → Save → repeat

Total: ~60 minutes for 20 products

AFTER: Updating 20 Products with Claude Code

You: "Apply a 25% discount to all products in the
      Winter Collection. Set the compare-at price
      to the original, and round to the nearest 10 EGP."

Claude Code: Done. 20 products updated:
      - 47 variants repriced
      - Compare-at prices preserved
      - All prices rounded to nearest 10 EGP
      - Summary saved to reports/price-update-log.json

Total: ~90 seconds
KEY TAKEAWAY
The shift isn't about replacing you — it's about removing the mechanical work so you can focus on strategy, creativity, and decisions that actually grow your business.

What You'll Have By the End of This Playbook

When you finish working through all thirteen sections, here's what you'll walk away with:

  • Claude Code fully connected to your Shopify store and Meta ad account — authenticated, tested, and ready to take instructions in plain English.

  • A library of 10 Standard Operating Procedures covering your most important daily tasks across both platforms — from creating campaigns to managing inventory to updating prices.

  • 17 ready-to-use Claude Skills that you can trigger with simple commands like /create-campaign, /check-performance, /update-prices, or /daily-ops. Each skill is a reusable automation you install once and use forever.

  • Agentic workflows that chain multiple operations together — for example, a single command that checks your inventory, pauses ads for out-of-stock products, and sends you a summary.

  • A self-improving system that records errors and their fixes, so every problem you encounter makes the system smarter. The next time it hits the same issue, it already knows what to do.

  • A memory framework that stores what works and what doesn't — your best-performing audiences, your proven budget strategies, your pricing rules — all documented and accessible to Claude Code in future sessions.

  • Browser automation that can verify your work visually — taking screenshots of your storefront, checking that ads look right in Ads Manager, and confirming that price changes went through correctly.

You won't need to write code. The playbook gives you everything pre-built. You'll copy skill files into a folder, fill in your store credentials, and start giving instructions in plain English.

Prerequisites Checklist

Before you begin Section 2, make sure you have the following ready:

☐ A computer — Mac, Windows, or Linux. Any modern machine will work. You don't need anything powerful.
☐ A Shopify store on any paid plan — This can be a live store or a development store. If you don't have one yet, you can create a free development store through the Shopify Partners program (we'll cover this briefly in Section 3).
☐ A Meta Business account with an active ad account — You need a Business Manager (business.facebook.com) with at least one ad account. If you've ever run a Facebook or Instagram ad, you already have this.
☐ A Claude subscription — You need a Pro, Max, or Team plan. The free tier of Claude does not include Claude Code. If you don't have one yet, visit claude.ai to subscribe.
☐ About 2 hours of focused time for initial setup — Sections 2 through 4 walk you through installing Claude Code and connecting it to Shopify and Meta. After that, each section takes 15 to 30 minutes.
☐ Basic comfort with following step-by-step instructions — You do not need coding experience. You do not need to understand programming. Every step tells you exactly what to type and what you should see. If you can follow a recipe, you can follow this playbook.

That's it. No special hardware, no programming background, no technical certifications. If you can run an online store and manage ad campaigns, you already have the skills you need. This playbook just gives you better tools.

How This Playbook Is Organized

A few things to know before you dive in:

Sections build on each other. Do them in order, at least the first time through. Section 2 installs Claude Code. Section 3 connects Shopify. Section 4 connects Meta. Every section after that assumes those connections are in place.

Every section from Section 3 onwards gives you a downloadable Claude Skill. These are pre-written instruction files that you drop into a folder on your computer. Once installed, you can trigger them by typing a simple command like /create-campaign or /check-inventory. You don't need to write these yourself — just copy and paste.

Look for the Skill Download boxes. They look like this:

⚡SKILL DOWNLOAD
Campaign Creator
What it does: Creates a complete Meta campaign — objective, budget, audience, creative — via a single command.
Tell Claude Code: "Install the media-buying-sop-pack skill from https://raw.githubusercontent.com/mazinzaki911/agentic-ecom-playbook/main/skills/media-buying-sop-pack.SKILL.md"
Type /create-campaign in Claude Code
  • Walks you through campaign setup interactively
  • Generates complete Meta API v25.0 code
  • Validates all parameters before submission
↓ Download Skill
ads-manager-campaign-created

Troubleshooting sections appear at the end of each section. If something doesn't work, check there first. The most common issues — expired tokens, wrong permissions, typos in configuration — are all covered with clear solutions.

The playbook is split into five parts:

Part Sections What It Covers
1. Foundation 1 -- 4 Setup: install tools, connect Shopify, connect Meta
2. Tooling 5 -- 6 Plugins, memory, error handling, self-improvement
3. Knowledge 7 -- 8 Reading API docs, writing SOPs
4. Operations 9 -- 10 10 ready-made SOPs for media buying and Shopify
5. Going Agentic 11 -- 13 Skills, workflows, strategy automation

By Part 4, you'll be running real operations through Claude Code. By Part 5, you'll have fully autonomous workflows handling multi-step tasks on their own.

REAL TALK
You might be thinking "this sounds too good to be true." It's not magic — it's structured automation. You still make the decisions. Claude Code just executes them faster and more consistently than you ever could by hand.
ACHIEVEMENT UNLOCKED
You now understand the full vision — from manual marketing to agentic workflows. You know what you're building and why it matters. Everything from here is execution.

Ready? Let's get started. Turn the page to Section 2, where you'll install Claude Code on your computer and run your first command.

Section 2: Installing Claude Code

Time required: 15-30 minutes Difficulty: Beginner -- no prior experience needed What you will have at the end: A working Claude Code installation, ready to automate your store
IN THIS SECTION YOU WILL
  • Open the terminal for the first time (it's not scary)
  • Install Homebrew, Node.js, and Claude Code
  • Set up your project folder and first configuration files
  • Run your first Claude Code command and see it work

Before you can give Claude instructions, you need to install it on your computer. This section walks you through every single step -- from the very first thing you type in the terminal to confirming everything works.

If you have never opened a terminal before, that is perfectly fine. By the end of this section, you will feel comfortable with it.

2.1 Prerequisites: Mac

You need three pieces of software installed before Claude Code will work: Homebrew, Node.js, and Git. Think of these as the foundation -- Claude Code sits on top of them.

What Is Homebrew?

Homebrew is a package manager for Mac. A package manager is like an app store you use from the terminal -- instead of going to a website, downloading an installer, and double-clicking it, you type one short command and the software installs itself.

Almost every developer tool on Mac is installed through Homebrew. Once you have it, installing anything else becomes a one-liner.

Installing Homebrew

Step 1: Open Terminal.

Press Cmd + Space on your keyboard. A search bar (called Spotlight) appears in the center of your screen. Type the word Terminal and press Enter.

A window will open with a blinking cursor. This is the terminal -- a text-based way to talk to your computer. Everything you type here is a command, and when you press Enter, your computer executes it.

Step 2: Paste the install command.

Copy this entire line, paste it into the terminal, and press Enter:

/bin/bash -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Homebrew/install/HEAD/install.sh)"

What does this do? It downloads the Homebrew installer from the internet and runs it. The letters after the dashes (-fsSL) are options that tell the download tool to follow redirects and stay quiet unless there is an error.

Step 3: Enter your Mac password.

The installer will ask for your Mac password -- the same one you type when you log in to your computer or install any app. When you type it, no characters will appear on screen. No dots, no asterisks, nothing. This is a security feature, not a bug. Just type your password and press Enter.

Step 4: Wait.

Installation takes 2-5 minutes. You will see text scrolling in the terminal -- that is normal. Let it finish on its own.

Step 5: Follow the "Next steps" instructions.

This is the step most people miss. When the installation finishes, Homebrew prints a message at the bottom that says "Next steps" with one or two commands you need to run. These commands add Homebrew to something called your PATH.

Your PATH is a list of places your computer looks when you type a command. If Homebrew is not on that list, your computer will not find it even though it is installed. Copy and paste those commands exactly as shown, pressing Enter after each one.

The commands will look something like this (your exact text may differ):

echo >> /Users/yourname/.zprofile
echo 'eval "$(/opt/homebrew/bin/brew shellenv)"' >> /Users/yourname/.zprofile
eval "$(/opt/homebrew/bin/brew shellenv)"

Step 6: Verify it worked.

Close the Terminal window entirely (Cmd + Q), then open a brand new Terminal window using Spotlight (Cmd + Space, type "Terminal", press Enter). Type:

brew --version

You should see something like Homebrew 4.x.x. If you see "command not found" instead, go back to Step 5 -- the PATH commands were likely missed.

What Is Node.js?

Your automation scripts are written in JavaScript -- the same language that makes websites interactive. Normally, JavaScript only runs inside a web browser. Node.js lets JavaScript run directly on your computer, outside of a browser. That is what makes it possible to write scripts that talk to Shopify, Meta, and other platforms.

Installing Node.js

In your terminal, type:

brew install node

This tells Homebrew to download and install Node.js. It will take 1-2 minutes.

When it finishes, verify both Node.js and npm are installed:

node --version

You should see something like v20.x.x or v22.x.x (the exact number does not matter as long as it is 20 or higher).

npm --version

You should see something like 10.x.x.

What is npm? npm stands for Node Package Manager. It installs JavaScript libraries -- small bundles of code that other people have written so you do not have to. For example, instead of writing code to connect to the Shopify API from scratch, you install a library that does it for you with npm.

What Is Git?

Git is a version control tool. It tracks every change you make to your files -- like the "Track Changes" feature in Microsoft Word, but for an entire folder of code. If you ever make a mistake, you can go back to a previous version.

Git also lets you push your project to the cloud (using services like GitHub) so you have a backup and can collaborate with others.

Installing Git

Most Macs already have Git installed (it comes with Apple's developer tools). Check first:

git --version

If you see a version number (like git version 2.x.x), you are good -- skip to section 2.3.

If you see "command not found" or a popup asking you to install developer tools, install Git through Homebrew:

brew install git

Verify again:

git --version

2.2 Prerequisites: Windows

Windows users need the same three tools, but the installation process is slightly different because Windows does not come with Homebrew. You will download installers from websites instead.

Which Terminal to Use on Windows

Windows has several terminal options. Here is the plain-English version:

Terminal What It Is When to Use It
PowerShell The modern Windows terminal. Already on your computer. Use this for everything in this guide.
Git Bash A mini Linux terminal that comes with Git. Useful when following online tutorials that show Mac/Linux commands.
WSL (Windows Subsystem for Linux) A full Linux environment running inside Windows. For advanced users only. Not needed for this playbook.

For this guide, use PowerShell. To open it: click the Start menu, type PowerShell, and click "Windows PowerShell."

Installing Git for Windows

Step 1: Open your web browser and go to:

https://git-scm.com/downloads/win

Step 2: Download the installer. It will be a file like Git-2.x.x-64-bit.exe.

Step 3: Run the installer. Accept all the default settings except for these two screens:

  • "Choosing the default editor" -- Select "Use Notepad". This is the simplest option. (The default, Vim, is notoriously confusing for beginners.)
  • "Adjusting your PATH environment" -- Select "Git from the command line and also from 3rd-party software". This ensures Git works everywhere, not just inside Git Bash.

For every other screen, just click "Next" until you reach "Install."

Step 4: After installation finishes, open PowerShell and verify:

git --version

You should see git version 2.x.x.

Bonus: Installing Git also gives you Git Bash, which appears in your Start menu. This is a Unix-like terminal that understands the same commands Mac and Linux users type. You do not need it for this guide, but it is handy to have.

Installing Node.js on Windows

Step 1: Open your browser and go to:

https://nodejs.org

Step 2: You will see two big download buttons. Click the one that says LTS (Long Term Support) -- it will be green. LTS means this version is stable and well-tested, which is exactly what you want.

Step 3: Run the downloaded installer (a .msi file).

Step 4: Click through the installer. When you see a checkbox that says "Automatically install the necessary tools", make sure it is checked. This installs additional components that some npm packages need.

Step 5: After the installer finishes, open a new PowerShell window (close the old one first -- the old window will not know Node.js was just installed). Then verify:

node --version
npm --version

You should see version numbers for both. Node should be v20.x.x or higher, and npm should be 10.x.x or higher.

Why a new PowerShell window? When you install software, it updates your system's PATH (the list of places the computer looks for commands). An already-open terminal loaded the old PATH when it started. Opening a new one picks up the changes.

Optional: Chocolatey (Windows Package Manager)

If you liked the Homebrew approach on Mac and want a similar experience on Windows, Chocolatey is the Windows equivalent. It is entirely optional -- the manual downloads above work perfectly.

If you want to try it, open PowerShell as Administrator (right-click PowerShell in the Start menu and choose "Run as administrator") and run:

Set-ExecutionPolicy Bypass -Scope Process -Force; [System.Net.ServicePointManager]::SecurityProtocol = [System.Net.ServicePointManager]::SecurityProtocol -bor 3072; iex ((New-Object System.Net.WebClient).DownloadString('https://community.chocolatey.org/install.ps1'))

Then you can install both tools with:

choco install nodejs git

Again, this is optional. Skip it if you already installed Node.js and Git using the methods above.

2.3 Installing Claude Code

This is the main event. All the setup above was preparation -- now you install the tool you will actually use every day.

Mac and Linux

Open your terminal and paste:

curl -fsSL https://claude.ai/install.sh | bash

What does this do? It downloads the Claude Code installer script from Anthropic's website and runs it. The | character (called a "pipe") takes the output of the download and feeds it directly into bash (your terminal's command processor) to execute it.

Windows (PowerShell)

Open PowerShell and paste:

irm https://claude.ai/install.ps1 | iex

What does this do? Same idea, Windows syntax. irm means "Invoke-RestMethod" (download something from the internet). iex means "Invoke-Expression" (run whatever was downloaded).

Wait and Verify

Installation takes 30-60 seconds. When it finishes, verify by typing:

claude --version

You should see a version number like 2.x.x. If you see "command not found," close your terminal, open a new one, and try again.

Important: Claude Code may ask you to fix your PATH.

During or after installation, you might see a message like this: echo 'export PATH="$HOME/.claude/bin:$PATH"' >> ~/.zshrc

This adds Claude Code's folder to your terminal's search list. Copy the exact command shown, paste it, then close your terminal entirely (Cmd + Q on Mac) and open a new one. The claude command should now work.

That is it. One command, and Claude Code is on your machine.

2.4 First Launch and Project Setup

Now that Claude Code is installed, you need to set up a project folder -- a dedicated directory where all your automation scripts, data files, and configuration will live.

Create Your Project Folder

Open your terminal and type:

mkdir my-store-automation

What does mkdir mean? It stands for "make directory." A directory is just a folder. This command creates a new empty folder called my-store-automation inside whatever folder your terminal is currently in (usually your home folder).

Now move into that folder:

cd my-store-automation

What does cd mean? It stands for "change directory." Think of it as double-clicking a folder to open it. After running this command, everything you do in the terminal happens inside my-store-automation.

Initialize the Project

Run these two commands, one at a time:

npm init -y

This creates a file called package.json inside your project folder. Think of package.json as a manifest -- it is a list that tracks which JavaScript libraries your project depends on. The -y flag means "yes to all defaults" so it does not ask you a bunch of questions.

npm install dotenv

This installs a library called dotenv. Its job is simple but important: it reads secret credentials (like API keys and passwords) from a special file called .env and makes them available to your scripts. You will create that .env file in the next section.

Why not just put credentials directly in your scripts? Because if you ever share your scripts (or accidentally upload them to the internet), your passwords would be exposed. The .env file keeps secrets separate from code, and you tell Git to ignore it so it never gets shared.

Launch Claude Code for the First Time

While still inside your project folder, type:

claude

Here is what happens:

  1. Your web browser opens automatically. Claude Code needs to verify who you are.
  2. Log in with your Claude account. You need an active plan -- Claude Pro, Max, or Team. If you do not have one yet, sign up at claude.ai.
  3. Approve the connection. The browser will ask you to confirm that this terminal session is allowed to use your Claude account. Click "Approve" or "Allow."
  4. Return to your terminal. Claude Code is now connected and waiting for your input.

You will see Claude Code's interface in the terminal -- a prompt where you can type natural language instructions, just like chatting with Claude on the web, except now it can read your files, run commands, and build things on your computer.

Test That Everything Works

Type the following into Claude Code:

What files are in this directory?

Claude Code should respond by listing the files in your project folder (you will see package.json, node_modules/, and package-lock.json from the npm install you did earlier). If it responds, congratulations -- everything is connected and working.

Understanding Your Workspace

A few important things to know about how Claude Code operates:

It works inside your project folder. Claude Code runs in a "working directory" -- the folder you launched it from. It can read files in this folder, create new ones, and run commands. It cannot access files outside this folder unless you specifically tell it to.

It asks for permission. Claude Code will not silently run commands or change files. Before it does anything, it shows you what it wants to do and asks you to approve. You will see prompts like:

Allow Bash: npm install axios?  [Y/n]

Press Enter (or type Y) to approve, or type N to reject. This safety feature means Claude Code never does anything behind your back.

It remembers context within a session. As long as your terminal session is open, Claude Code remembers what you have discussed. If you ask it to create a file and then ask it to modify that file, it knows what you are talking about. When you close the terminal and start a new session, it starts fresh -- but your files remain on disk.

What Is a .env File?

You will hear about .env files a lot in this playbook, so let us explain it now.

A .env file is a plain text file that stores secret credentials -- API keys, access tokens, passwords, and any other sensitive information your scripts need to connect to services like Shopify and Meta.

It looks like this:

SHOPIFY_STORE=my-store.myshopify.com
SHOPIFY_ACCESS_TOKEN=shpat_abc123...
META_ACCESS_TOKEN=EAABx...

The .env file lives in your project folder, but you tell Git to ignore it (using a .gitignore file) so it never gets uploaded or shared. Your scripts read it using the dotenv library you just installed. This keeps your secrets safe and separate from your code.

You will create your actual .env file in Section 3, when you connect your Shopify store.

Quick Troubleshooting

Problem Solution
command not found: brew You missed the "Next steps" after Homebrew installed. Re-run the installer or manually add Homebrew to your PATH.
command not found: node Close the terminal and open a new one. If still missing, reinstall Node.js.
command not found: claude Close the terminal and open a new one. If still missing, re-run the install command from section 2.3.
Password not appearing when I type That is normal. It is hidden for security. Just type it and press Enter.
npm ERR! code EACCES (permission error on Mac) Do not use sudo. Instead, fix npm permissions: mkdir ~/.npm-global && npm config set prefix '~/.npm-global' and add ~/.npm-global/bin to your PATH.
Browser does not open when launching Claude Copy the URL shown in the terminal, paste it into your browser manually, and complete the login.

What You Have Now

Take a moment to appreciate what you just set up:

  • Homebrew (Mac) or direct installers (Windows) -- your software installation toolkit
  • Node.js and npm -- the engine that runs your automation scripts and installs their dependencies
  • Git -- version control that tracks every change and protects against mistakes
  • Claude Code -- your AI-powered automation assistant, installed and authenticated
  • A project folder (my-store-automation) -- initialized and ready for scripts

This is the foundation everything else builds on. You will not need to repeat any of these steps.

KEY TAKEAWAY
The terminal is just a way to type instructions instead of clicking buttons. Once you're comfortable with it, everything else in this playbook becomes faster.
REAL TALK
If the terminal felt intimidating, you're not alone. Most media buyers have never opened one. But now that you've done it once, the hard part is behind you.
ACHIEVEMENT UNLOCKED
Claude Code is installed, authenticated, and your project folder is ready. You have a working development environment — the same setup used by professional developers.

What Is Next

In Section 3: Connecting Shopify, you will create your first API connection and get your first downloadable Claude Skill -- a pre-built automation that connects to your Shopify store and verifies everything is working.

Section 3: Connecting Shopify via Dev Dashboard

Part 1 -- Getting Set Up | Estimated reading time: 12 minutes
IN THIS SECTION YOU WILL
  • Create a Shopify Developer App with the right permissions
  • Generate API credentials (Client ID and Client Secret)
  • Store credentials securely in a .env file
  • Install the Project Scaffolder and Shopify Connector skills

By the end of this section, your Claude Code environment will be able to talk to your Shopify store -- reading products, checking inventory, updating prices, and more. We will walk through every click, every field, and every decision so nothing is left to guesswork.

3.0 Prerequisites: You Need a Shopify Partners Account

Before you touch any settings, you need a Shopify Partners account. This is free and separate from your regular Shopify store login. The Partners account gives you access to the Dev Dashboard (dev.shopify.com) where you will create your automation app.

If you do not have one yet:

  1. Go to partners.shopify.com
  2. Click "Join now" and sign up (it is free -- no credit card required)
  3. Once your Partners account is created, you will be able to access the Dev Dashboard at dev.shopify.com

Tip: If you already manage a Shopify store, use the same email address you use for your store admin. This makes it easier to link your Partners account to your existing store later.

Without a Partners account, you will not be able to create a Custom App in the Dev Dashboard, and the steps in this section will not work. Take two minutes to set this up now if you have not already.

3.1 Dev Stores vs Managed Stores

Before we touch a single setting, you need to know which kind of Shopify store you are working with. Shopify has two types, and the installation path is slightly different for each.

Dev Store

A dev store is a free practice environment that Shopify gives to developers and agencies. No real customers, no real money, no paid plan required. Think of it as a sandbox -- a safe place to experiment without breaking anything. You can create as many dev stores as you want from the Dev Dashboard.

Who has a dev store? Agencies, freelancers, and developers who are building or testing something before it goes live on a client's real store.

Managed Store

A managed store is your actual, live Shopify store running on a paid plan. This is where your real products, real customers, and real orders live. Because real money is involved, Shopify adds an extra security step (called "distribution") before you can install a custom app.

Who has a managed store? If you are a store owner managing your own shop -- or you are working directly inside a client's existing store -- you have a managed store.

Which Path Should You Follow?

Your situation Store type Installation path
I own the store and manage it myself Managed store Path B (Section 3.4)
I am an agency building for a client Dev store first, then managed store Both paths
I am a developer testing before going live Dev store Path A (Section 3.4)
I am not sure Check your Shopify admin -- if you are on a paid plan with real products, it is a managed store Likely Path B

Do not worry about memorizing the difference. When we reach the installation step, you will pick one path and follow it. The rest of the section is identical for both.

3.2 Create the App in the Dev Dashboard

This is where we create the "identity" that your automation scripts will use when talking to Shopify.

Step-by-step

  1. Open your browser and go to dev.shopify.com
  2. Alternatively, from your Shopify Admin: go to Settings (bottom-left) then Apps then click "Build apps in Dev Dashboard"
  3. If prompted, log in with your Shopify account
  4. Click the "Create app" button
  5. Give your app a name -- something descriptive like "Store Automation" or "Claude Automation"
  6. Click "Create"

That is it. Your app now exists.

What You Get: Client ID and Client Secret

After creating the app, Shopify gives you two pieces of information on the app's settings page:

  • Client ID -- a long string starting with something like shp_ci_...
  • Client Secret -- another long string starting with something like shp_cs_...

These are NOT access tokens. This is a very common point of confusion, so let us clear it up with an analogy:

Concept Real-world analogy
Client ID Your app's username -- it tells Shopify who is asking for access
Client Secret Your app's password -- it proves you are really you
Access Token The keycard to a specific store -- you will get this automatically later, through code

The Client ID and Secret identify your app. The access token is what actually opens the door to a specific store. You never copy an access token from the browser. Instead, your scripts will request a fresh token automatically every time they run. (More on this in Section 3.5.)

Save Your Credentials

Copy both values -- the Client ID and the Client Secret -- and save them somewhere secure right now. A password manager is ideal. You will need them in a few minutes when we set up your project's configuration file.

Security note: The Client Secret is exactly what it sounds like -- secret. Never share it in a chat message, never paste it into a public document, and never commit it to version control (Git). We will set up protections for this in the next section.

3.3 Configure Scopes and URLs

Still in the Dev Dashboard, we need to tell Shopify two things: where your app "lives" and what it is allowed to do.

App URL

Since we are building automation scripts (not a web app with buttons and a user interface), there is no real URL to point to. Shopify requires one anyway, so use their built-in placeholder:

https://shopify.dev/apps/default-app-home

Paste this into the App URL field in the Configuration tab. This tells Shopify "this app does not have a web interface" -- and that is perfectly fine for our purposes.

API Scopes (Permissions)

Scopes are permissions. Each scope grants your app the ability to read or write a specific type of data. Without the right scopes, your scripts will get "access denied" errors.

Go to your app's Configuration tab and select the scopes you need. Here is a starter set that covers most ecommerce automation tasks:

Scope What it allows Why you need it
read_products View products, variants, and images Fetching your catalog data
write_products Create, update, and delete products Updating titles, prices, tags, images
read_orders View orders and transactions Sales reporting, order analysis
read_customers View customer information Customer segmentation, export lists
read_inventory Check stock levels at each location Stock-based automations and alerts
write_inventory Update stock quantities Sync inventory from external systems
read_price_rules View discounts and price rules Auditing active promotions
write_price_rules Create and manage discounts Automated sale pricing
read_content View pages and blog posts Content auditing
write_content Create and update pages Publishing content programmatically

A practical tip: Only select the scopes you actually need right now. You can always add more later by releasing a new version of the app (explained next). Starting with fewer permissions is both safer and simpler.

Release a Version

This is the step people most often forget -- and then spend an hour wondering why nothing works.

After configuring your scopes and URLs, you must release a version to make those settings active. Think of it like saving a document: until you click save, your changes exist only as a draft.

  1. Click the "Release" button (usually at the top-right of the Configuration page)
  2. Optionally give it a name like "v1 - Initial setup"
  3. Click "Release" to confirm

Your app configuration is now live. If you ever change scopes in the future, you will need to release a new version for those changes to take effect.

Key point: Releasing a version does NOT install the app on any store. It just "publishes" the configuration. Installation is a separate step (coming up next).

3.4 Installing the App on Your Store

This is where the two paths diverge. Pick the one that matches your situation.

Path A: Installing on a Dev Store (simple)

If you are working with a dev store, installation is straightforward:

  1. In the Dev Dashboard, go to your app
  2. Click "Install" (or "Test on dev store")
  3. Shopify shows you the permissions your app is requesting -- review them
  4. Click "Approve"
  5. Done. Your app is now installed.

That is all there is to it. Skip ahead to Section 3.5.

Path B: Installing on a Managed Store (requires distribution)

Managed stores (real, paid stores) require an extra step called distribution. This is Shopify's way of making sure random apps cannot install themselves on live stores without permission.

Here is the process:

  1. In the Dev Dashboard, go to your app and click Distribution in the left sidebar
  2. Choose "Custom" distribution
  3. Do NOT choose "Public" -- that is for listing your app on the Shopify App Store, which is not what we want
  4. Enter your managed store's domain: your-store.myshopify.com
  5. Optional: Uncheck "Allow multi-store installs" if this app is only for one store (recommended for most store owners)
  6. Click "Generate link"
  7. Copy the installation link that Shopify generates
  8. Open that link in a browser where you are already logged into your Shopify admin
  9. Shopify shows a confirmation page listing the permissions your app is requesting
  10. Review the permissions and click "Install"
  11. Done. Your app is now installed on your managed store.

Important warning: The distribution method you choose is permanent. Once you select "Custom," you cannot switch to "Public" later. For automation scripts on your own store, Custom is always the right choice. This restriction only matters if you were planning to list your app for other merchants to install -- which is not what this playbook is about.

Confirming the Installation

After installation (regardless of which path you took), you can verify everything worked:

  1. Go to your Shopify Admin
  2. Navigate to Settings (bottom-left corner)
  3. Click Apps and sales channels
  4. Your app should appear in the list

If you see it, you are good. If not, double-check that you followed every step above and that you released a version before attempting installation.

3.5 The Client Credentials Grant (How Tokens Work)

This section explains the single most important concept in connecting to Shopify. Read it carefully -- understanding this will save you hours of confusion down the road.

The Old Way (Before 2025)

In the past, when you created a custom app in Shopify, you would go to a page in your admin panel, and there would be an access token sitting right there. You would copy it, paste it into your code, and you were done. Simple. But not very secure -- that token never expired, and if it leaked, anyone could access your store.

The New Way (Dev Dashboard)

With the Dev Dashboard, there is no token to copy from a browser page. Instead, your code requests a fresh token every time it needs one. Here is how it works:

  1. Your script sends the Client ID and Client Secret to Shopify
  2. Shopify verifies them and sends back a temporary access token
  3. That token is valid for 24 hours (86,399 seconds, to be exact)
  4. When the token expires, your script automatically requests a new one

This is called the client credentials grant -- a standard security pattern used across the internet. The "grant" part just means "a way of getting permission."

Why is this better? Because even if a token somehow leaks, it stops working within 24 hours. And since tokens are generated on-the-fly, there is nothing static to steal from a configuration file.

Your .env File

A .env file (pronounced "dot env") is a simple text file that stores configuration values your scripts need. It lives in your project folder and is never uploaded to the internet or shared with anyone.

Create a file called .env in your project's root folder with these three lines:

SHOPIFY_SHOP=your-store-name
SHOPIFY_CLIENT_ID=your-client-id-from-dev-dashboard
SHOPIFY_CLIENT_SECRET=your-client-secret-from-dev-dashboard

Replace the placeholder values with your actual credentials:

Variable What to put here Example
SHOPIFY_SHOP Just the store name -- NOT the full URL my-awesome-store
SHOPIFY_CLIENT_ID The Client ID from Section 3.2 shp_ci_abc123def456...
SHOPIFY_CLIENT_SECRET The Client Secret from Section 3.2 shp_cs_xyz789ghi012...

Common mistake: For SHOPIFY_SHOP, use only the store name. If your store URL is https://my-awesome-store.myshopify.com, the value should be just my-awesome-store. Do not include https://, do not include .myshopify.com, and do not add a trailing slash. Getting this wrong is the number one cause of connection errors.

What Happens Behind the Scenes

When your automation script runs, it does something like this (in plain English):

  1. "Hey Shopify, I am the app with Client ID shp_ci_abc123. Here is my Client Secret to prove it."
  2. Shopify checks: "Is this a real app? Is it installed on this store? Are the credentials correct?"
  3. If everything checks out, Shopify replies: "Here is a temporary access token. It is good for 24 hours."
  4. Your script uses that token for all its API calls.
  5. If the token expires mid-session, the script automatically requests a new one.

You do not need to understand or memorize any of this. The Shopify Connector skill (introduced in Section 3.7) handles all the authentication plumbing for you. Just provide the Client ID and Secret once (in your .env file), and Claude Code handles the rest — no manually copying tokens, no remembering to refresh anything, no security headaches.

3.6 Using Shopify Dev MCP with Claude Code

What is MCP?

MCP stands for Model Context Protocol. That is a mouthful, so here is the plain-English version:

MCP is a way to give Claude Code "superpowers" by connecting it to external services. Think of MCP plugins as phone apps -- each one adds a new capability. One plugin might let Claude Code talk to Shopify. Another might let it browse the web. Another might let it interact with Meta's ad platform.

Without MCP, Claude Code can only work with files on your computer. With MCP, it can reach out and interact with live services -- pulling product data from your Shopify store, checking ad performance on Meta, and more.

How Does This Relate to Shopify?

You can configure a Shopify Dev MCP plugin so that Claude Code can interact with your store directly -- without you having to write a single line of code. When it is set up, you can say things like:

  • "Show me my top 10 products by inventory"
  • "Update the price on product X to 500 EGP"
  • "List all orders from last week"

...and Claude Code will talk to Shopify on your behalf.

When Do We Set This Up?

Not right now. MCP plugin configuration is covered in detail in Section 5: Plugin Setup Wizard. For now, just know that this capability exists and that the Shopify connection you set up in this section is what makes it possible.

Preview: In Section 5, you will run the Plugin Setup Wizard skill, which walks you through configuring MCP plugins for both Shopify and Meta in about five minutes.

3.7 Introducing Claude Skills

This is where things start getting exciting. Everything we have done so far -- creating the app, configuring scopes, installing on your store, setting up credentials -- was groundwork. Now we are going to use that groundwork.

What is a Skill?

A Claude Skill is a reusable instruction file that teaches Claude Code how to do something specific. Think of it as a recipe card:

  • A recipe card tells a chef how to make a specific dish, step by step
  • A skill tells Claude Code how to perform a specific task, step by step

Once Claude Code has a skill, it can follow those instructions anytime you ask. You do not need to explain the steps each time.

Where Do Skills Live?

Skills live inside your project in a specific folder structure:

your-project/
  .claude/
    skills/
      skill-name/
        SKILL.md       <-- The instruction file
      another-skill/
        SKILL.md

Each skill gets its own folder inside .claude/skills/, and the instructions are in a file called SKILL.md.

How to Install a Skill

Installing a skill is as simple as copying a file:

  1. Create the skill's folder: .claude/skills/skill-name/
  2. Place the SKILL.md file inside it
  3. That is it -- Claude Code automatically detects new skills the next time it starts

Throughout this playbook, each section includes downloadable skills. You will build up a library of skills that, together, form a complete ecommerce automation toolkit.

How to Use a Skill

In Claude Code, type a forward slash followed by the skill name:

/skill-name

For example, /shopify-connect runs the Shopify Connector skill. You can also just ask Claude Code a question related to the skill's purpose -- if the skill is relevant, Claude Code will use it automatically.

Skill Download: Project Scaffolder

This is the first skill you will install. It sets up a brand-new automation project with everything in the right place.

⚡SKILL DOWNLOAD
Project Scaffolder
What it does: Creates a new automation project with the correct folder structure, a .env template, a .gitignore, and a starter CLAUDE.md file.
Tell Claude Code: "Install the setup-project skill from https://raw.githubusercontent.com/mazinzaki911/agentic-ecom-playbook/main/skills/setup-project.SKILL.md"
Type /setup-project in Claude Code
  • Generates complete folder structure (scripts, data, config)
  • Pre-fills .env with all required credential fields
  • Creates .gitignore to protect secrets from version control
↓ Download Skill

What It Creates

When you run /setup-project, the skill builds out the following project structure for you:

your-project/
  .env                 <-- Credentials template (you fill in the values)
  .gitignore           <-- Prevents secrets from being uploaded to Git
  CLAUDE.md            <-- Instructions for Claude Code about your project
  package.json         <-- Node.js project configuration
  scripts/
    shopify/           <-- Scripts that interact with your Shopify store
    meta/              <-- Scripts that interact with Meta ads
    reports/           <-- Analytics and reporting scripts
  data/                <-- Intermediate data files (JSON, CSV, etc.)

Tip: If you already have a project set up from Section 2, you can run this skill in a new folder to see what a clean starting point looks like, then copy over any pieces you are missing.

Skill Download: Shopify Connector

This is the skill that brings everything in this section together. It handles the entire token exchange process described in Section 3.5 -- automatically.

⚡SKILL DOWNLOAD
Shopify Connector
What it does: Handles Shopify authentication using the client credentials grant. Provides reusable functions for token exchange and GraphQL queries.
Tell Claude Code: "Install the shopify-connect skill from https://raw.githubusercontent.com/mazinzaki911/agentic-ecom-playbook/main/skills/shopify-connect.SKILL.md"
Type /shopify-connect in Claude Code or just ask about Shopify
  • Exchanges credentials for access tokens automatically
  • Caches and auto-refreshes tokens before expiry
  • Provides a reusable shopifyGraphQL() function for all scripts
↓ Download Skill

What It Does

The Shopify Connector skill:

  1. Reads your .env file to get the store name, Client ID, and Client Secret
  2. Exchanges those credentials for a token using the process described in Section 3.5
  3. Caches the token so it does not request a new one on every single API call
  4. Automatically refreshes the token when it is about to expire
  5. Provides a shopifyGraphQL() function that any script can use to query your store

In practice, this means you can ask Claude Code to "list my products" or "check inventory for SKU 12345" and it handles all the authentication plumbing behind the scenes.

Testing Your Connection

After installing the skill and filling in your .env file, test the connection by telling Claude Code:

test shopify

Claude Code will run a quick diagnostic that:

  1. Checks that your environment variables are set
  2. Attempts to exchange credentials for a token
  3. Makes a test query to fetch your store's name and URL
  4. Reports whether the connection passed or failed

If you see "Connection test PASSED" along with your store's name and URL, everything is working. If not, check the troubleshooting table below.

Troubleshooting

Even with careful setup, things can go wrong. Here are the most common issues and exactly how to fix them.

Quick Reference Table

Problem What you see Cause Fix
shop_not_permitted Error in token exchange The app and the store are not in the same Shopify organization Go to Dev Dashboard and confirm both the app and the store belong to the same org
401 Unauthorized Token request failed Wrong Client ID or Client Secret Double-check the values in your .env -- copy-paste them fresh from the Dev Dashboard
403 Forbidden API call rejected Missing scopes (permissions) Add the needed scopes in Dev Dashboard, release a new version, and re-install the app
HTML instead of JSON Garbled error output SHOPIFY_SHOP includes .myshopify.com Use just the store name (e.g., my-store), not the full URL
ENOTFOUND DNS resolution failure SHOPIFY_SHOP is empty or .env is not loading Make sure .env exists in the project root and SHOPIFY_SHOP has a value

Detailed Fixes

"shop_not_permitted"

This error means Shopify does not recognize your app as belonging to the same organization as the store. The client credentials grant only works when the app and the store are under the same Shopify organization.

How to fix: 1. Open the Dev Dashboard at dev.shopify.com 2. Check which organization the app belongs to (shown in the top-left corner) 3. Go to your Shopify admin and check which organization the store belongs to (Settings > Organization) 4. If they are different, either move the store to the correct organization or recreate the app under the right one

"401 Unauthorized"

The credentials you provided do not match what Shopify has on file.

How to fix: 1. Open your app in the Dev Dashboard 2. Go to the Settings or Client credentials section 3. Copy the Client ID and Client Secret exactly -- even a single extra space will cause this error 4. Paste them into your .env file, replacing the old values 5. Save the file and try again

"403 Forbidden"

Your app does not have permission to do what you are asking.

How to fix: 1. Open your app in the Dev Dashboard 2. Go to the Configuration tab and check the API access scopes 3. Add the scopes you need (refer to the table in Section 3.3) 4. Release a new version -- this is the step people forget. Scope changes do not take effect until you release 5. You may need to re-install the app on your store after releasing the new version

HTML response instead of JSON

If your script crashes with an error about "unexpected token <" or you see HTML in the response, the store URL is malformed.

How to fix: - Open your .env file - Find the SHOPIFY_SHOP line - Make sure the value is only the store name - Correct: SHOPIFY_SHOP=my-awesome-store - Wrong: SHOPIFY_SHOP=my-awesome-store.myshopify.com - Wrong: SHOPIFY_SHOP=https://my-awesome-store.myshopify.com - Wrong: SHOPIFY_SHOP=my-awesome-store.myshopify.com/

"ENOTFOUND" or DNS resolution failure

This means the SHOPIFY_SHOP value is empty or undefined, so the script is trying to connect to undefined.myshopify.com -- which obviously does not exist.

How to fix: 1. Confirm that your .env file exists in the project root (the same folder where package.json lives) 2. Confirm that SHOPIFY_SHOP has a value (not blank) 3. Confirm that your script loads the .env file at the very top, before trying to use any environment variables

Section Recap

Here is everything we accomplished:

Step What we did Why it matters
3.1 Identified your store type (dev vs managed) Determines your installation path
3.2 Created an app in the Dev Dashboard Gave your automation an identity (Client ID + Secret)
3.3 Configured scopes and released a version Told Shopify what your app is allowed to do
3.4 Installed the app on your store Connected your app to a specific store
3.5 Understood the client credentials grant Know how tokens work so you can troubleshoot confidently
3.6 Learned about MCP Previewed how Claude Code will talk to Shopify directly
3.7 Installed your first two skills Project Scaffolder and Shopify Connector are ready to use

Your Shopify connection is now live. Your Claude Code environment can authenticate, query products, check inventory, and manage your store programmatically.

KEY TAKEAWAY
API credentials are just a secure handshake between two systems. Once set up, you never think about them again — Claude Code handles authentication invisibly.
REAL TALK
The Shopify Developer Dashboard looks intimidating with all its options. You only need the three things we configured. Everything else is for advanced use cases you can explore later.
ACHIEVEMENT UNLOCKED
Shopify is connected. You can now read products, check inventory, update prices, and manage collections — all through plain English commands to Claude Code.

What's Next

In Section 4, you will connect your Meta (Facebook/Instagram) ad account using the same pattern: create an app, configure permissions, and get Claude Code talking to your ad platform. The concepts are very similar to what you just learned, so it will feel familiar.

[End of Section 3]

Section 4: Meta Developer Setup & Token Generation

IN THIS SECTION YOU WILL
  • Create a Meta App and configure API permissions
  • Generate a long-lived User Access Token
  • Store Meta credentials in your .env file
  • Install the Meta Ads Connector skill and test the connection

Why This Section Matters

In Section 3, you connected Claude Code to your Shopify store. That gave you control over your products, collections, prices, and inventory. Now you're going to connect the other half of your business: your advertising.

Meta (the company behind Facebook and Instagram) provides an API -- a behind-the-scenes connection -- that lets software interact with your ad account the same way you do when you click around in Ads Manager. When you create a campaign, check your ROAS, or update your product catalog, the Ads Manager interface sends requests through this API. Claude Code will send the exact same requests -- it just skips the clicking.

But Meta doesn't hand out this access freely. You need to create something called a "Meta App," generate an access token (think of it as a password that proves you're allowed to make changes), and configure a few settings. That's what this section walks you through.

By the end, you'll have Claude Code fully connected to your Meta ad account, ready to create campaigns, pull performance reports, manage your product catalog, and more -- all through plain English instructions.

The whole process takes about 20 minutes.

4.1 Create a Meta App

A "Meta App" is not something your customers will ever see or use. It's a container that Meta uses to track which software is accessing your data and what permissions it has. Think of it like creating an employee badge -- it identifies your automation tools to Meta's servers.

Here's how to create one:

  1. Go to developers.facebook.com and log in with the same Facebook account that has access to your ad account.

  2. Click "My Apps" in the top navigation bar. If this is your first time, Meta might ask you to register as a developer. Just confirm your account and continue.

  3. Click "Create App." You'll see a screen asking what type of app you want to build.

  4. Choose the app type. Meta changes this screen from time to time. Look for "Business" or "Other" -- either one works. The app type mainly affects which features Meta highlights for you; it doesn't limit what your app can do.

  5. Important: Do NOT link a specific Business Manager. When Meta asks if you want to connect the app to a Business Portfolio (or Business Manager), choose "I don't want to connect a business portfolio" or leave it unlinked. Why? Linking to a Business Manager adds restrictions and approval requirements that you don't need for personal automation. You can always link it later if needed.

  6. Enter your app details:

  7. App name: Something descriptive, like "Store Automation" or "My Ads Connector." This name is just for you -- customers never see it.

  8. Contact email: Your email address. Meta uses this to send you important notices about your app.

  9. Click "Create App."

That's it. Your app now exists. Meta immediately gives you two important pieces of information:

  • App ID -- A number that identifies your app (like an employee badge number).
  • App Secret -- A private key that proves your app is really yours (like the PIN for that badge).

You can find both of these under Settings > Basic in your app's dashboard. Copy them somewhere safe -- you'll need them in a few minutes.

Keep your App Secret private. Never share it publicly, never paste it in a chat, and never commit it to a code repository. Anyone who has your App ID and App Secret can impersonate your app.

4.2 Privacy Policy & Going Live

Before your app can actually do anything useful, Meta requires two things. This isn't bureaucracy for the sake of it -- Meta uses these requirements to ensure that apps accessing their platform are legitimate.

Add a Privacy Policy URL

  1. In your app dashboard, go to Settings > Basic.
  2. Find the Privacy Policy URL field.
  3. Enter a URL that points to a privacy policy page.

This doesn't need to be a legal masterpiece. It just needs to be a real page at a public URL that says something about how you handle data. Options:

  • A simple page on your website (e.g., yourstore.com/privacy)
  • A published Google Doc (click Share > "Anyone with the link can view" > copy the link)
  • Even a single paragraph saying "This app is for internal business automation and does not collect or share personal data from third parties"

Meta's system checks that the URL exists and loads a real page. It doesn't read the content or grade it. But the URL must work.

Switch from Development to Live Mode

When you first create a Meta App, it starts in Development mode. This is like a sandbox: the app only works for people who are listed as administrators or developers of the app itself. That's fine for testing, but it means the API calls you make might behave differently or fail for certain operations.

To switch to Live mode:

  1. Look at the top of your app dashboard. You'll see a toggle that says "In development" or "Development."
  2. Click the toggle to switch to Live mode.
  3. Meta may ask you to confirm. Click through.

If the toggle is grayed out, it usually means you haven't added the Privacy Policy URL yet. Go back and add it, save your settings, and try the toggle again.

Once your app is in Live mode, it works the way you'd expect -- your API calls function normally for any ad account and page you have access to.

4.3 Generate a Token via Graph API Explorer

Now comes the key step: getting your access token. An access token is a long string of characters that acts as your digital ID badge. Every time Claude Code talks to Meta's servers, it includes this token to prove "I'm authorized to do this."

Meta provides a handy tool called the Graph API Explorer that lets you generate tokens through a visual interface, without writing any code.

Step by step:

  1. Go to the Graph API Explorer: Open your browser and navigate to developers.facebook.com/tools/explorer/

  2. Select your app. In the top-right area, you'll see a dropdown labeled "Meta App" (or "Application"). Click it and select the app you just created -- NOT "Graph API Explorer" (that's a default test app).

  3. Click "Generate Access Token" (the button might also say "Get Token" > "Get User Access Token" depending on the current UI).

  4. A permissions popup appears. This is where you tell Meta exactly what your app is allowed to do. Think of each permission as a key to a specific room. You need to select all of the following:

Permission What It Unlocks
ads_management Create and manage campaigns, ad sets, and ads
ads_read Read campaign performance data (spend, impressions, ROAS)
business_management Access Business Manager resources and settings
pages_manage_ads Manage ads that appear on your Facebook Page
pages_read_engagement Read your page's engagement data (likes, comments)
pages_manage_metadata Manage page settings and configuration
catalog_management Manage your product catalog (for feed uploads and DPA)
instagram_basic Read your connected Instagram profile info
instagram_manage_insights Read Instagram analytics and performance data

Check every box in the list above. If you skip one, certain operations will fail later with a "Permission denied" error, and you'll have to come back and redo this step.

  1. Click "Generate Access Token."

  2. Authorize when prompted. Facebook will show you a screen listing which pages and ad accounts you're granting access to. Review it and click "Continue" or "OK." If you have multiple pages or ad accounts, make sure the ones you need are selected.

  3. You now have a short-lived token. It appears in the Access Token field at the top of the Explorer. You can click it to select and copy it.

Important: This token is short-lived -- it expires in 1 to 2 hours. That's fine. The next section shows you how to extend it to 60 days.

4.4 Extend to a Long-Lived Token (60 Days)

The short-lived token you just generated is useless for automation. If it expires in an hour, Claude Code would stop working while you're asleep. You need a long-lived token that lasts 60 days.

The process is simple: you send one API request that says "take this short-lived token and give me back a long-lived one." Meta checks that the request is coming from a legitimate app (using your App ID and App Secret) and returns a new token.

How to do it

Open Claude Code and say:

"Extend my Meta access token. My App ID is [your app ID], my App Secret is [your app secret], and here is my short-lived token: [paste your token]"

Claude Code will call Meta's token exchange endpoint, get back a long-lived token, and tell you the result. No need to construct URLs or make API calls yourself.

If you have already set up your .env file with the App ID and App Secret (covered in the next section), it is even simpler:

"Extend my Meta token — here is the short-lived token: [paste your token]"

Claude Code reads your .env for the App ID and Secret, handles the exchange, and gives you the new token.

What comes back

{
  "access_token": "EAABs...(very long string)...ZD",
  "token_type": "bearer",
  "expires_in": 5184000
}

Let's break that down:

  • access_token -- This is your new long-lived token. It's a very long string of letters and numbers. Copy it carefully -- you need every character.
  • token_type -- Always "bearer." You can ignore this.
  • expires_in -- The number of seconds until it expires. 5184000 seconds = 60 days.

Set a calendar reminder right now for 50 days from today to regenerate this token. Don't wait until it expires -- if it dies at 2 AM on a Friday when your biggest campaign is running, you'll have a bad weekend. Give yourself a 10-day buffer.

The renewal process is the same every time: open Graph API Explorer, generate a new short-lived token, extend it, update your .env file. It takes about 3 minutes once you've done it once.

4.5 Store Your Credentials & Test the Connection

Now you need to save all your credentials so Claude Code can use them. These go in your .env file -- the same file where you stored your Shopify credentials in Section 3.

Add these lines to your .env file

Open the .env file in your project's root directory and add:

META_ACCESS_TOKEN=your-long-lived-token-from-the-previous-step
META_AD_ACCOUNT_ID=act_your_account_id
META_APP_ID=your-app-id
META_APP_SECRET=your-app-secret
META_PAGE_ID=your-page-id
META_PIXEL_ID=your-pixel-id

Replace each placeholder with your actual values. Here's where to find each one:

Finding your IDs

What You Need Where to Find It
Ad Account ID Open Ads Manager (adsmanager.facebook.com). Look at the URL in your browser -- it contains act_ followed by a long number, like act_7781591668619406. Include the act_ prefix. This is the single most common mistake people make. If you write just the number without act_, every API call will fail.
App ID Your app dashboard at developers.facebook.com > Settings > Basic. It's the number at the top.
App Secret Same page as App ID (Settings > Basic). Click "Show" next to the App Secret field. You may need to re-enter your Facebook password.
Page ID Go to your Facebook Page. Click About (or Page Transparency on newer layouts). The Page ID is listed there. Alternatively, go to your Page's URL and add /about -- the ID is usually visible.
Pixel ID Go to Events Manager (facebook.com/events_manager). Click on Data Sources in the left sidebar. Click on your pixel. The Pixel ID is displayed at the top of the page.

Double-check your .env file

Your complete .env file should now look something like this (with your real values):

# Shopify (from Section 3)
SHOPIFY_STORE=your-store.myshopify.com
SHOPIFY_ACCESS_TOKEN=shpat_xxxxxxxxxxxxx

# Meta
META_ACCESS_TOKEN=EAABs...very-long-string...ZD
META_AD_ACCOUNT_ID=act_7781591668619406
META_APP_ID=3149700961890809
META_APP_SECRET=abc123def456
META_PAGE_ID=233470590747992
META_PIXEL_ID=1065634690926731

Never share this file. It contains everything someone would need to control your ad account and Shopify store. Make sure .env is listed in your .gitignore file so it's never accidentally uploaded anywhere.

Test the connection

Open Claude Code and type:

"List my active Meta campaigns"

Claude Code will read your .env file, use the token to connect to Meta's API, and return a list of your campaigns with their names, statuses, and budgets.

If you see your campaigns listed -- congratulations! You're connected.

If you see an error, jump to the Troubleshooting section at the end of this section.

4.6 A Note on System User Tokens (Advanced)

The setup you just completed uses a User Access Token -- a token tied to your personal Facebook account. This works perfectly for store owners and media buyers managing their own accounts. But it has one limitation: it's tied to you, personally.

If you ever leave the company, change your Facebook password, or deactivate your account, the token stops working. For a solo operation or a small team, this is a non-issue. You're the one running things.

But for larger teams or production setups where continuity matters, Meta offers something called System User Tokens through Business Manager. A System User is a virtual account that isn't tied to any real person. Its tokens never expire and aren't affected when someone leaves the team.

Setting up System User tokens involves more steps -- creating the System User in Business Manager, assigning it to your ad account, granting permissions through the Business Manager UI rather than the Graph API Explorer -- and the process changes frequently as Meta updates their interface.

For now, the long-lived User Access Token approach you set up above is the right choice. It works, it's straightforward, and the 60-day renewal is a minor inconvenience (set that calendar reminder and it takes 3 minutes). If you decide to move to System User tokens later, the migration is simple: you replace one line in your .env file.

Skill Download: Meta Ads Connector

This is the first skill that connects Claude Code to an external advertising platform. Install it now -- every Meta-related section from here forward depends on it.

⚡SKILL DOWNLOAD
Meta Ads Connector
What it does: Handles Meta API v25.0 authentication, GET/POST requests, rate limiting, token expiry warnings, and connection testing.
Tell Claude Code: "Install the meta-connect skill from https://raw.githubusercontent.com/mazinzaki911/agentic-ecom-playbook/main/skills/meta-connect.SKILL.md"
Type /meta-connect in Claude Code or just ask about Meta ads
  • Authenticates every API request using your .env token
  • Auto-throttles to stay within Meta's 200 calls/hour limit
  • Detects expired tokens and walks you through renewal
↓ Download Skill

What this skill does for you

You don't need to understand the technical details, but here's what's happening under the hood when you ask Claude Code to do something with Meta:

  • Authentication: The skill reads your .env file, grabs your token, and includes it in every API request. You never have to think about tokens again (until renewal day).

  • Rate limiting: Meta allows 200 API calls per hour per ad account. The skill automatically tracks this and slows down if you're getting close to the limit. If Meta says "too many requests," the skill waits and retries automatically.

  • Token expiry detection: If your token has expired, the skill immediately tells you what happened and walks you through the 3-minute renewal process instead of failing with a cryptic error.

  • Error handling: When something goes wrong, the skill translates Meta's error codes into plain language. Instead of "Error 100: Invalid parameter," you get "The ad account ID is missing the act_ prefix. Add it and try again."

Installation

Open Claude Code and say:

"Install the Meta Ads Connector skill from the playbook. Put it in .claude/skills/meta-connect.SKILL.md"

Claude Code will create the directory and copy the file for you. Then verify by asking:

"What skills do you have installed?"

Troubleshooting

Things don't always work on the first try. Here are the most common problems and exactly how to fix each one.

Problem Error Code What It Means How to Fix It
Token expired 190 Your access token has passed its 60-day lifetime (or you changed your Facebook password, which invalidates all tokens). Redo the full flow: Graph API Explorer > generate short-lived token > extend to long-lived > update .env. Takes 3 minutes.
Rate limited 17 You've made too many API calls in a short period. Meta's limit is 200 calls per hour per ad account. Wait 5 minutes, then try again. If you're running a script that makes many calls, the Meta Ads Connector skill handles this automatically with exponential backoff.
Permission denied 10 Your token doesn't include a permission that the operation requires. For example, trying to create a campaign without the ads_management permission. Go back to Graph API Explorer, generate a new token with ALL the permissions listed in Section 4.3 (every single checkbox), extend it, and update your .env file.
Invalid parameter 100 Something in your request is wrong. The most common cause: the ad account ID is missing the act_ prefix. Check your META_AD_ACCOUNT_ID in .env. It must start with act_ (e.g., act_7781591668619406, not just 7781591668619406). Also check that all other IDs are correct numbers with no extra spaces.
App not in Live mode (none) Your Meta App is still in Development mode, which restricts API access to app admins only. Some operations work; others silently fail or return incomplete data. Go to developers.facebook.com > your app > look for the Development/Live toggle at the top of the dashboard > switch it to Live. If the toggle is grayed out, add your Privacy Policy URL first (Section 4.2).
"Session has expired" 190 Same as token expired, but with different wording. Meta uses this message when the token was invalidated by a password change or security event rather than natural expiry. Same fix as token expired: regenerate and extend.
"The user has not authorized application" 200 You generated the token with a different app than the one specified in your META_APP_ID. Make sure you select YOUR app (not "Graph API Explorer") in the Graph API Explorer dropdown before generating the token.

Still stuck?

If none of the above applies, try this diagnostic checklist:

  1. Is your .env file saved? Open it and visually confirm all six META_ variables are present with real values (no placeholder text).

  2. Are there extra spaces or line breaks? Open .env in a text editor and make sure there are no spaces before or after the = sign, and no blank lines inside a value.

  3. Is the token complete? Meta tokens are very long (200+ characters). If you accidentally cut off part of it when copying, it won't work. Go back to the Explorer and copy the full string.

  4. Did you extend the token? The short-lived token from the Explorer expires in 1-2 hours. If you skipped Section 4.4, your token is already dead.

  5. Is the right Facebook account logged in? The token is tied to whichever Facebook account you were logged into when you generated it. That account must have admin access to the ad account.

What's Next

KEY TAKEAWAY
The 60-day token renewal is the one recurring maintenance task. Set a calendar reminder and it takes 3 minutes — a tiny price for complete API access.
REAL TALK
Meta's developer portal is more complex than Shopify's. Don't worry about understanding every option — you configured exactly what you need. The rest is for advanced use cases.
ACHIEVEMENT UNLOCKED
Both platforms connected. You now have Claude Code wired to Shopify and Meta — the two pillars of your ecommerce business, both controllable through plain English.

You now have Claude Code connected to both Shopify and Meta. That means you can manage your products AND your advertising through plain English instructions. Those are the two pillars of running an ecommerce business.

Section 5 takes you one level deeper: setting up Claude Code plugins, including browser automation that can take screenshots of your storefront, visually verify that campaigns look right in Ads Manager, and confirm that everything you build actually works the way it should.

Part 2: Tooling & Framework
Sections 5 -- 6

Add plugins for browser automation and document generation, then build a memory system and error handling framework that makes your setup self-improving.

Section 5: Claude Code Plugins & MCP Servers

Time required: 15-20 minutes Difficulty: Beginner -- no coding required What you will have at the end: A fully configured plugin system that gives Claude Code a web browser, presentation builder, document tools, and more
IN THIS SECTION YOU WILL
  • Understand what MCP servers are and why they matter
  • Configure Playwright for browser automation and screenshots
  • Learn about built-in tools (web search, web fetch, PDF reading)
  • Install the Plugin Setup Wizard skill

In Sections 2 through 4, you installed Claude Code and connected it to Shopify and Meta. At this point, Claude Code can read your files, run commands in the terminal, and talk to both platforms through their APIs. That is already powerful.

But what if you could also tell it to open a web browser, navigate to your storefront, and take a screenshot to prove the sale price is showing correctly? Or ask it to generate a professional slide deck summarizing your ad performance for a client meeting? Or have it read a PDF catalog from a supplier and pull out the data you need?

That is what plugins do. They give Claude Code entirely new abilities that it does not have out of the box.

This section explains what plugins are, how they work, and walks you through setting up the ones that matter most for running an ecommerce store and managing ad campaigns.

5.1 What Are MCP Servers?

Let us start with the name. MCP stands for Model Context Protocol. That sounds technical, but the concept is simple.

Plugins that give Claude Code new abilities.

Out of the box, Claude Code can do two things really well: read files on your computer and run terminal commands. Those two abilities alone got you through Sections 3 and 4 -- connecting to Shopify's API, connecting to Meta's API, running scripts.
