But there is a whole world of tasks that do not involve terminal commands or files.

What about controlling a web browser? Claude Code cannot do that on its own. It does not have eyes. It cannot click a button, fill in a form, or read what is on a webpage.

What about creating a PowerPoint presentation? Claude Code can write text and code, but it cannot generate slide decks with layouts, images, and themes.

MCP servers solve this. Each MCP server is a small program that runs in the background and gives Claude Code access to a specific set of tools. When Claude Code connects to an MCP server, it gains the ability to use those tools -- just as naturally as it reads files or runs commands.

Think of it this way: MCP servers are like apps on your phone. Your phone works without them -- it can make calls and send texts. But each app you install adds a new capability. Instagram lets you share photos. Google Maps lets you navigate. Uber lets you call a ride. The phone itself did not change -- it just gained access to new tools through those apps.

MCP servers work the same way for Claude Code. Install the Playwright plugin, and Claude Code can suddenly control a web browser. Install the Gamma plugin, and it can create presentations. The core of Claude Code did not change -- it just has more tools available.

Where Are Plugins Configured?

All your plugins are defined in a single file called .mcp.json that lives in your project's root folder (the same folder where your .env file lives). It is a simple text file in JSON format that lists which plugins to load and how to start them.

Here is what it looks like with one plugin configured:

{
  "mcpServers": {
    "playwright": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["@anthropic-ai/mcp-playwright"]
    }
  }
}

That is the entire file. Three lines of actual configuration inside a JSON wrapper. The command tells Claude Code how to start the plugin (npx runs a Node.js package), and args tells it which package to run.

When you launch Claude Code, it reads this file, starts up each listed plugin in the background, and makes their tools available for the session. You never interact with the plugins directly -- you just talk to Claude Code in plain English, and it decides which plugin tools to use based on what you asked for.

How the Connection Works

Here is the flow, step by step:

You type a request in Claude Code
        |
        v
Claude Code reads your request and decides which tools it needs
        |
        v
If it needs a browser tool, it calls the Playwright MCP server
If it needs a presentation tool, it calls the Gamma MCP server
If it needs to read a file, it uses its built-in file tools
        |
        v
The MCP server executes the action and returns the result
        |
        v
Claude Code processes the result and responds to you

You never need to think about this flow. It happens automatically. You just say "take a screenshot of my homepage" and Claude Code figures out that it needs to use the Playwright plugin's screenshot tool, calls it, and shows you the result.

5.2 The Playwright Plugin (Browser Automation)

This is the single most important plugin for anyone running an online store or managing ad campaigns. It gives Claude Code a real web browser -- one it can control programmatically to visit pages, click things, fill in forms, read content, and take screenshots.

Why This Matters for Your Store

Without Playwright, when you ask Claude Code to "check if the sale price shows on my product page," it has no way to do that. It can call the Shopify API to read the product data, but the API data and the live storefront are two different things. A product might have the right price in Shopify's database but display incorrectly on the website because of a theme bug.

With Playwright, Claude Code opens an actual browser, navigates to your product page, reads what is displayed, and tells you exactly what it sees. It can even take a screenshot so you have visual proof.

This is the difference between "the data says it should be right" and "I looked at the page and confirmed it is right."

What Playwright Can Do

Here is the full list of capabilities, explained in plain English:

What You Say What Happens Behind the Scenes Why It Matters
"Open my store homepage" Playwright launches a browser and navigates to the URL You can inspect your live site through Claude Code
"What is on this page?" Playwright reads the page structure (text, buttons, links, images) Claude Code can understand and describe any webpage
"Click the Add to Cart button" Playwright finds the button element and clicks it You can test interactive features like checkout flows
"Fill in the search box with winter boots" Playwright types text into the search field You can test search, forms, login flows
"Take a screenshot" Playwright captures a PNG image of the page You get visual proof -- for your records, client reports, or debugging
"Check for JavaScript errors" Playwright reads the browser's console log You can catch hidden errors your customers might be hitting
"What API calls does this page make?" Playwright monitors all network traffic You can debug tracking pixels, API integrations, and slow-loading resources

Use Cases for Your Store

Here are five real scenarios where Playwright saves you time and catches problems:

1. Verify storefront changes after a price update.

You just ran a script to apply a 25% discount across your Winter Collection. Did it actually work on the live site? Instead of opening your browser, navigating to the collection page, and scrolling through products to check, you say:

"Open my Winter Collection page and tell me the prices of the first five products. Are they showing the discounted price with the compare-at price crossed out?"

Claude Code opens the page, reads the content, and reports back. If something looks wrong, it can take a screenshot so you can see exactly what it sees.

2. Check your Ads Manager dashboard.

You want a quick snapshot of how your campaigns are performing today. You could log in to Ads Manager, set the date range, and scroll through the numbers -- or you say:

"Navigate to my Ads Manager, take a screenshot of the campaign dashboard."

Claude Code opens Ads Manager, captures the screen, and saves the image. You have a timestamped record without lifting a finger. (Note: you may need to handle authentication for this -- see the tips at the end of this section.)

3. Test the checkout flow.

You just updated your theme or installed a new Shopify app. Does checkout still work? Rather than placing a test order yourself:

"Go to my store, add the first product to the cart, and proceed to checkout. Take a screenshot at each step."

Claude Code walks through the flow and gives you screenshots of the cart, the checkout page, and any errors it encounters.

4. Capture proof for client reporting.

If you manage ads for someone else, you need to send performance screenshots regularly. Instead of logging in, screenshotting, cropping, and emailing:

"Take a full-page screenshot of my Ads Manager showing this week's results."

One screenshot, saved to a file, ready to attach to your report.

5. Debug issues customers report.

A customer says "your homepage takes forever to load" or "I see a weird error on mobile." You can investigate:

"Open my homepage and check for JavaScript errors. Also, show me how many network requests the page makes and how long they take."

Claude Code inspects the page like a developer would, but you do not need to know anything about developer tools.

Installing Playwright

You already know the configuration goes in .mcp.json. Here is the exact setup:

Step 1: Open Claude Code in your project folder and say:

"Create a .mcp.json file in the project root with the Playwright plugin configured."

Or, if you prefer to do it manually, create a file called .mcp.json in your project's root directory with this content:

{
  "mcpServers": {
    "playwright": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["@anthropic-ai/mcp-playwright"]
    }
  }
}

Step 2: Restart Claude Code. Close your terminal session and launch Claude Code again. It reads .mcp.json on startup, so it needs a fresh start to pick up the new plugin.

Step 3: Verify it works. Type:

"Take a screenshot of https://example.com"

If Playwright is configured correctly, Claude Code will open a browser, navigate to example.com, and save a screenshot. If you see an error about a missing browser, Claude Code will usually offer to install it for you -- just approve the command.

Quick Reference: Playwright Commands

You do not need to memorize these. Claude Code picks the right tool automatically based on what you ask for. This table is here so you understand what is happening under the hood when you see these names in Claude Code's output:

Command What It Does When Claude Code Uses It
browser_navigate Opens a URL in the browser Any time you say "go to" or "open" a webpage
browser_snapshot Reads the page structure as text When it needs to understand what is on a page before acting
browser_click Clicks a button, link, or element When you say "click" something on a page
browser_type Types text into an input field When you say "type" or "enter" text on a page
browser_fill_form Fills in multiple form fields at once When there is a form with several inputs to fill
browser_take_screenshot Captures a PNG or JPEG image When you say "screenshot" or "capture" a page
browser_console_messages Reads JavaScript errors and logs When you say "check for errors" on a page
browser_network_requests Lists all HTTP requests the page made When you say "what requests" or want to debug loading
browser_evaluate Runs JavaScript code on the page When advanced page inspection is needed
browser_tabs Manages multiple browser tabs When working with multiple pages at once
browser_wait_for Waits for something to appear or disappear When a page takes time to load content

Tip on authentication: Playwright opens a fresh browser with no saved logins. If you need to access a page that requires logging in (like Ads Manager), Claude Code can fill in your credentials through the form -- but you should never put passwords in your chat history. A better approach is to ask Claude Code to navigate to the login page, then handle the login yourself (or use environment variables for non-sensitive test accounts).

5.3 Other Essential Plugins

Playwright is the headliner, but there are several other tools that come in handy for day-to-day ecommerce operations. Some of these are built directly into Claude Code and do not need a plugin at all -- they are available the moment you start a session.

Gamma (Presentation Generator)

Gamma is a tool that creates professional presentations, documents, social media posts, and even simple web pages -- all from a text description. Instead of opening PowerPoint, choosing a template, creating slides one by one, and formatting everything, you describe what you want and Gamma builds it.

What it can do:

  • Generate slide decks with layouts, images, and consistent styling
  • Create long-form documents with headers, sections, and formatting
  • Build social media post graphics
  • Export to PowerPoint (.pptx) or PDF format
  • Apply custom themes to match your brand

Use cases for your store:

Weekly performance summary:

"Create a 5-slide presentation summarizing this week's ad performance. Include our total spend, ROAS, top 3 campaigns by revenue, and a slide with recommendations for next week."

Client report:

"Generate a professional PDF report showing our Meta Ads results for January. Use a clean, modern theme."

Product launch deck:

"Create a presentation for our new Spring Collection launch. Include slides for the collection overview, key products, pricing strategy, and marketing timeline."

Configuration:

Gamma is often pre-configured in Claude Code environments and may not need any setup on your part. If it is not available, it can be added to your .mcp.json:

{
  "mcpServers": {
    "gamma": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["-y", "@anthropic-ai/mcp-gamma"]
    }
  }
}

To check if Gamma is already available, type /mcp in Claude Code. If you see Gamma listed, you are good to go.

Quick reference:

What You Say What Gamma Does
"Create a presentation about X" Generates a full slide deck with AI-chosen layouts and images
"Make it 8 slides" Controls the number of slides/pages
"Use a professional/modern/colorful theme" Applies visual styling to match your preference
"Export as PowerPoint" Saves the result as a .pptx file you can download and edit
"Export as PDF" Saves a non-editable PDF for sharing

Web Search and Web Fetch (Built-In)

These are not plugins -- they are built directly into Claude Code. No configuration needed. They let Claude Code search the internet and read web pages.

What they can do:

  • Search the web for current information
  • Read and summarize web pages
  • Pull data from public URLs

Use cases for your store:

Competitive research:

"Search for trending footwear brands in Egypt this month."

Stay current with platform changes:

"What are the latest changes to Meta's advertising policies for 2026?"

Research pricing:

"Check what price range competitors are selling winter boots at in the Egyptian market."

Read documentation:

"Read the Meta Marketing API changelog and tell me if there are any breaking changes I should know about."

You do not need to install or configure anything for these. Just ask Claude Code to search for something or read a URL, and it handles it.

Document Skills (PDF, Spreadsheets, Images)

Claude Code has built-in abilities to work with various file types. Combined with its terminal access, it can handle most document tasks you throw at it.

What it can do:

  • Read PDF files and extract text, tables, and data
  • Read and analyze CSV and Excel files
  • Create new files in various formats using scripts
  • Process images (read, describe, and manipulate)

Use cases for your store:

Supplier catalogs:

"Read this PDF catalog from my supplier and create a spreadsheet with the product names, SKUs, and wholesale prices."

Data analysis:

"Read the CSV export from Shopify and tell me which products had the highest return rate last month."

Report generation:

"Take the sales data from this spreadsheet and create a summary with totals by category."

Image inspection:

"Look at this product photo and tell me if it meets Meta's ad requirements (aspect ratio, text overlay limits)."

These capabilities come from Claude Code's ability to read files and run scripts -- no extra plugin needed.

5.4 Plugin Configuration Reference

This section pulls everything together into a single reference you can come back to whenever you need to check or update your plugin setup.

The Complete .mcp.json File

Here is a recommended configuration with the most useful plugins for ecommerce:

{
  "mcpServers": {
    "playwright": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["@anthropic-ai/mcp-playwright"]
    }
  }
}

That is the minimal recommended setup. Playwright is the one plugin you absolutely need to install yourself. The other capabilities (web search, document reading, file manipulation) are built into Claude Code already.

If you also want Gamma for presentations:

{
  "mcpServers": {
    "playwright": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["@anthropic-ai/mcp-playwright"]
    },
    "gamma": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["-y", "@anthropic-ai/mcp-gamma"]
    }
  }
}

How to Check Which Plugins Are Active

Once Claude Code is running, type:

/mcp

This shows you a list of all connected MCP servers and their status. If a plugin shows as "connected," it is ready to use. If it shows an error, Claude Code will usually tell you what went wrong.

How to Add a New Plugin

The process is always the same three steps:

  1. Edit .mcp.json -- Add the new plugin's entry inside the mcpServers object.
  2. Restart Claude Code -- Close your session and launch Claude Code again. It reads the config file on startup.
  3. Verify with /mcp -- Confirm the new plugin appears as connected.

If you find a new MCP server you want to try (the ecosystem is growing rapidly), it follows this same pattern. The plugin's documentation will tell you what to put in the command and args fields.

How to Remove a Plugin

Delete the plugin's entry from .mcp.json and restart Claude Code. That is it.

Plugin Troubleshooting

Problem Solution
Plugin shows "disconnected" in /mcp Restart Claude Code. If it persists, check that the package name in args is spelled correctly.
"npx: command not found" Node.js is not installed or not on your PATH. Revisit Section 2 to install it.
Playwright says "browser not found" Run npx playwright install chromium in your terminal to download the browser.
Gamma generation is slow or times out This is normal for complex presentations. Wait 30-60 seconds. Check status with the generation ID if available.
.mcp.json changes have no effect You must restart Claude Code after editing the file. Changes are not picked up mid-session.
Screenshot comes out blank The page may not have finished loading. Ask Claude Code to wait a few seconds before taking the screenshot.

Skill Download: Plugin Setup Wizard

⚡SKILL DOWNLOAD
Plugin Setup Wizard
What it does: An interactive wizard that asks which integrations you need and configures all MCP plugins automatically. Checks prerequisites, writes .mcp.json, and verifies everything works.
Tell Claude Code: "Install the plugin-setup-wizard skill from https://raw.githubusercontent.com/mazinzaki911/agentic-ecom-playbook/main/skills/plugin-setup-wizard.SKILL.md"
Type /setup-plugins in Claude Code
  • Asks which integrations you need and configures them
  • Checks your system for prerequisites before installing
  • Verifies every plugin connection works after setup
↓ Download Skill

What You Have Now

After completing this section, your Claude Code setup has grown significantly:

  • Playwright plugin configured -- Claude Code can now control a web browser: navigate pages, click buttons, fill forms, take screenshots, and inspect errors.

  • An understanding of the plugin ecosystem -- You know that MCP servers are modular add-ons, where they are configured, and how to add or remove them.

  • Built-in tools recognized -- You know that web search, web fetch, and document reading are already available without any extra configuration.

  • Gamma available for presentations -- When you need to create a report, a pitch deck, or a client summary, you can generate it from a text description.

  • A troubleshooting reference -- When something does not work, you have a table of common issues and their fixes right here.

Here is a summary of everything Claude Code can now do:

Capability Source Setup Required?
Read and write files Built into Claude Code No
Run terminal commands Built into Claude Code No
Search the internet Built into Claude Code No
Read web pages Built into Claude Code No
Read PDFs and spreadsheets Built into Claude Code No
Connect to Shopify API .env credentials (Section 3) Yes -- done
Connect to Meta API .env credentials (Section 4) Yes -- done
Control a web browser Playwright plugin (this section) Yes -- done
Generate presentations Gamma plugin (this section) Optional -- done if configured

That is a remarkably capable system. You have an AI assistant that can talk to your store, talk to your ad platform, browse the web, read documents, and create visual reports -- all from plain English instructions in your terminal.

KEY TAKEAWAY
Plugins are modular — add what you need, skip what you don't. Playwright is the most impactful because it lets Claude Code verify its own work visually.
REAL TALK
You don't need every plugin right now. Playwright is the must-have. Gamma is a nice-to-have. Start lean and add more as your workflows demand them.
ACHIEVEMENT UNLOCKED
Claude Code is now supercharged with browser automation, web search, and document reading. It can verify its own work by taking screenshots of your storefront.

What Is Next

In Section 6: Error Handling and Memory, you will build the framework that makes everything self-improving. Right now, when something goes wrong, you fix it and move on. After Section 6, the system records every error and every fix, so it never makes the same mistake twice. You will also set up a memory system that stores what works -- your best audiences, proven budget rules, pricing strategies -- and makes that knowledge available to Claude Code in every future session. This is the section that turns a collection of tools into an intelligent system.

Section 6: Memory, Error Handling & Self-Improvement Framework

Time required: 20-30 minutes Difficulty: Beginner -- no coding required What you will have at the end: A system that remembers what it learns, handles errors automatically, and gets smarter every time something goes wrong
IN THIS SECTION YOU WILL
  • Set up CLAUDE.md as your project's persistent memory
  • Build an error handling framework that auto-resolves common issues
  • Create topic-based memory files for patterns and lessons
  • Install Error Handler, Memory Manager, and Health Check skills

Up to this point, you have built something impressive. Claude Code is connected to your Shopify store, connected to your Meta ad account, and equipped with a browser it can control through the Playwright plugin. You can create campaigns, update prices, take screenshots, and manage your catalog -- all through plain English commands.

But there is a problem.

Every time you close your terminal and start a new session, Claude Code forgets everything. It does not remember that your winter boots collection uses a 25% discount. It does not remember that the Meta token expired last week and you spent twenty minutes figuring out how to fix it. It does not remember that uploading to your supplementary feed always shows num_persisted_items: 0 and that is normal, not an error.

Right now, your system is like a brilliant employee with amnesia. Every morning, they show up and you have to re-explain everything from scratch.

This section fixes that. You are going to give your system a brain -- a persistent memory that survives across sessions, an error handling framework that resolves most problems automatically, and a self-improvement cycle that makes the whole thing smarter with every task you run.

This is the section that turns a collection of tools into an intelligent system.

6.1 The CLAUDE.md File (Project Memory)

Every project has facts that never change. Your Shopify store URL is always the same. Your Meta ad account ID is always the same. Your pixel ID, your page ID, your catalog ID -- these are constants. The API version you are targeting, the naming convention for your campaigns, the budget rules you follow -- these are decisions you made once and want Claude Code to respect every time.

The problem is that Claude Code does not inherently know any of this. Each time you start a new conversation, it starts from a blank slate. If you tell it to create a campaign, it has no idea which ad account to use, what naming format you prefer, or what your budget ceiling is -- unless you tell it every single time.

CLAUDE.md solves this. It is a plain text file that lives in the root of your project folder. Claude Code reads it automatically, every single time it starts a conversation in your project. You never have to tell it to read the file. You never have to reference it by name. It just happens.

Think of it as a cheat sheet taped to your desk -- except Claude reads it before you even ask your first question.

What Goes in CLAUDE.md

Here is a template showing every section you should include. You do not need to fill in all of them on day one. Start with the essentials and add more as your project grows.

# CLAUDE.md

## Project Overview
Performance marketing management platform for [Your Store Name].
Integrates with Meta Marketing API for ads management, catalog sync,
and performance analytics.

## Account Configuration
- Ad Account: act_XXXXXXXXXX
- Business ID: XXXXXXXXXX
- Pixel ID: XXXXXXXXXX
- Primary Catalog: XXXXXXXXXX
- Facebook Page: XXXXXXXXXX
- Instagram: XXXXXXXXXX
- Currency: EGP
- Timezone: Africa/Cairo

## Shopify Configuration
- Store: your-store.myshopify.com
- API Version: 2025-01
- Theme: Dawn (or whatever theme you use)

## Meta Graph API Integration
- API Version: v25.0
- Base URL: https://graph.facebook.com/v25.0/
- Auth: Long-lived User Access Token (~60 days)
- Rate Limit: 200 calls/hour per ad account

## Important Rules
- NEVER exceed 5,000 EGP daily budget on any single campaign
- Campaign naming: [Season]-[Gender]-[Type]-[Date]
  (e.g., Winter-Men-DPA-Jan2026)
- Always exclude purchasers from the last 14 days in prospecting audiences
- Retargeting frequency cap: stay under 6 per week
- Budget scaling: never increase more than 20% per day

## Known Issues and Solutions
- Meta supplementary feed returns num_persisted_items: 0 -- this is NORMAL
- Use variant IDs (not product IDs) for Meta catalog retailer_id
- Shopify image prepending does not work for Meta -- use supplementary feed
- Token expired (Error 190): regenerate via Graph API Explorer

## Key File Paths
- Products data: data/active/products-to-process.json
- Image map: data/active/product-image-map.json
- Upload map: data/active/gallery-upload-map.json
- Feed file: data/feeds/meta-supplementary-feed-synced.csv

Why Each Section Matters

Let me walk through the sections so you understand what each one does for you.

Project Overview is a one-line summary that gives Claude Code context. It sounds trivial, but it matters. When Claude Code knows it is working on a "performance marketing management platform," it makes smarter decisions about how to structure scripts, name files, and format output. Without this line, it might treat your project like a generic code repository.

Account Configuration is the section you will reference the most -- even though you never reference it directly. Every time you say "create a campaign" or "upload to the catalog," Claude Code needs to know which ad account, which catalog, and which page to use. Instead of asking you every time, it reads these values from CLAUDE.md.

Important Rules is where you encode your business logic. This is the section that prevents Claude Code from doing something technically correct but strategically wrong. Creating a campaign with a 50,000 EGP daily budget? Technically possible. But if your rule says "never exceed 5,000 EGP," Claude Code will catch it before it happens.

Known Issues and Solutions is a critical time-saver. Remember that num_persisted_items: 0 panic? If it is documented here, the next time Claude Code encounters it, it will immediately say "this is normal behavior for supplementary feeds" instead of spending ten minutes debugging a non-problem.

Key File Paths tells Claude Code where to find the important data files in your project. Without this, every time you say "update the feed," Claude Code has to search your entire directory tree. With it, Claude Code goes directly to the right file.

When to Update CLAUDE.md

Update it whenever one of these things happens:

  1. You discover a new gotcha. If you spend more than five minutes debugging something, write the solution in CLAUDE.md so you never waste that time again.

  2. A configuration changes. New API version? New pixel ID? New catalog? Update the file.

  3. You establish a new rule. Decided that all retargeting campaigns should have a frequency cap? Add it to the Rules section.

  4. A known issue gets fixed. If Meta starts reporting num_persisted_items correctly for supplementary feeds, remove or update that entry so Claude Code does not give outdated advice.

The file lives at the root of your project directory, right next to your .env file and your .mcp.json file. You can edit it with any text editor, or you can ask Claude Code to update it for you:

"Add to CLAUDE.md under Known Issues: when creating carousel ads, the image order in the API response does not match the display order in Ads Manager. Always verify the order visually."

Claude Code will read the file, find the right section, and append the new entry.

A good rule of thumb: if you ever find yourself explaining the same thing to Claude Code twice, put it in CLAUDE.md so you never have to explain it again.

6.2 Auto Memory (Cross-Session Learning)

CLAUDE.md handles the facts that are true from day one -- your account IDs, your rules, your configuration. But what about things you learn along the way?

Last Tuesday, you discovered that a specific Shopify GraphQL query times out if you request more than 250 products at once. Yesterday, you found that Meta rejects ad creatives with more than 20% text overlay, even though they claim that restriction was removed. Next week, you will learn that a particular audience segment performs three times better when you exclude ages under 25.

These are lessons -- discoveries you make through experience that do not belong in your project configuration but are incredibly valuable to remember.

The problem is that Claude Code forgets everything between conversations. When you close the terminal and open it again tomorrow, all those lessons are gone. You are back to square one, and you will waste time re-discovering the same things.

The Solution: A Persistent Memory Directory

Claude Code has a feature called project memory. It stores files in a directory on your computer that persists between sessions. Every time Claude Code starts a new conversation in your project, it can check this directory for relevant knowledge.

The directory lives at:

~/.claude/projects/<project-hash>/memory/

You do not need to create this directory manually or memorize that path. The Memory Manager skill (installed later in this section) handles it for you. But understanding the structure helps you know what is happening under the hood.

How It Is Organized

The memory directory contains a main index file and multiple topic files:

memory/
  MEMORY.md              -- The master index (loaded automatically)
  meta-api-gotchas.md    -- Lessons about Meta's API
  shopify-patterns.md    -- Lessons about Shopify integration
  pricing-rules.md       -- Lessons about pricing and discounts
  catalog-feed.md        -- Lessons about catalog feed management
  campaign-ops.md        -- Lessons about campaign operations

MEMORY.md is the main file. Think of it as a table of contents for everything the system has learned. It contains a summary of each topic file and how many entries it has. This is the file that gets loaded automatically at the start of every session, so you want to keep it under 200 lines. If it grows beyond that, the topic files handle the detail -- MEMORY.md just points to them.

Topic files contain the detailed lessons, grouped by subject. Each entry follows a consistent format: the date it was learned, a descriptive title, the full lesson, the context that triggered it, and a prevention note for next time.

Here is what an actual entry looks like inside a topic file:

## 2026-01-15 - Meta Supplementary Feed Shows num_persisted_items: 0

When uploading a supplementary feed to the Meta catalog via the API,
the response always shows num_persisted_items: 0 regardless of how
many items were in the feed. This caused a false alarm -- the items
ARE being processed, but Meta does not report persistence counts for
supplementary feeds the way it does for primary feeds.

The items appear in Commerce Manager after 5-10 minutes.

**Context:** Uploading offer images via supplementary feed for 47 products.

**Prevention:** Do not treat num_persisted_items: 0 as a failure for
supplementary feeds. Wait 10 minutes and verify via Commerce Manager UI.

---

That is a real lesson from a real project. It took about thirty minutes of confused debugging to figure out the first time. With memory, it will never take more than thirty seconds again.

What to Save vs. What Not to Save

This distinction matters. If you save everything, the memory becomes a junk drawer that nobody searches. If you save nothing, the system never learns.

Save these things:

What to Save Example
Solutions to problems that took more than 5 minutes to solve "Meta returns error 100 when ad creative URL has a trailing space"
Patterns you have confirmed work reliably "Batch size of 50 variants per GraphQL mutation is the sweet spot for Shopify"
API quirks that are not documented anywhere "Meta catalog feed upload silently drops rows with commas in the title field unless the entire field is quoted"
User preferences Claude Code should remember "I always want campaign names in English, even though ad copy is in Arabic"
Key file paths that changed "Feed file moved from data/feeds/ to data/active/ in January 2026"
Performance insights from real campaigns "Retargeting campaigns with frequency above 4 see a 50% drop in ROAS"

Do NOT save these things:

What NOT to Save Why Not
Anything already in CLAUDE.md Duplicating information creates contradictions when one copy gets updated and the other does not
Temporary states "Campaign X is paused" changes daily -- memory is for stable patterns, not current status
Incomplete information or guesses Only save confirmed facts -- a wrong guess in memory will cause problems later
Extremely specific one-time fixes "Changed the image URL for product 7281 on January 15" is too granular to ever be useful again

Using Memory in Practice

Once the Memory Manager skill is installed (end of this section), you interact with memory through simple commands.

To save a lesson -- just tell Claude Code in plain English:

"Remember that Meta rejects carousel ads with fewer than 3 images, even though the documentation says 2 is the minimum."

Or use the slash command for structured saving:

/memory save meta-api Meta rejects carousel ads with fewer than 3 images
  despite docs saying 2 is the minimum

To search for past knowledge:

"Do we know anything about carousel ad image requirements?"

Or:

/memory search carousel

To check the health of your memory system:

/memory audit

This shows you how many entries you have, which topics have the most knowledge, which entries are stale and might need review, and whether any files are missing from the index.

To export everything to a single file:

/memory export

This creates a knowledge-base.md file with all your lessons in one place -- useful for onboarding a team member, backing up your institutional knowledge, or just reviewing what the system has learned.

The Key Insight: CLAUDE.md vs. Auto Memory

Here is the mental model to keep in mind:

CLAUDE.md    = Project facts       (what is always true)
Auto Memory  = Learned lessons     (what you discovered through experience)

CLAUDE.md answers questions like "what is the ad account ID?" and "what are our budget rules?" These are things you know before you start working.

Auto Memory answers questions like "what happened last time we tried to upload a feed with 5,000 rows?" and "what is the workaround for that Shopify GraphQL timeout?" These are things you learn by doing.

Both are loaded at the start of every session. Together, they give Claude Code comprehensive knowledge about your project -- the fixed configuration AND the accumulated wisdom.

6.3 Error Handling Framework

Errors will happen. APIs change without warning. Tokens expire at 2 AM. Rate limits kick in during your biggest batch upload. A field name that worked yesterday returns "Invalid parameter" today because Meta silently deprecated it.

The question is not whether errors will happen. The question is whether you lose thirty minutes debugging each one, or thirty seconds auto-fixing it.

The Error Loop

Every error flows through the same loop:

Error Occurs
     |
     v
Identify the Category (AUTH, RATE_LIMIT, DATA, API_CHANGE, NETWORK)
     |
     v
Check Known Solutions (search memory + CLAUDE.md for this error)
     |
     v
Try Automatic Fix (if a safe, known fix exists)
     |
     v
Verify the Fix (confirm the operation succeeds on retry)
     |
     v
Save the Lesson to Memory (so it auto-resolves next time)

The first time an error occurs, the system diagnoses it from scratch. The second time, it checks memory, finds the previous fix, and applies it immediately. By the third time, you might not even notice the error happened -- it was caught and resolved before you saw a thing.

The Five Error Categories

Every API error you will encounter falls into one of five categories. Each category has a different detection pattern, a different automatic response, and a different point at which the system asks for your help.

# Category What It Means Common Codes Auto Response Escalate When
1 AUTH Your credentials are wrong or expired Meta 190, 10; Shopify 401, 403 Verify token, guide refresh Credentials revoked by platform
2 RATE_LIMIT You sent too many requests too fast Meta 17, 32; Shopify 429 Exponential backoff and retry Persists after 5 retries
3 DATA A parameter is invalid or missing Meta 100; Shopify 400, 404, 422 Identify wrong field, suggest fix Requires business logic decision
4 API_CHANGE The platform deprecated a field or endpoint Meta 12, 2635; Shopify 410 Find changelog, list affected files Migration needs significant rewrite
5 NETWORK Connection failed, timed out, or was dropped ECONNREFUSED, ETIMEDOUT, 502, 503 Retry after delay, check connectivity Platform-wide outage

Let me walk through each one so you know exactly what to expect.

Category 1: AUTH Errors -- "Who are you?"

What it looks like: You ask Claude Code to list your campaigns, and Meta responds with "Error 190: Session has expired." Or Shopify comes back with "401 Unauthorized."

Think of this like trying to enter your office with an expired badge. The building recognizes the badge format, but the access period has ended. You need a new one.

Detection pattern: The error message contains words like "token," "expired," "session," "unauthorized," or "permission denied." The error code is 190 (Meta token expired), 10 (Meta permission denied), 401 (Shopify unauthorized), or 403 (Shopify forbidden).

Automatic response: The system checks your .env file for the relevant token. It tests whether the token is still valid by making a simple API call. If confirmed expired, it provides the exact step-by-step instructions to generate a new one -- the same process from Section 4, condensed into a three-minute checklist.

When it escalates to you: If the error is not about expiry but about revoked access -- for example, Meta disabled your app because of a policy violation, or your Shopify store changed its plan and removed API access -- the automatic fix will not work. In this case, the system tells you exactly what happened and points you to the right place to resolve it.

Category 2: RATE_LIMIT Errors -- "Slow down"

What it looks like: You run a script that updates prices on 500 products, and halfway through, Shopify responds with "429: Too Many Requests."

Think of it like a coffee shop line. You walk up and the barista says "we are slammed, come back in a few minutes." You do not walk right back 10 seconds later. You wait, check again, and if it is still busy, you wait a bit longer. That is exactly what exponential backoff does.

Detection pattern: Error code 429 (both platforms), 17 or 32 (Meta), or any message containing "rate limit," "throttled," or "too many requests."

Automatic response: The system pauses and retries with exponential backoff -- the first retry waits 2 seconds, the second waits 4 seconds, the third waits 8 seconds, the fourth waits 16 seconds, the fifth waits 32 seconds. This gives the API time to cool down without hammering it with immediate retries.

If your script does not already have retry logic built in, the system offers to add it.

When it escalates to you: If the error persists after five retries (about a minute of total waiting), the system alerts you. This usually means you need to restructure your approach -- for example, batching 10 updates into one GraphQL mutation instead of sending 10 separate requests.

Category 3: DATA Errors -- "That does not make sense"

What it looks like: You ask Claude Code to create a campaign, and Meta responds with "Error 100: Invalid parameter -- objective." The field name in your script does not match what Meta expects.

Think of it like filling out a form with a typo. You wrote "campagin" instead of "campaign." The system cannot process it because the word does not match anything it knows.

Detection pattern: Error code 100 (Meta invalid parameter), 400/404/422 (Shopify), or messages containing "invalid," "missing," "does not exist," or "unsupported."

Automatic response: The system extracts the problematic field name from the error message. It searches your project files to find where that field is used. It looks up the correct field name or format from the API documentation. If the fix is straightforward -- a typo, a renamed field, a missing prefix -- it offers to update the code directly.

When it escalates to you: If the error involves a business decision -- like trying to create an ad that violates Meta's advertising policies, or setting a price that conflicts with your discount rules -- the system cannot make that call. It explains the problem and lets you decide.

Category 4: API_CHANGE Errors -- "The rules changed"

What it looks like: A script that worked perfectly last month suddenly fails with "Error 2635: API version v23.0 is deprecated." Meta or Shopify retired the API version your script was using.

Think of it like a road closure. The route you always take to work is suddenly blocked. You need to find the new route. The good news is that both Meta and Shopify publish detailed changelogs (like road maps) showing exactly what changed and what the new path is.

Detection pattern: Error message contains "deprecated," "removed," "no longer supported," or "upgrade." HTTP status 410 (Gone). Error codes 12 or 2635 (Meta).

Automatic response: The system identifies which API version your scripts are using. It searches the platform's changelog for breaking changes between your version and the current version. It lists every file in your project that needs updating. For simple version bumps (changing v23.0 to v25.0 in URL strings), it offers to make the change across all affected files in one pass.

When it escalates to you: If the deprecation removed a feature entirely -- not just renamed it but eliminated it -- the system cannot automatically migrate. It explains what was removed, what alternatives exist, and lets you choose the new approach.

Category 5: NETWORK Errors -- "Cannot reach the server"

What it looks like: Your internet drops for thirty seconds during a batch upload, and the script crashes with "ECONNRESET: Connection reset by peer." Or Meta's servers are having a bad day and return "503: Service Unavailable."

Think of it like a phone call that drops. Neither side did anything wrong -- the connection just failed. Usually, calling back (retrying) fixes it.

Detection pattern: Error codes like ECONNREFUSED, ETIMEDOUT, ENOTFOUND, or HTTP status 502, 503, 504. No response received from the server at all.

Automatic response: The system first tests basic connectivity -- can it reach Meta's servers? Can it reach Shopify? If both are unreachable, it is probably your internet connection. If only one is down, it checks the platform's status page for reported outages. For intermittent failures, it retries with the same exponential backoff used for rate limits.

When it escalates to you: If the platform is experiencing a widespread outage (confirmed by their status page), the system tells you to wait and try again later. There is nothing to fix on your end -- you just need to be patient.

Quick Reference Card

Keep this table handy. When you see an error, find its code here for an instant diagnosis:

Code Platform Category What It Means First Thing to Do
190 Meta AUTH Token expired Regenerate long-lived token via Graph API Explorer
10 Meta AUTH Permission denied Regenerate token with all required scopes
17 Meta RATE_LIMIT Too many API calls Wait 5 minutes, then retry
32 Meta RATE_LIMIT Ad account limit Wait 1 hour, reduce batch size
100 Meta DATA Invalid parameter Check field names and types against API docs
12 Meta API_CHANGE Deprecated endpoint Check Meta API changelog for migration path
2635 Meta API_CHANGE API version deprecated Update version string in all scripts
401 Shopify AUTH Unauthorized Verify access token in .env
403 Shopify AUTH Forbidden App lacks required API scope
429 Shopify RATE_LIMIT Too many requests Add 500ms delay between requests
404 Shopify DATA Not found Verify resource ID exists and is correct format
422 Shopify DATA Validation failed Check field values and required fields
ETIMEDOUT Node.js NETWORK Connection timed out Check internet, increase timeout, retry
ECONNREFUSED Node.js NETWORK Connection refused Server down or wrong URL
502/503 Any NETWORK Server error Retry after 60 seconds, check status page

You do not need to memorize this. The Error Handler skill (installed at the end of this section) does the lookup for you automatically. This table is here so you have a quick reference if you ever want to understand what an error means before the skill kicks in.

6.4 The Self-Improvement Cycle

This is what makes the system genuinely better over time -- not just a tool that runs commands, but something that learns from experience and improves its own processes.

Every other section in this section is a component: CLAUDE.md stores facts, memory stores lessons, the error handler resolves problems. The self-improvement cycle is what connects them. It is the feedback loop that turns individual fixes into permanent improvements.

The Cycle

Encounter an Error or Make a Discovery
          |
          v
Resolve It (manually or automatically)
          |
          v
Save the Lesson to Memory
          |
          v
Update the SOP (if the documented steps need changing)
          |
          v
Update the Skill (if the code needs changing)
          |
          v
Update CLAUDE.md (if the project rules changed)
          |
          v
Next time --> the system handles it automatically

Each step feeds the next. A resolved error becomes a saved lesson. A saved lesson triggers an SOP update. An SOP update triggers a skill improvement. A skill improvement prevents the error from happening again. The cycle is complete.

Real Example: Meta Error 190 (Token Expired)

Let me walk you through a concrete example so this is not abstract.

Day 1: The Error Occurs

It is Monday morning. You open Claude Code and ask it to pull your weekend campaign performance. Claude Code calls the Meta API and gets back:

Error 190: Error validating access token: Session has expired
on Monday, 02-Mar-26 08:15:00 UTC

Your Meta access token -- the long-lived token you generated in Section 4 -- has reached the end of its 60-day lifespan. Every Meta API call will fail until you generate a new one.

This is the first time this has happened. The error handler categorizes it as AUTH, searches memory (empty -- no lessons yet), and does not find a known fix. It falls back to diagnosis mode: it checks your .env file, confirms the token is invalid by making a test API call, and presents the resolution steps.

You follow the steps. You open Graph API Explorer, generate a new short-lived token, exchange it for a long-lived one, and paste it into your .env file. Five minutes later, everything works again.

Day 1: The Lesson Gets Saved

After the fix, the error handler saves this entry to meta-api-gotchas.md in your memory directory:

Date: 2026-03-02
Title: Token expired after 60 days -- Error 190 (subcode 463)
Error: Error validating access token: Session has expired
Cause: Meta long-lived user tokens have a hard 60-day expiry
Fix: Generated new token via Graph API Explorer, extended to
     long-lived, updated .env
Prevention: Set calendar reminder for day 50 after each refresh.
     Run /health-check weekly to catch expiring tokens early.

It also updates CLAUDE.md with a new entry under Known Issues:

- Token expired (Error 190): regenerate via Graph API Explorer.
  Tokens last ~60 days. Set reminder for day 50 after each refresh.

Day 45: The Proactive Warning

Six weeks later, you run /health-check as part of your weekly routine. The health check queries the Meta token debug endpoint and calculates that your token expires in 15 days. It prints:

Meta Token: WARNING -- Expires in 15 days (2026-04-16)
Recommendation: Refresh your token within the next 5 days
  to avoid disruption.

You refresh the token that afternoon. No error ever occurs. The lesson from Day 1 prevented the problem from happening again.

Day 120: Fully Automatic

Four months in, the token expiry cycle is fully baked into your workflow. The health check warns you proactively. If you somehow miss the warning and the token does expire, the error handler recognizes the error instantly (it has seen it before), provides the exact fix from memory (no diagnosis needed), and you are back up in under three minutes.

What took five confused minutes the first time now takes thirty seconds -- and will never take five minutes again.

The Compound Effect

Here is what the compound effect looks like in practice:

Week Errors Encountered Known to System Auto-Resolved Manual Resolution Total Time Lost
1 10 0 0 10 ~80 min
2 10 4 3 7 ~60 min
4 10 7 5 5 ~42 min
8 10 9 7 3 ~26 min
16 10 10 9 1 ~10 min

By week 16, you have gone from losing 80 minutes per week to losing 10 minutes -- an 87% reduction. And the one error that still needs manual resolution is a genuinely new edge case, not something you have seen before.

This is not magic. It is just a feedback loop: encounter, resolve, save, improve. The power is in doing it consistently -- saving the lesson every single time, not just when you remember to. The skills at the end of this section automate that consistency for you.

6.5 Playwright-Powered Verification

Trust but verify. That is the principle behind this section.

When you make a change through an API -- updating a price, creating a campaign, adding products to a collection -- you get back a response that says "success." But "success" in API terms means "I processed your request without errors." It does not always mean "the result looks correct on the live page."

A price update might succeed in Shopify's database but display incorrectly on your storefront because of a theme caching layer. A campaign might create successfully in Meta's system but show the wrong creative because of an image formatting issue. A collection might update in the backend but display with missing products because of a timing delay.

The Playwright plugin you set up in Section 5 closes this gap. After making changes through the API, Claude Code can open a real browser, navigate to the relevant page, and visually confirm that everything looks the way it should.

Post-Campaign Creation: Screenshot Ads Manager

After creating a new campaign through the Meta API, verify it appears correctly:

"I just created the Spring-Women-DPA campaign. Open Ads Manager and take a screenshot showing the new campaign in the campaign list."

Claude Code navigates to your Ads Manager, locates the campaign in the list, and captures a screenshot. You get visual confirmation that the campaign exists, has the right name, and shows the correct status -- all without logging in yourself.

ads-manager-campaign-verification.png -- screenshot of Ads Manager showing a newly created campaign with correct name and Active status

Post-Price Update: Verify the Storefront

After running a script to apply discounts across your Winter Collection:

"Take a screenshot of the Winter Boots - Classic Chelsea product page on our storefront. Confirm the price shows the discounted amount with the original price crossed out."

Claude Code opens your store, navigates to the product page, and reads the displayed prices. If the sale price is showing correctly with a strikethrough on the compare-at price, it confirms success. If something looks wrong -- the discount was not applied, or the compare-at price is missing -- it tells you immediately.

storefront-price-verification.png -- product page showing sale price and crossed-out original price

Post-Collection Update: Confirm Products Display

After creating or updating a collection:

"Open our Lucky Sizes collection page and verify that products are displaying correctly. Take a screenshot of the first row."

Claude Code loads the collection page, confirms that product cards are rendering with images and prices, and saves a screenshot for your records. This catches issues like empty collections (products not properly tagged), broken images, or sorting problems.

collection-verification.png -- collection page showing product grid with images and prices

Automated Health Checks: Catch Problems Before Customers Do

Beyond one-off verifications, you can run periodic sweeps of your storefront:

"Check our homepage for any broken images, layout issues, or JavaScript errors."

Claude Code opens your homepage, takes a screenshot, inspects the page structure to identify missing images (which render as broken icons), reads the browser's console log for JavaScript errors that might be silently breaking functionality, and reports everything it finds.

This is like having someone walk through your store every morning and check that the lights are on, the signs are correct, and nothing fell off a shelf overnight. Except it takes fifteen seconds instead of fifteen minutes, and it catches things a human eye might miss (like a hidden JavaScript error that only affects one browser).

How It Works Under the Hood

When you ask for a visual verification, Claude Code follows these steps:

  1. It calls browser_navigate to open the URL in a real browser (running invisibly in the background).
  2. It calls browser_wait_for to make sure the page has finished loading before inspecting it.
  3. It calls browser_snapshot to read the page structure as text -- every heading, every paragraph, every price, every button. This is how Claude Code "understands" what is on the page.
  4. It calls browser_take_screenshot to capture exactly what a customer would see -- a real image of the page.
  5. It can call browser_console_messages to check for JavaScript errors that are invisible to users but might indicate underlying problems.
  6. It reports its findings: whether the page loaded correctly, what content it contains, whether prices match expectations, and whether any errors were detected.

You never need to remember these tool names or call them manually. Just describe what you want to verify in plain English, and Claude Code picks the right tools automatically.

Combining Verification with the Self-Improvement Cycle

The real power comes when visual verification feeds into the self-improvement cycle:

  1. Claude Code updates prices via the Shopify API. The API returns "success."
  2. Claude Code opens the storefront and checks the displayed price. It does not match the expected value.
  3. The system identifies this as a verification failure. It investigates.
  4. It discovers that the theme has a caching layer that delays price updates by 5 minutes.
  5. It saves the lesson to memory: "After Shopify price updates, the storefront may show old prices for up to 5 minutes due to theme caching. Wait before verifying."
  6. Next time, after updating prices, it automatically waits 5 minutes before running the visual check.

One unexpected result became a permanent improvement. Every future price update will include the appropriate wait time, and you will never see a false alarm again.

Skill Downloads

This section delivers three skills that implement everything you just learned. Each skill is a file you copy into your project. Once installed, it becomes a command you can invoke in Claude Code.

⚡SKILL DOWNLOAD
Error Handler
What it does: Categorizes errors, checks memory for known solutions, attempts auto-fix, and saves the outcome for next time. Activates automatically when Claude Code encounters errors.
Tell Claude Code: "Install the error-handler skill from https://raw.githubusercontent.com/mazinzaki911/agentic-ecom-playbook/main/skills/error-handler.SKILL.md"
Type /handle-error [error message] in Claude Code
  • Diagnoses Meta and Shopify errors by code or full message
  • Checks your memory files for previously solved issues
  • Auto-activates during script execution — no manual invocation needed
↓ Download Skill
⚡SKILL DOWNLOAD
Memory Manager
What it does: Organizes your system's learned knowledge into searchable, persistent topic files. Four commands for saving, searching, auditing, and exporting.
Tell Claude Code: "Install the memory-manager skill from https://raw.githubusercontent.com/mazinzaki911/agentic-ecom-playbook/main/skills/memory-manager.SKILL.md"
Type /memory save [topic] [lesson] in Claude Code
  • Saves lessons learned by topic for future reference
  • Searches across all memory files for relevant solutions
  • Audits memory health and exports a combined knowledge base
↓ Download Skill
⚡SKILL DOWNLOAD
Health Check
What it does: Verifies all connections (Shopify, Meta, token health) and optionally checks your storefront visually via Playwright. Outputs a status dashboard.
Tell Claude Code: "Install the health-check skill from https://raw.githubusercontent.com/mazinzaki911/agentic-ecom-playbook/main/skills/health-check.SKILL.md"
Type /health-check in Claude Code
  • Tests Shopify and Meta API connections in seconds
  • Checks token expiry dates and warns before they expire
  • Visually verifies your storefront loads correctly via Playwright
↓ Download Skill

What You Have Now

After completing this section, here is a summary of everything you have set up across the playbook so far:

Capability Source Status
Shopify API Section 3 Connected
Meta API Section 4 Connected
Browser automation Section 5 Configured
Error handling This section Active
Memory system This section Active
Health monitoring This section Active

Let me spell out what this means in practical terms:

  • Shopify API connected means Claude Code can read and write to your store -- products, prices, collections, inventory, orders, customers, and more.

  • Meta API connected means Claude Code can manage your advertising -- campaigns, ad sets, ads, audiences, catalogs, and performance data.

  • Browser automation configured means Claude Code can open a real web browser, navigate to any page, take screenshots, fill in forms, and verify that changes actually appear correctly on the live site.

  • Error handling active means when something goes wrong, the system categorizes the error, checks for known solutions, attempts an automatic fix, and saves the lesson. Most routine errors resolve without your involvement.

  • Memory system active means the system remembers what it learns across sessions. Every discovery, every workaround, every gotcha is saved and searchable. Claude Code never starts from scratch.

  • Health monitoring active means you can run a single command to verify that all connections are working, your token is not about to expire, your storefront is loading correctly, and your plugins are available.

This is no longer a collection of disconnected tools. It is an integrated system with a feedback loop. It learns, it remembers, it adapts, and it gets better every time you use it. That is the foundation everything else in this playbook builds on.

KEY TAKEAWAY
The error-memory-health loop is what separates a tool from a system. Every problem you encounter makes the system smarter — and that intelligence persists across sessions.
REAL TALK
Most automation guides skip error handling entirely. But errors are the reality of working with APIs. This framework means you solve each problem once, not every time.
ACHIEVEMENT UNLOCKED
Your system is now self-improving. Errors auto-resolve, memory persists across sessions, and a single health check verifies everything is working. This is the foundation that makes everything else reliable.

What Is Next

Now that you have a bulletproof foundation -- connected APIs, browser automation, error handling, persistent memory, and a self-improvement cycle -- it is time to learn how to read Meta's API documentation yourself.

In Section 7, you will learn how to navigate Meta's developer documentation so you can find and build anything yourself, not just what this playbook explicitly covers. When Meta releases a new ad format, a new targeting option, or a new catalog feature, you will know exactly where to look, how to understand the documentation, and how to tell Claude Code to implement it. That is the section that makes you self-sufficient.

Part 3: Knowledge
Sections 7 -- 8

Learn how to read Meta's API documentation like a developer, and master the art of writing Standard Operating Procedures.

Section 7: Reading Meta v25.0 Docs Like a Developer

Part 2 -- Setup | Estimated reading time: 20 minutes Difficulty: Beginner -- no coding required, just reading and clicking What you will have at the end: The ability to find any Meta API endpoint, understand its parameters, test it live, and turn it into working code
IN THIS SECTION YOU WILL
  • Navigate Meta's four documentation areas (Reference, Guide, Explorer, Changelog)
  • Follow a five-step process to turn any API doc into working code
  • Identify v25.0 breaking changes before they break your scripts
  • Install the Meta Docs Navigator skill

You have built the foundation. Claude Code is installed, connected to Shopify and Meta, your plugins work, and your memory and error handling systems are in place. You are ready to start doing real things -- creating campaigns, reading performance reports, managing your catalog.

But here is the problem. Meta's Marketing API is enormous. There are hundreds of endpoints, thousands of parameters, and the documentation spans what feels like a small encyclopedia. When Claude Code writes a script that does not work, or when you want it to do something new, you need to know where to look.

This section teaches you how to read Meta's documentation -- not as a software engineer, but as someone who needs to find an answer, understand it, and get Claude Code to turn it into a working script. Think of it as learning how to use a dictionary. You do not need to memorize every word. You just need to know how to look things up.

By the end of this section, you will be able to navigate Meta's docs, decode a reference page, test an API call in a live playground, and hand the right information to Claude Code so it writes correct scripts on the first try.

7.1 How Meta Docs Are Organized

Meta's developer documentation lives at developers.facebook.com/docs. If you have never visited, go open it now in a browser tab. It is public. You do not need to log in to read it (though you will need to log in later to use the testing playground).

The site is massive, but for our purposes -- running ads, managing catalogs, reading performance data -- you only need to visit four areas. Think of them as four different types of books in a library:

1. Graph API Reference -- The Dictionary

URL: developers.facebook.com/docs/marketing-api/reference/

This is the most important section. Every single object in Meta's system -- campaigns, ad sets, ads, catalogs, audiences, images -- has its own page here. Each page lists every parameter the object accepts, what type it is (text, number, list), and whether it is required or optional.

It is dry. It reads like a dictionary definition. There are no friendly explanations or examples. Just the raw facts: here is the endpoint, here are the fields, here are the allowed values.

But when you need the exact parameter name for a specific field, this is where you find it. Not Google. Not a blog post from 2023. The reference page.

2. Marketing API Guide -- The Tutorial

URL: developers.facebook.com/docs/marketing-api/

This is the friendlier cousin of the Reference. The Guide explains concepts in plain English. It tells you what a campaign is, how it relates to ad sets and ads, what CBO (Campaign Budget Optimization) means, and how targeting works.

If you are learning something for the first time -- "What is a Product Set?" or "How does retargeting work?" -- start here. The Guide gives you the context you need to understand what you are building before you dive into the Reference for the exact details.

3. Graph API Explorer -- The Playground

URL: developers.facebook.com/tools/explorer/

This is the hidden gem that most non-developers never discover. The Explorer is a live, interactive tool where you can make real API calls from your browser. No code needed. You select GET or POST, type in an endpoint, add your parameters, click Submit, and see the actual response from Meta's servers.

Think of it as a test kitchen. Before you cook the full meal (write a script), you can taste-test individual ingredients (API calls) to make sure they work. Did you get the parameter name right? Does your access token have the right permissions? What does the response actually look like? The Explorer answers all of these questions in seconds.

Graph API Explorer interface showing the method selector, endpoint field, parameters panel, and response area

4. Changelog -- The Newspaper

URL: developers.facebook.com/docs/graph-api/changelog/

Every few months, Meta releases a new version of their API. Each release comes with a changelog -- a list of what changed, what was added, and critically, what was removed or renamed.

When a script that used to work suddenly stops working, the changelog is the first place to check. It tells you: "In v25.0, we removed the smart_promotion_type field" or "Starting v25.0, age and gender are treated as suggestions, not hard limits." These are the kinds of changes that break scripts silently if you do not know about them.

How They Work Together

Here is the relationship between the four areas, and this is the key insight:

Guide explains the concept
    --> Reference gives you the exact parameter names and types
        --> Explorer lets you test before writing code
            --> Changelog tells you if anything changed since last time

A practical example. You want to create a campaign through the API.

  1. Guide tells you: "A campaign is the top-level container. It has an objective like OUTCOME_SALES. You create it by making a POST request to your ad account."
  2. Reference tells you: "The endpoint is POST /act_{ad_account_id}/campaigns. The required parameters are name (string), objective (enum), status (enum), and special_ad_categories (list)."
  3. Explorer lets you test it: you paste in the endpoint, add the parameters, hit Submit, and see whether it returns a campaign ID or an error.
  4. Changelog tells you: "In v25.0, the smart_promotion_type field was removed. Do not include it."

Pro tip: When Claude Code writes a script that does not work, the answer is in the Reference 90% of the time, not the Guide. The Guide tells you what to do conceptually. The Reference tells you the exact field name that the API actually accepts. A script fails because of wrong field names, not wrong concepts.

7.2 Anatomy of a Docs Page

Let us look at what you actually see when you open a Meta API reference page. We will use campaign creation as our example because it is the most common operation and the one you will use first.

Open this URL in your browser:

developers.facebook.com/docs/marketing-api/reference/ad-campaign-group/

Yes, the URL says "ad-campaign-group." This is one of Meta's quirks -- what everyone calls a "Campaign" is internally called an "Ad Campaign Group." The URL uses the internal name. Do not let this confuse you. They are the same thing.

Top of the Campaign reference page showing the endpoint URL and description

Here is what you see on the page, section by section:

The Endpoint URL

At the very top of the page, you will see something like:

POST /act_{ad_account_id}/campaigns

This tells you three things: - POST -- The HTTP method. POST means "create something new." GET means "read existing data." DELETE means "remove something." - /act_{ad_account_id} -- The path. The curly braces mean "replace this with your actual value." So if your ad account ID is act_7781591668619406, the real endpoint is /act_7781591668619406/campaigns. - /campaigns -- The object type you are working with.

The Parameter Table

Below the endpoint, you will find a table. This is the most important part of the page. It lists every parameter the endpoint accepts. Here is what the columns mean:

Column What It Means Example
Name The exact field name you pass in your API call. Spelling matters. Capitalization matters. objective
Description What this field controls. Usually one or two sentences. "The campaign's objective"
Type The data format the field expects. enum (pick from a list), string (text), int (number), bool (true/false), list (multiple values)
Required Whether the API will reject your request if you leave this out. Yes or No

When you see type: enum, that means the field only accepts specific predefined values. You cannot make up your own. The docs will list the valid options somewhere nearby -- either inline or in a linked page. More on enums in a moment.

When you see type: list, that means the field accepts multiple values in an array format. Even if you only have one value (or zero), you still need to format it as a list. This catches a lot of people -- special_ad_categories is a list, so even if you have no special categories, you must pass [] (an empty list), not leave it out entirely.

The CRUD Sections

Most reference pages are divided into four sections, sometimes with tabs at the top:

  • Creating -- How to make a new object (POST request). Lists all required and optional fields.
  • Reading -- How to retrieve an existing object (GET request). Lists all fields you can ask for.
  • Updating -- How to change an existing object (POST to the object's ID). Lists which fields can be changed after creation.
  • Deleting -- How to remove an object (DELETE request). Usually very simple.

Not every object supports all four operations. For example, you can read Insights data but you cannot create or delete it -- it is generated automatically by Meta.

Edges

This is a concept that confuses people at first, but it is actually intuitive once you see it.

An "edge" is a connection between two objects. Think of it like a folder structure on your computer:

Ad Account
  └── Campaign           (the "campaigns" edge of the ad account)
        └── Ad Set       (the "adsets" edge of the campaign)
              └── Ad     (the "ads" edge of the ad set)

When the reference page for Campaign lists an edge called "adsets," it means you can make a GET request to /{campaign_id}/adsets and get all the ad sets that belong to that campaign. The edge is the relationship -- it tells you which objects are connected.

In practical terms, edges let you navigate the hierarchy. Want all ads in a specific campaign? Follow the edges: Campaign -> Ad Sets -> Ads. Want to know which campaign an ad belongs to? Read the ad and check its campaign_id field.

Enums

When a parameter has type "enum," it means you must pick from a fixed list of allowed values. You cannot make up your own. If the list says the valid objectives are:

  • OUTCOME_AWARENESS
  • OUTCOME_ENGAGEMENT
  • OUTCOME_LEADS
  • OUTCOME_SALES
  • OUTCOME_TRAFFIC
  • OUTCOME_APP_PROMOTION

Then sending objective: "SALES" will fail. It must be objective: "OUTCOME_SALES" -- exactly as listed.

This is probably the most common source of errors in Meta API scripts. Someone writes SALES instead of OUTCOME_SALES, or PAUSED instead of... well, actually, PAUSED is correct for the status field. The point is: always check the enum list. Do not guess.

Return Type

At the bottom of the "Creating" section, the docs tell you what the API sends back when the call succeeds. For campaign creation, it returns:

{
  "id": "120345678901234"
}

That id is the new campaign's ID. You need it for everything else -- creating ad sets under it, checking its performance, updating its name. Your script should always capture and log this ID.

The Common Naming Trap

Here is something that trips up even experienced users. Meta uses different names for the same things in different contexts:

What You Call It What the Docs Call It What the API URL Uses
Campaign Ad Campaign Group /campaigns
Ad Set Ad Campaign /adsets
Ad Ad Group /ads

Yes, what everyone in the real world calls an "Ad Set," Meta's internal documentation sometimes calls an "Ad Campaign." And what everyone calls a "Campaign," the docs call an "Ad Campaign Group." This is a legacy naming issue from years ago, and it has never been fixed.

The practical impact: when searching the docs, if you search for "ad set" and get no results, try "ad campaign." If you are looking at a URL path and it says /ad-campaign/, that is the ad set page, not the campaign page.

Important: Do not let the naming confusion discourage you. Once you visit each page once and bookmark it, you will never be confused again. The URLs are stable -- they do not change between API versions.

7.3 From Docs to Working Code

Now you know where to find things and how to read a docs page. Let us put it all together with a five-step process that takes you from "I want to do something" to "here is a working script that does it." This process works for any Meta API operation -- campaign creation, reading performance data, managing audiences, updating catalog products.

The 5-Step Process

Step 1: Find the endpoint.

Search Meta's docs for what you want to do. The fastest way is to go to developers.facebook.com/docs/marketing-api/reference/ and scan the list of objects on the left sidebar. Or use Google with site:developers.facebook.com to narrow results:

create campaign site:developers.facebook.com

What you are looking for is the endpoint -- the URL pattern like POST /act_{ad_account_id}/campaigns.

Step 2: Read the parameters.

Open the reference page. Go to the "Creating" section (or "Reading" if you want to retrieve data). Look at the parameter table. Note which parameters are marked Required. Those are non-negotiable -- skip one and the API returns an error.

For the optional parameters, scan the descriptions. Many of them have sensible defaults, so you only need to include them if you want something other than the default.

Step 3: Check the enums.

For every parameter with type "enum," find the list of valid values. The reference page usually lists them right there in the description. If not, follow any links to related pages.

Do not guess enum values. Even if you think you know what Meta calls something, verify it. OUTCOME_SALES is not SALES. PAUSED is not paused (actually, Meta is case-insensitive for most enums, but you should still use the official casing to avoid surprises).

Step 4: Test in Graph API Explorer.

Before writing a single line of code, open the Explorer at developers.facebook.com/tools/explorer/:

  1. Select your App from the dropdown at the top
  2. Make sure your Access Token is filled in (you can generate one or paste your existing one)
  3. Change the method from GET to POST (or whatever the endpoint requires)
  4. Type the endpoint path -- for example, act_7781591668619406/campaigns
  5. Add your parameters in the fields panel on the left
  6. Click Submit

If it works, you will see a response with the created object's ID. If it fails, you will see an error message that tells you exactly what went wrong -- wrong field name, missing required parameter, invalid enum value, expired token.

The Explorer is your safety net. If a call works in the Explorer, it will work in code. If it fails in the Explorer, no amount of scripting will fix it -- the problem is in the parameters, not the code.

Graph API Explorer with a POST request to create a campaign, showing the parameters panel filled in and a successful response

Step 5: Ask Claude Code to do it.

Now you have the context: the endpoint, the required parameters, the valid enum values, and proof that it works (from the Explorer test). You can now ask Claude Code directly:

"Create a CBO campaign called 'Spring Collection - Sales' with a daily budget of 500 EGP, targeting Egypt, with a sales objective. Start it paused so I can review before it goes live."

Claude Code knows the correct API endpoint, parameter names, and enum values from its training and your project's CLAUDE.md. Because you understand what the parameters mean (from Steps 1-4), you can describe what you want in plain English and Claude Code translates it into the correct API calls.

A Complete Walkthrough

Let us do the full process end-to-end. Goal: create a campaign.

Step 1: Find the endpoint. We search the docs and find the campaign creation endpoint: POST /act_{ad_account_id}/campaigns.

Step 2: Read the parameters. We open the reference page and find the required parameters:

Parameter Type Required Description
name string Yes The name of the campaign
objective enum Yes The campaign's optimization objective
status enum Yes ACTIVE or PAUSED
special_ad_categories list Yes Categories like housing, credit, employment. Empty list [] if none apply

And some important optional ones:

Parameter Type Required Description
daily_budget integer No Daily budget in cents. 500 EGP = 50000
lifetime_budget integer No Total budget in cents
bid_strategy enum No LOWEST_COST_WITHOUT_CAP, LOWEST_COST_WITH_BID_CAP, COST_CAP
buying_type enum No AUCTION (default) or RESERVED

Step 3: Check the enums. The objective field must be one of:

  • OUTCOME_AWARENESS -- brand awareness, reach
  • OUTCOME_ENGAGEMENT -- post engagement, video views, messages
  • OUTCOME_LEADS -- lead generation
  • OUTCOME_SALES -- conversions, catalog sales (this is what we want)
  • OUTCOME_TRAFFIC -- website clicks, landing page views
  • OUTCOME_APP_PROMOTION -- app installs

We want OUTCOME_SALES.

The status field must be either ACTIVE or PAUSED. We will use PAUSED so the campaign does not start spending money immediately. Always create campaigns as PAUSED first, review them, then activate.

Step 4: Test in the Explorer.

We open the Graph API Explorer and configure:

  • Method: POST
  • Endpoint: act_7781591668619406/campaigns
  • Parameters:
{
  "name": "Test Campaign - API Learning",
  "objective": "OUTCOME_SALES",
  "status": "PAUSED",
  "special_ad_categories": [],
  "daily_budget": 50000
}

We click Submit. The response comes back:

{
  "id": "120212345678901234"
}

It worked. We have a campaign ID. (We can go to Meta Ads Manager and see the new paused campaign sitting there. We will delete it after this exercise.)

Step 5: Claude Code generates the script.

We tell Claude Code:

Write a script that creates a Meta campaign with these exact parameters:
- Endpoint: POST /act_{ad_account_id}/campaigns
- name: passed as command line argument
- objective: OUTCOME_SALES
- status: PAUSED
- special_ad_categories: [] (empty array)
- daily_budget: passed as command line argument, in EGP (multiply by 100 for cents)
- API version: v25.0
- Support --dry-run flag

Claude Code produces a complete script following the project's existing pattern -- with environment variable loading, the metaAPI helper, error handling, retry logic, and dry-run support. Because we gave it the exact parameter names (verified from the Reference) and valid enum values (confirmed in the Explorer), the script works immediately.
