That is the process. Five steps. Find the endpoint, read the parameters, check the enums, test in the Explorer, and ask Claude Code to write the code. It works for creating campaigns, reading insights, managing audiences, updating catalog products -- anything the Meta API supports.

7.4 v25.0 Changes You Must Know

API versions matter. Meta releases a new API version roughly every three to four months, and each version can change, rename, or remove fields that your scripts depend on. If you are following a tutorial from 2024 or using scripts that were written for v23.0, some of them will break on v25.0 -- not because the code is wrong, but because Meta changed the rules.

Here are the critical changes in v25.0 that directly affect how you create and manage ad campaigns. Read this section carefully. It will save you hours of debugging.

ASC (Advantage+ Shopping Campaigns) -- Deprecated

This is the biggest change. Advantage+ Shopping Campaigns were a special campaign type that Meta introduced a couple of years ago. They gave Meta's algorithm almost complete control over targeting, placements, and creative -- you just set a budget and let the machine learning figure out the rest.

In v25.0, ASC as a separate campaign type is gone. You can no longer create ASC campaigns through the API. The old smart_promotion_type: SMART_APP_PROMOTION field that was used to flag a campaign as ASC has been removed entirely.

What this means for you: If you see any script, tutorial, or blog post that includes smart_promotion_type in a campaign creation payload, that code is outdated. It will fail with an "invalid parameter" error on v25.0.

What to do instead: Create a standard Sales campaign (objective: OUTCOME_SALES) and enable Advantage+ controls at the ad set level. The algorithm still does its optimization -- it is just configured differently now.

AAC (Advantage+ App Campaigns) -- Deprecated

Same story as ASC, but for app promotion campaigns. AAC was a separate campaign type for driving app installs. In v25.0, it has been merged into regular campaigns.

What to do instead: Create a standard campaign with objective OUTCOME_APP_PROMOTION and enable the relevant Advantage+ controls.

Targeting Automation Changes

This one is subtle and catches people off guard.

In earlier API versions, when you set age and gender targeting on an ad set -- say, women aged 25 to 45 -- those were hard constraints. Meta would only show your ad to women between 25 and 45. Period.

In v25.0, when Advantage+ audience is enabled (which it is by default for many campaign types), those age and gender values become suggestions. Meta's algorithm treats them as starting points, not boundaries. It may show your ad to a 22-year-old or a 50-year-old if the algorithm thinks they are likely to convert.

What this means for you: If your targeting strategy depends on strict demographic filters -- for example, you sell women's boots and never want men to see the ad -- you need to explicitly disable Advantage+ audience controls. Open Claude Code and say:

"Create an ad set targeting women aged 25-45 only, and make sure Advantage+ audience expansion is disabled so Meta doesn't show ads outside that demographic."

Claude Code will set advantage_audience to 0, which tells Meta: "I mean exactly what I said. Do not expand beyond these demographics."

If you leave advantage_audience at its default (which is on), your age and gender settings are just hints. The algorithm will go wider if it thinks that will improve results.

Migration Timeline

Here is the timeline you need to know:

  • v25.0 (current): ASC and AAC creation is blocked. Existing ASC/AAC campaigns continue to run. New campaigns must use the standard structure with Advantage+ controls.
  • v26.0 (expected September 2026): Meta will automatically pause any campaigns that were created with the old ASC/AAC structure and have not been migrated. If you have old ASC campaigns still running, they will stop.

This gives you about six months from today to audit your campaigns and migrate any that use the old format. The good news: Claude Code can help you identify which campaigns need migration and create replacement campaigns in the new format.

Quick Reference: Old vs New

Old (pre-v25.0) New (v25.0+) What Changed
smart_promotion_type: SMART_APP_PROMOTION Use regular campaign with Advantage+ controls Field removed entirely
ASC-specific campaign creation Standard campaign + Advantage+ audience on ad set Merged into regular campaign flow
Hard age/gender targeting in ASC targeting_automation (suggestions only by default) Algorithm may show ads outside your specified range
Separate AAC campaign type Standard campaign + app promotion controls Merged into regular campaign flow
Read reach from Page insights New "Page Viewer" metric replacing reach Dual availability until June 2026, then old metric removed

How to Protect Yourself

Three habits that will save you from version-related headaches:

1. Always specify the API version explicitly. Tell Claude Code to use v25.0 in all Meta API calls. Never let it use a versionless URL — without a version, Meta defaults to the oldest supported version, which behaves differently and will eventually be retired. Add this rule to your CLAUDE.md:

"Always use Meta API version v25.0 explicitly in all API requests."

2. When reading tutorials or blog posts, check the date. A "How to create ASC campaigns" tutorial from 2024 will not work on v25.0. If the tutorial mentions smart_promotion_type, it is outdated. If it was written before 2025, assume some fields may have changed and verify against the current Reference page.

3. When Claude Code generates code with deprecated fields, catch it. If you see smart_promotion_type in a generated script, that is a red flag. Open Claude Code and say: "This field was deprecated in v25.0. Please update the script to use the current campaign creation format." Claude Code will fix it -- it just needs to know the field is no longer valid.

Important: When you see error messages about "invalid parameter" and the parameter name looks correct -- you spelled it right, the type is right, the value is from the enum list -- check whether that parameter was deprecated in v25.0. This is the single most common gotcha for people who are following older tutorials or using scripts that were written for earlier API versions. The parameter was valid once. It is just not valid anymore.

Skill Download

This section comes with one skill that automates the entire docs-to-code workflow you just learned.

⚡SKILL DOWNLOAD
Meta Docs Navigator
What it does: Searches Meta's documentation, extracts relevant endpoints and parameters, and generates a working Node.js script — while flagging v25.0 deprecations.
Tell Claude Code: "Install the meta-docs-navigator skill from https://raw.githubusercontent.com/mazinzaki911/agentic-ecom-playbook/main/skills/meta-docs-navigator.SKILL.md"
Type /read-meta-docs [topic] in Claude Code
  • Finds the right API endpoint for any Meta advertising task
  • Extracts required and optional parameters from the docs
  • Generates working code and flags deprecated parameters
↓ Download Skill

What It Does

The Meta Docs Navigator automates the five-step process from Section 7.3. When you type:

/read-meta-docs creating ad set

The skill:

  1. Searches Meta's developer documentation for the topic
  2. Fetches the relevant reference page and extracts the endpoint, parameters, and enums
  3. Cross-references against known v25.0 breaking changes (ASC deprecation, targeting automation changes, removed fields)
  4. Generates a complete, working Node.js script following your project's existing code patterns
  5. Explains how to read the specific docs page, so you can modify the script yourself later

If any v25.0 deprecations apply to your topic, the skill warns you before generating the code. You will never accidentally use a removed field.

Example Usage

/read-meta-docs campaign insights with breakdowns

/read-meta-docs create product set from catalog

/read-meta-docs custom audience from customer list

/read-meta-docs dynamic product ads

Each of these produces a structured API specification, any relevant version warnings, a complete ready-to-run script, and links to the source documentation for your reference.

When to Use It

Use the Meta Docs Navigator whenever you need to:

  • Implement a new API operation you have never done before
  • Verify that an existing script uses the correct field names for v25.0
  • Understand what parameters an endpoint accepts before building an SOP around it
  • Generate a starting script that you can customize for your specific needs

The skill does not replace understanding. Everything it does is based on the same five-step process you learned in this section. But it does the lookup, fetching, cross-referencing, and code generation in seconds instead of the twenty minutes it would take you to do manually.

Quick Troubleshooting

Problem Solution
Cannot find an endpoint in the docs Try the internal name. Campaigns = "Ad Campaign Group." Ad Sets = "Ad Campaign." Ads = "Ad Group." Or use the sidebar navigation at developers.facebook.com/docs/marketing-api/reference/.
Explorer returns "Invalid OAuth access token" Your token has expired (they last about 60 days) or was never generated for the right app. Go to the Explorer, click "Generate Access Token," select the permissions you need (ads_management, ads_read), and generate a fresh one.
Explorer returns error 100: "Invalid parameter" You used a field name that does not exist or a value that is not in the enum list. Go back to the Reference page and double-check the exact spelling and allowed values.
Script works in Explorer but fails in Node.js Most likely a formatting issue. The Explorer sends parameters as form-urlencoded by default, while your script might be sending JSON. Check the Content-Type header in your script. Most Meta Marketing API endpoints expect application/x-www-form-urlencoded, not application/json.
Tutorial code does not work Check the API version. If the tutorial was written before 2025, it may use deprecated fields like smart_promotion_type. Compare the tutorial's parameters against the current v25.0 Reference page.
"This field is not available in version v25.0" The field was deprecated. Check the changelog at developers.facebook.com/docs/graph-api/changelog/ for the v25.0 entry. It will tell you what replaced the old field.

What You Have Now

After this section, you can:

  • Navigate Meta's documentation with confidence. You know the four areas (Reference, Guide, Explorer, Changelog) and when to use each one.
  • Read any reference page and understand the endpoint, parameters, types, enums, edges, and return values.
  • Test API calls live in the Graph API Explorer before writing a single line of code.
  • Follow the five-step process to go from "I want to do something" to "here is a working script" for any Meta API operation.
  • Recognize v25.0 breaking changes and avoid deprecated fields that will cause your scripts to fail.
  • Use the Meta Docs Navigator skill to automate the entire lookup-and-generate workflow.

This is a skill you will use constantly. Every new campaign type, every new report, every new catalog operation starts with the same process: find the endpoint, read the parameters, test in the Explorer, generate the code. The more you do it, the faster it gets. After a few weeks, you will be able to go from "I need a script for X" to "here it is, working" in under five minutes.

KEY TAKEAWAY
The five-step process (find endpoint → read parameters → test in Explorer → generate code → handle errors) works for any Meta API operation. Once you internalize it, nothing is out of reach.
REAL TALK
Meta's documentation is genuinely hard to navigate at first. Their internal naming conventions (calling Ad Sets "Ad Campaigns" in the API) trip up everyone. Don't feel bad — just use the mappings in this section.
ACHIEVEMENT UNLOCKED
You can now read Meta's API documentation independently. When Meta adds new features or deprecates old ones, you'll know exactly where to look and what to do.

What Is Next

You can now read Meta's documentation and turn what you find into working code. In Section 8: What Is an SOP & Writing Your First One, you will learn what a Standard Operating Procedure is and why it is the single most important concept in this entire playbook. You will write your first SOP from scratch -- a real, working recipe that checks today's revenue from your Shopify store. SOPs are what turn your knowledge into repeatable steps that anyone (or any AI agent) can follow, every single time.

[End of Section 7]

Section 8: What Is an SOP & Writing Your First One

Part 3 -- Knowledge | Estimated reading time: 15 minutes
IN THIS SECTION YOU WILL
  • Understand what an SOP is and why it's the most important concept in the playbook
  • Write your first SOP from scratch — a real revenue-checking procedure
  • Learn the standard SOP template used throughout Parts 4 and 5
  • Install the SOP Builder skill

You have spent the first seven sections setting things up. You installed Claude Code. You connected it to Shopify. You connected it to Meta. You configured plugins, set up memory, built error handling, and learned how to read API documentation.

That was the foundation. Starting now, we shift from setting up the kitchen to writing the recipes.

This section introduces the single most important concept in the entire playbook: the Standard Operating Procedure. By the end of it, you will know what an SOP is, why it matters more than any individual script, and you will have written your first one from scratch -- a real, working SOP that checks today's revenue from your Shopify store.

8.1 What Is an SOP?

An SOP -- a Standard Operating Procedure -- is a written set of step-by-step instructions for completing a specific task the same way every time.

That is the whole idea. No fancy management theory. No business school jargon. Just a recipe.

And that is exactly the right word for it: a recipe. If you have ever followed a cooking recipe, you already understand SOPs completely. A recipe tells you:

  • The ingredients you need (chicken, garlic, cumin, olive oil)
  • The steps to follow, in order (marinate, preheat, roast, rest)
  • What the result should look like ("golden brown, internal temperature 75C")
  • What to do if something goes wrong ("too dry? baste with butter")

An SOP does the exact same thing, except instead of making chicken shawarma, you are creating a Meta ad campaign, updating Shopify prices, or checking your daily revenue.

Here is a side-by-side comparison to make the analogy concrete:

Cooking Recipe SOP
Title: "Chicken Shawarma" Title: "Check Today's Revenue"
Ingredients: chicken, garlic, cumin... Prerequisites: .env file, Shopify credentials, Node.js
Preheat oven to 200C Step 1: Load environment variables
Mix spices in a bowl Step 2: Query the Shopify API
Marinate for 2 hours Step 3: Sum the order totals
"Chicken should be golden brown" Expected output: "Total revenue: 15,420 EGP"
"Too dry? Add yogurt" Troubleshooting: "No orders? Check timezone"

The analogy works all the way through. And just like a good recipe, a good SOP should be so clear that someone who has never done the task before can follow it without guessing.

Why You Need SOPs

You might be thinking: "I know how to check my revenue. I just open Shopify and look at the dashboard. Why would I write down something I already know how to do?"

Four reasons. Every single one will save you real pain at some point.

1. Consistency -- the task gets done the same way every time, by anyone.

Right now you check revenue by opening Shopify, eyeballing the dashboard number, and maybe doing some mental math. Tomorrow you might forget to filter by paid orders and accidentally include pending ones. Next week you might check a different metric entirely because you are in a hurry.

An SOP eliminates that drift. The steps are the same every time. The query is the same. The output format is the same. Whether you run it on a Monday morning or a Friday night, whether you are alert or exhausted, the result is reliable.

2. Delegation -- hand the SOP to someone new and they can do the task.

When you hire a new team member -- a virtual assistant, a media buyer, a part-time helper -- the training conversation usually goes like this: you sit next to them for a few days, show them how you do things, answer their questions, and hope they remember it all. Two weeks later, they ask you the same questions again.

With SOPs, training looks different. You hand them a folder of recipes and say: "Follow these." They do. If they get stuck, the troubleshooting section has the answer. If they need to verify their work, the verification section tells them how. You just freed up days of your time.

3. Automation -- Claude Code can follow SOPs to do the task for you.

This is the big one for this playbook. Claude Code can follow an SOP exactly like a human would. It reads the steps, executes the code, checks the expected output, and handles errors using the troubleshooting section. But it can only do this if the SOP exists.

Think of it this way: you cannot teach someone a recipe by saying "just make it the way I usually do." You need to write it down. The same applies to Claude Code. Once a task is documented as an SOP, it becomes a blueprint for a Claude Skill that automates it completely.

The pipeline is simple:

Knowledge in your head
    --> Written down as an SOP
        --> Converted into a Claude Skill
            --> Executed automatically on command

No SOP, no automation. It all starts with the recipe.

4. Improvement -- when you find a better way, update the SOP and everyone benefits.

How many times have you forgotten to add UTM parameters to a campaign URL? Or applied a discount to the wrong collection? Or uploaded a feed without checking that the variant IDs were correct?

When the steps are written down with checkboxes and expected outputs, mistakes drop dramatically. And when you do discover a better approach -- a faster query, a smarter filter, a more reliable check -- you update the SOP once, and every person and every automated skill that uses it gets the improvement instantly.

Without SOPs vs. With SOPs

Without SOPs, knowledge is locked in your head. Every new hire starts from scratch. You cannot take a day off without things falling apart. Your "system" is really just your personal memory -- and memory is fragile, inconsistent, and not searchable at 2 AM when something breaks.

With SOPs, your business runs on documented processes, not on any single person's memory. You are replaceable (in the best possible way). Your team can operate independently. And your AI agent has a complete instruction manual for every task you care about.

The difference is not theoretical. It is the difference between a restaurant where only the head chef can make the signature dish and a restaurant where any cook can make it -- because the recipe is written down, tested, and available in the kitchen.

8.2 Anatomy of a Good SOP

Not all SOPs are created equal. A bad SOP is worse than no SOP at all, because it gives you false confidence that a task is documented when it actually is not. You follow the steps, something goes wrong, and the SOP has no answer. That is a recipe with missing ingredients -- frustrating and useless.

A good SOP is complete, specific, and testable. Every SOP in this playbook follows the same template. Here is the skeleton, and then we will walk through every section:

# SOP: [Task Name]

Status: Tested (or Draft, or Broken)
API Version: v25.0 (or 2025-01 for Shopify)
Last Updated: 2026-03-02
Estimated Time: X minutes (manual) / Y seconds (automated)

## Quick Reference
One-line summary of what this SOP does and when to use it.

## Prerequisites
<div class="checklist-item" style="margin-left:0px"><span class="check-icon">&#9744;</span> What must be true before starting (credentials, connections, data)</div>

## Steps

### Step 1: [Action Name]
What to do, with exact commands or instructions.
Expected result: what you should see when it works.

### Step 2: [Action Name]
...

## Code
The complete, working code for Claude Code to execute.

## Troubleshooting
| Problem | Cause | Fix |
|---------|-------|-----|
| Error X | Y is wrong | Do Z |

## Verification
How to confirm the task completed correctly.

That is it -- seven sections. Now let us go through each one and explain why it exists and what makes it useful. In plain English, no jargon.

Status Badge

At the very top, every SOP has a status. Think of this as the label on a jar in your pantry -- one glance tells you what you need to know.

  • Tested means the SOP has been run against real data and it works. You executed every step, the output matched expectations, and you confirmed the result in the Shopify or Meta dashboard. This is a recipe you have cooked successfully at least once. You can follow it with confidence.

  • Draft means the SOP is written but has not been fully tested yet. The steps look right on paper, but nobody has actually run them end-to-end against a live system. Think of it as a recipe you wrote from memory but have not tried in the kitchen yet. It is probably close, but there might be a missing step or a wrong measurement. Follow it carefully, with extra attention at each step.

  • Broken means something changed and the SOP no longer works as written. Maybe Meta updated their API and a field was renamed. Maybe Shopify changed their rate limits. The SOP was working before, but it is not working now. It needs to be updated before anyone relies on it again.

Why does this matter? Because when you or a team member opens an SOP, the first thing they see is whether they can trust it. A Tested SOP can be followed with confidence. A Draft SOP should be followed cautiously. A Broken SOP is a warning sign: do not use until fixed.

Always know at a glance.

Quick Reference

This is the "cheat sheet" line at the top. It answers the question: "What does this SOP do, and when would I use it?"

Imagine you have thirty SOPs in a folder. You need to find the one that updates product prices. You do not want to open every file and read the introduction. You want to scan a list of one-liners and find the right one in seconds.

That is what the Quick Reference is for. Experienced users glance at it and get what they need immediately. New users skip it and go straight to the prerequisites. Both approaches work, and the SOP supports both.

Prerequisites

Prerequisites are everything that must be true BEFORE you start Step 1. Think of this as preheating the oven and gathering your ingredients before you begin cooking. If you skip this section and jump straight to the steps, something will fail -- maybe not immediately, but inevitably.

Prerequisites typically include:

  • Credentials: Is your .env file set up with the right tokens and IDs?
  • Dependencies: Is Node.js installed? Are the right npm packages available?
  • Data files: Does a particular JSON or CSV file need to exist already?
  • Prior SOPs: Does another SOP need to be completed first? (e.g., "Run the 'Generate Product Images' SOP before running 'Upload Images to CDN'")

The checkbox format (- [ ]) is intentional. You go through the list, check each one, and only proceed when everything has a checkmark. This prevents the number one cause of SOP failures: "I followed all the steps and it did not work because I forgot to set up my .env file first."

Steps

This is the heart of the SOP -- the actual recipe. Each step has a clear structure:

  • A name that tells you what happens ("Query today's orders," "Calculate totals," "Display summary")
  • Instructions explaining what to do, with exact commands or code
  • Expected result describing what you should see when the step works correctly

The expected result is crucial and often skipped in bad SOPs. Without it, you have no way of knowing whether a step succeeded or silently failed. You run a query and get some output -- was that the right output? Without an expected result to compare against, you are guessing.

Each step should be specific enough that there is zero ambiguity about what to do. "Query the API" is bad. "Send this exact GraphQL query to the Shopify Admin API and expect a JSON response containing an orders array" is good.

Code

This section contains the complete, working code for Claude Code to execute. Not pseudo-code. Not "add your logic here." Not "do the same thing as above but for Meta." The actual, copy-paste-ready, tested, working code.

Why so strict? Because incomplete code is the second biggest cause of SOP failures (after skipped prerequisites). If a developer writes pseudo-code and says "you'll figure out the rest," they are shifting the burden of completion onto every person who follows the SOP. That defeats the entire purpose.

The code section should be a single, self-contained script that handles everything: loading credentials, making API calls, processing data, formatting output, and catching errors. Anyone should be able to copy it into a file, run it, and get the expected result.

Troubleshooting

Things go wrong. Always. The troubleshooting section is your "what to do when the recipe fails" guide. It is formatted as a table with three columns:

Column What It Contains
Problem What you see -- the error message, the HTTP status code, the unexpected output
Cause Why it happened -- token expired, wrong ID format, missing permission, timezone mismatch
Fix How to resolve it -- specific, actionable steps (not "check your settings" but "open .env and verify SHOPIFY_CLIENT_ID matches the value in your Shopify Partners dashboard")

A good troubleshooting section covers the three or four most common failures. You do not need to anticipate every possible error -- just the ones that actually happen in practice. And every time you encounter a new error while using the SOP, you add it to the table. The troubleshooting section grows over time, becoming more valuable with every failure it documents.

Verification

The final section answers one question: "How do I know it actually worked?"

This is the step that separates a reliable system from a hopeful one. Anyone can run a script and see output in the terminal. But did the output actually land in the right place? Did Shopify actually update the price? Did Meta actually receive the feed? Did the campaign actually go live with the correct budget?

Verification typically includes:

  • A comparison check: "Compare the script's output against the Shopify Admin dashboard. The numbers should match within a few EGP."
  • A visual check: "Open Meta Ads Manager and confirm the campaign appears with the correct name and budget."
  • A repeat check: "Run the script again an hour later. If new orders came in, the total should be higher -- confirming the date filter is working."

Never skip verification. A recipe is not done when the food comes out of the oven -- it is done when you taste it and confirm it is right.

8.3 Writing Your First SOP: "Check Today's Revenue"

Enough theory. Let us build a real SOP from scratch.

We are going to document a task that every store owner does multiple times a day: checking how much revenue came in today. Right now, you probably open Shopify admin, glance at the Home dashboard or the Analytics page, and eyeball the number. That works, but it is manual, it requires a browser, and it cannot be automated.

Let us turn that into a proper SOP, walking through the entire process step by step.

Step 1: Define What You Need

Before writing a single line of the SOP, ask yourself three questions:

What platform am I working with? Shopify. The revenue data lives in Shopify's order system.

What data do I need? Today's orders, the total revenue, the number of orders, and the average order value. Nothing more, nothing less.

What should the output look like? A simple, clean summary that gives me the numbers at a glance:

Today's Revenue (2026-03-02)
-------------------------------
Orders:   42
Revenue:  18,540 EGP
Average:  441.43 EGP
-------------------------------

That is the specification. Now we know exactly what we are building.

Step 2: Identify the API Approach

Shopify does not have a single "give me today's revenue" endpoint. Instead, we need to:

  1. Use the Shopify GraphQL Admin API (which we set up in Section 3)
  2. Query all orders filtered by today's date
  3. Sum up the totalPriceSet amounts from each order
  4. Calculate the average by dividing the total by the count

The GraphQL query will look something like this:

{
  orders(first: 250, query: "created_at:>='2026-03-02T00:00:00+02:00' AND created_at:<='2026-03-02T23:59:59+02:00'") {
    edges {
      node {
        name
        totalPriceSet {
          shopMoney {
            amount
            currencyCode
          }
        }
        createdAt
      }
    }
  }
}

The +02:00 in the timestamps is the timezone offset for Egypt (Africa/Cairo, which is UTC+2). This is important -- if you get the timezone wrong, you will either miss orders from early morning or include orders from yesterday. We will address this in the troubleshooting section.

Step 3: Write the SOP Using the Template

Now we put it all together. Here is the complete, finished SOP:

# SOP: Check Today's Revenue

Status: Tested
API Version: 2025-01 (Shopify)
Last Updated: 2026-03-02
Estimated Time: 5 minutes (manual) / 10 seconds (automated)

## Quick Reference
Query Shopify for today's orders and display total revenue, order count, and average order value.

## Prerequisites
<div class="checklist-item" style="margin-left:0px"><span class="check-icon">&#9744;</span> Shopify connection configured (Section 3)</div>
<div class="checklist-item" style="margin-left:0px"><span class="check-icon">&#9744;</span> .env file has SHOPIFY_SHOP, SHOPIFY_CLIENT_ID, SHOPIFY_CLIENT_SECRET</div>
<div class="checklist-item" style="margin-left:0px"><span class="check-icon">&#9744;</span> Node.js 18+ installed (run <code>node --version</code> to check)</div>
<div class="checklist-item" style="margin-left:0px"><span class="check-icon">&#9744;</span> dotenv package installed (run <code>npm install dotenv</code> if missing)</div>

## Steps

### Step 1: Get Today's Date Range
Calculate the start and end of today in ISO format for your store's timezone.
This ensures we only count orders that were placed today in your local time,
not UTC. For Egypt (Africa/Cairo), the offset is +02:00.

Expected result: Two ISO 8601 timestamps like:
- Start: 2026-03-02T00:00:00+02:00
- End:   2026-03-02T23:59:59+02:00

### Step 2: Query Today's Orders
Use the Shopify GraphQL Admin API to fetch all orders created within
today's date range. We request the order name (for reference), the total
price in shop currency, and the creation timestamp.

Query:
{
  orders(first: 250, query: "created_at:>='2026-03-02T00:00:00+02:00' AND created_at:<='2026-03-02T23:59:59+02:00' AND financial_status:paid") {
    edges {
      node {
        name
        totalPriceSet {
          shopMoney {
            amount
            currencyCode
          }
        }
        createdAt
      }
    }
    pageInfo {
      hasNextPage
    }
  }
}

Expected result: A JSON response with a data.orders.edges array containing
each order's name, total price, and currency.

### Step 3: Calculate Totals
- Count the number of orders returned
- Sum all totalPriceSet.shopMoney.amount values
- Divide total revenue by order count to get the average order value

Expected result: Three numbers -- order count, total revenue, and average.

### Step 4: Display Summary
Present the results in a clean, readable format:

Today's Revenue (2026-03-02)
-------------------------------
Orders:   42
Revenue:  18,540.00 EGP
Average:  441.43 EGP
-------------------------------

## Code

// scripts/shopify/check-today-revenue.js

require('dotenv').config();

const SHOP = process.env.SHOPIFY_SHOP;
const CLIENT_ID = process.env.SHOPIFY_CLIENT_ID;
const CLIENT_SECRET = process.env.SHOPIFY_CLIENT_SECRET;

async function getAccessToken() {
  const response = await fetch(
    `https://${SHOP}.myshopify.com/admin/oauth/access_token`,
    {
      method: 'POST',
      headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/x-www-form-urlencoded' },
      body: new URLSearchParams({
        grant_type: 'client_credentials',
        client_id: CLIENT_ID,
        client_secret: CLIENT_SECRET,
      }),
    }
  );

  if (!response.ok) {
    const text = await response.text();
    throw new Error(`Token exchange failed (${response.status}): ${text}`);
  }

  const data = await response.json();
  return data.access_token;
}

async function shopifyGraphQL(token, query, variables = {}) {
  const response = await fetch(
    `https://${SHOP}.myshopify.com/admin/api/2025-01/graphql.json`,
    {
      method: 'POST',
      headers: {
        'Content-Type': 'application/json',
        'X-Shopify-Access-Token': token,
      },
      body: JSON.stringify({ query, variables }),
    }
  );

  if (!response.ok) {
    const text = await response.text();
    throw new Error(`GraphQL request failed (${response.status}): ${text}`);
  }

  return response.json();
}

async function getTodayRevenue() {
  try {
    const token = await getAccessToken();

    // Build today's date range in Egypt timezone (UTC+2).
    // We construct the start and end of today explicitly
    // so the query only includes orders from today.
    const now = new Date();
    const offset = '+02:00'; // Africa/Cairo

    const year = now.toLocaleString('en-CA', { timeZone: 'Africa/Cairo', year: 'numeric' });
    const month = now.toLocaleString('en-CA', { timeZone: 'Africa/Cairo', month: '2-digit' });
    const day = now.toLocaleString('en-CA', { timeZone: 'Africa/Cairo', day: '2-digit' });

    const todayStart = `${year}-${month}-${day}T00:00:00${offset}`;
    const todayEnd = `${year}-${month}-${day}T23:59:59${offset}`;

    console.log(`Querying orders from ${todayStart} to ${todayEnd}\n`);

    const query = `
      {
        orders(first: 250, query: "created_at:>='${todayStart}' AND created_at:<='${todayEnd}' AND financial_status:paid") {
          edges {
            node {
              name
              createdAt
              totalPriceSet {
                shopMoney {
                  amount
                  currencyCode
                }
              }
            }
          }
          pageInfo {
            hasNextPage
          }
        }
      }
    `;

    const result = await shopifyGraphQL(token, query);

    if (result.errors) {
      console.error('GraphQL errors:', JSON.stringify(result.errors, null, 2));
      return;
    }

    const orders = result.data.orders.edges;

    if (orders.length === 0) {
      console.log('No paid orders found for today yet.');
      return;
    }

    // Sum the totals
    let totalRevenue = 0;
    const currency = orders[0].node.totalPriceSet.shopMoney.currencyCode;

    for (const edge of orders) {
      const amount = parseFloat(edge.node.totalPriceSet.shopMoney.amount);
      totalRevenue += amount;
    }

    const avgOrder = totalRevenue / orders.length;

    // Display results
    const dateStr = `${year}-${month}-${day}`;
    console.log(`Today's Revenue (${dateStr})`);
    console.log('-'.repeat(35));
    console.log(`Orders:   ${orders.length}`);
    console.log(`Revenue:  ${totalRevenue.toLocaleString('en-US', {
      minimumFractionDigits: 2,
      maximumFractionDigits: 2,
    })} ${currency}`);
    console.log(`Average:  ${avgOrder.toLocaleString('en-US', {
      minimumFractionDigits: 2,
      maximumFractionDigits: 2,
    })} ${currency}`);
    console.log('-'.repeat(35));

    if (result.data.orders.pageInfo.hasNextPage) {
      console.log('\nNOTE: More than 250 orders today.');
      console.log('This total covers the first 250 only.');
      console.log('Add pagination for the exact total.');
    }

  } catch (error) {
    console.error('Error:', error.message);
  }
}

getTodayRevenue();

## Troubleshooting

| Problem | Cause | Fix |
|---|---|---|
| Revenue shows 0 or "No paid orders found" but you know there are orders | Wrong timezone. Your date range starts at midnight UTC instead of midnight Cairo time. | Check the timezone offset in the script. Egypt is `+02:00`. If your system clock is set to a different timezone, the `todayStart` and `todayEnd` values will be wrong. Print them out and verify they match your local midnight. |
| `Token exchange failed (401)` | Wrong Client ID or Client Secret in `.env` | Open `.env` and verify `SHOPIFY_CLIENT_ID` and `SHOPIFY_CLIENT_SECRET` match what the Shopify Partners dashboard shows. No extra spaces. No quotes around the values. Copy-paste fresh. |
| `ENOTFOUND` or DNS error | `SHOPIFY_SHOP` contains the full URL instead of just the store name | Open `.env` and make sure `SHOPIFY_SHOP` is just the store name (e.g., `my-store`), not `my-store.myshopify.com` and not `https://my-store.myshopify.com`. |
| Missing orders (count is lower than expected) | The `financial_status:paid` filter excludes pending and refunded orders | If you want ALL orders regardless of payment status, remove the `AND financial_status:paid` part of the query. |
| "More than 250 orders" warning | Shopify GraphQL returns max 250 items per request | If your store regularly gets 250+ orders per day, add cursor-based pagination using `pageInfo.endCursor` and the `after` parameter. |

## Verification
Compare the automated total against your Shopify Admin dashboard:

1. Open your Shopify Admin (your-store.myshopify.com/admin)
2. Go to Analytics > Overview (or check the Home page "Total sales" for today)
3. The number should match the script's output within a few EGP
   (small differences can occur if an order came in between running
   the script and checking the dashboard)
4. Check the order count: go to Orders and filter by today.
   The count should match.
5. Run it twice: run again an hour later. If new orders came in,
   the total should be higher -- confirming the date filter works
   and you are not seeing cached results.

How We Built Each Section

Let us walk through the thought process behind each part, so you can replicate it for any task you want to document.

Choosing the status. We marked this as "Tested" because we ran it against a live Shopify store and the output matched the admin dashboard. If you are writing an SOP for a task you have not yet tested, start with "Draft" and upgrade to "Tested" once you have confirmed it works. Never mark something as "Tested" until you have actually run it against real data.

Writing the Quick Reference. We asked ourselves: "If someone had thirty SOPs open in a file browser, could they tell what this one does from one line?" The answer is the Quick Reference. Short, specific, and scannable.

Filling in the prerequisites. We thought about everything that must be in place before Step 1 can succeed. The script needs environment variables (.env), it needs a JavaScript runtime (Node.js), it needs the dotenv package, and the Shopify connection from Section 3 needs to be working. If any of those are missing, the script fails. So they all go in the checklist.

Structuring the steps. We broke the task into the smallest logical pieces:

  1. Calculate the date range (because getting this wrong is the most common failure)
  2. Send the query (the core API interaction)
  3. Process the results (math)
  4. Display the output (presentation)

Could we have combined them all into one step? Sure. But separating them makes debugging dramatically easier. If your total is wrong, is the date range wrong (Step 1)? Is the query returning bad data (Step 2)? Is the math off (Step 3)? With separate steps and expected results for each, you can pinpoint the problem instantly.

Writing the code. We used the Shopify Connector pattern from Section 3 -- getAccessToken() and shopifyGraphQL() -- so the code is consistent with everything else in the project. The script is completely self-contained. Copy it into a file, run it, get results. No assembly required.

Building the troubleshooting table. We listed the errors we actually encountered while building and testing this SOP. The timezone issue is the most subtle -- it caused "no orders found" even though orders existed, because the computer's midnight was two hours different from the store's midnight. The credential errors are universal to any Shopify SOP. We included them because they catch the most common beginner mistakes.

Designing the verification. We asked: "How would I prove this is correct, beyond trusting the terminal output?" The answer: compare it with the source of truth (Shopify admin). And run it again later to confirm it picks up new data. Both checks are simple, manual, and definitive.

Step 4: Test It

This is the moment of truth. Tell Claude Code:

"Run the Check Today's Revenue SOP."

You should see the revenue report. If you do not, check the troubleshooting table -- the answer is almost certainly there.

Step 5: Save It

Save the SOP as a markdown file in your project. Now anyone on your team can open it, follow the steps, and get the same result. No training session required. No "let me show you how I do it." Just the recipe.

But saving it as a file is just the beginning. In Section 11, you will learn how to convert this SOP into a Claude Skill -- turning it from a document you read into a command you type. Instead of opening the file and running the script manually, you will simply type /check-revenue and Claude Code will handle everything.

That is the SOP-to-Skill pipeline:

Your knowledge ("I know how to check revenue")
  --> SOP (documented recipe anyone can follow)
    --> Claude Skill (automated recipe that runs on command)
      --> Workflow (multiple skills chained together)

Everything starts with the SOP.

Skill Download: SOP Builder

You just learned how to write an SOP by hand. That is important -- you need to understand the structure before you automate the creation of it. But from here on, you do not have to start from a blank page every time.

⚡SKILL DOWNLOAD
SOP Builder
What it does: Asks you five questions about the task you want to document, then generates a complete SOP using the standard template. Can convert the SOP into a skill right away.
Tell Claude Code: "Install the sop-builder skill from https://raw.githubusercontent.com/mazinzaki911/agentic-ecom-playbook/main/skills/sop-builder.SKILL.md"
Type /build-sop in Claude Code
  • Interactive five-question interview generates a complete SOP
  • Auto-fills API versions, auth types, and rate limits per platform
  • Optionally converts the SOP into a skill immediately
↓ Download Skill

How It Works

When you type /build-sop, the skill runs an interactive interview. Five questions, one at a time:

Question 1: "What task does this SOP cover?" Describe the task in a sentence or two. For example: "Apply a 25% seasonal discount to all products in a specific collection."

Question 2: "What platform?" Shopify, Meta, or both. The skill automatically fills in the correct API version, authentication type, and rate limits for your chosen platform. You do not need to look these up.

Question 3: "What are the prerequisites?" List what needs to be in place before starting. Or type "standard" and the skill fills in the default prerequisites for your chosen platform (.env file, Node.js, credentials, etc.).

Question 4: "How many steps?" The skill generates that many step sections, each with a clear structure and a code placeholder for you to fill in with the specific logic.

Question 5: "What can go wrong?" List common errors you have encountered or expect. Or type "standard" and the skill fills in the common errors for your platform -- token expiry, rate limits, invalid parameters, and so on.

After the interview, the skill generates the full SOP document with all sections filled in, formatted exactly like the template from Section 8.2. It asks where you want to save it. And it offers one more option: convert the SOP into a Claude Skill right away, so you can go from "knowledge in your head" to "automated workflow" in a single conversation.

Example Session

Here is what a real /build-sop session looks like:

You:    /build-sop Apply a 25% seasonal discount to all products in a collection

Claude: What platform does this task target?
        1. Shopify  2. Meta  3. Both  4. Other

You:    1

Claude: What are the prerequisites?
        (Type "standard" for default Shopify prerequisites, or list your own)

You:    standard

Claude: Roughly how many main steps does this task have?

You:    4

Claude: What can go wrong?
        (Type "standard" for common Shopify errors, or list specific issues)

You:    standard, plus sometimes the compare-at price is missing

Claude: SOP generated! Saved to: docs/sops/APPLY-SEASONAL-DISCOUNT.md

        Would you like me to convert this into a Claude Skill as well? (y/n)

You:    y

Claude: Skill created at: .claude/skills/apply-seasonal-discount.SKILL.md
        Use it with: /apply-seasonal-discount [collection-handle]

In under two minutes, you have a complete SOP with all sections, working code structure, a troubleshooting table, and verification steps -- plus a ready-to-use skill. That is the power of having a standard template: once the format is consistent, generation becomes trivial.

Section Recap

Here is what you learned and built in this section:

What Why It Matters
What an SOP is You can now document any repeatable task as a step-by-step recipe
The cooking recipe analogy SOPs are not abstract business jargon -- they are recipes, and you already know how recipes work
Four reasons you need SOPs Consistency, delegation, automation, improvement -- each one solves a real operational pain
The SOP template (7 sections) Every SOP you create follows the same proven structure: status, quick reference, prerequisites, steps, code, troubleshooting, verification
What makes each section good You know what goes where, why it exists, and what "good" vs. "bad" looks like for each section
Your first SOP from scratch "Check Today's Revenue" -- a real, working, tested SOP you can use right now
How to build each section You walked through the thought process behind every part, so you can replicate it for any task
The SOP-to-Skill pipeline SOPs become Skills become Workflows -- documentation is the foundation of automation
The SOP Builder skill You can generate new SOPs interactively instead of writing from scratch every time

The most important takeaway from this section: an SOP is a recipe, and recipes are what make automation possible. You cannot automate what you have not documented. From this point forward, every time you do a task more than twice, your instinct should be: "I should write an SOP for this."

KEY TAKEAWAY
You cannot automate what you haven't documented. SOPs are the bridge between tribal knowledge in your head and repeatable automation that runs itself.
REAL TALK
Writing your first SOP feels like overkill for a simple task. But the second time you use it, you'll realize it saved you 10 minutes of remembering steps. By the fifth use, it's saved you an hour. The payoff compounds.
ACHIEVEMENT UNLOCKED
You can now write SOPs from scratch using the standard template. This is the single most important skill in the entire playbook — everything from here forward builds on documented procedures.

What Is Next

You now know what an SOP is, what a good one looks like, and how to write one from scratch. In the next two sections, you will get ten pre-written SOPs -- five for Meta media buying (Section 9: creating campaigns, checking performance, managing ad creatives, monitoring spend, and scaling winners) and five for Shopify operations (Section 10: product management, order processing, inventory checks, pricing updates, and collection management).

Each SOP follows the exact template you learned here. Full working code. Troubleshooting tables. Verification steps. Ready to use as-is or convert into Claude Skills.

You are moving from "learning the format" to "filling your recipe book." Let us go.

[End of Section 8]

REMINDER
The SOPs in this section generate real API calls to your live Shopify store and Meta ad account. Test each SOP with a single product or a $1/day budget before running it at scale. Verify the output after every step.
Part 4: Operations
Sections 9 -- 10

Ten ready-made SOPs covering every core task: five for Meta ad campaigns, five for Shopify store operations. Complete code, full documentation, instant automation.

Section 9: Media Buying SOPs

Part 4 -- Operations | Estimated reading time: 30 minutes
IN THIS SECTION YOU WILL
  • Get five complete Meta advertising SOPs with working code
  • Learn campaign creation, ad set setup, creative building, performance checks, and scaling
  • Install the Media Buying SOP Pack and Meta Strategy Builder skills
  • Run your first automated campaign creation via Claude Code

This section gives you five ready-made SOPs for every core media buying task. Each one follows the template from Section 8. Each one includes complete, working code. You don't need to write these -- just read them to understand what they do, then use the skills at the end to run them automatically.

Think of this section as the media buying section of your recipe book. Section 8 taught you the recipe format. Now you are filling the book with actual recipes -- five of them, covering the full lifecycle of a Meta ad campaign:

  1. Create a campaign
  2. Configure an ad set with targeting
  3. Build the ad creative
  4. Monitor performance
  5. Scale winners and pause losers

Each SOP is self-contained. You can use any one of them independently. But together, they form a complete workflow: create a campaign, set up the targeting, add the creative, check how it performs, and then scale or cut based on the numbers.

Let us get into it.

SOP 1: Campaign Creation

# Campaign Creation SOP

Status:       Verified
API Version:  v25.0 (Meta Graph API)
Last Updated: 2026-03-02
Platform:     Meta

Quick Reference

Field Value
Endpoint POST /act_{ad_account_id}/campaigns
Method POST
Auth Meta User Access Token
Rate Limit 200 calls/hour per ad account
Base URL https://graph.facebook.com/v25.0/

One-sentence summary: Create a new Meta ad campaign with the correct objective, budget strategy, and settings.

Prerequisites

☐ .env file configured with META_ACCESS_TOKEN and META_AD_ACCOUNT_ID
☐ Meta access token is valid (not expired -- tokens last ~60 days)
☐ Node.js 18+ installed
☐ dotenv package installed (npm install dotenv)
☐ You know which campaign objective you need (see Step 1 below)

Step 1: Choose Your Campaign Objective

What: Decide what you want Meta to optimize for. This is the single most important decision in the entire campaign setup.

Why: The objective tells Meta's algorithm what kind of result you are paying for. If you pick the wrong objective, Meta will optimize for the wrong thing. Choose "traffic" when you want purchases, and you will get lots of cheap clicks from people who never buy. Choose "sales" and Meta will find the people most likely to pull out their credit card.

Here are the five objectives available in the current API (v25.0):

Objective API Value When to Use Example
Sales OUTCOME_SALES You want purchases on your website "Buy these winter boots"
Leads OUTCOME_LEADS You want form submissions or sign-ups "Sign up for our VIP list"
Traffic OUTCOME_TRAFFIC You want website visits (not purchases) "Check out our new collection"
Awareness OUTCOME_AWARENESS You want reach and impressions "We just opened a new store"
Engagement OUTCOME_ENGAGEMENT You want likes, comments, shares "What's your favorite style?"

For 90% of e-commerce campaigns, use OUTCOME_SALES. The other objectives have their place, but if you are selling products online, sales is almost always the right choice. Traffic sounds appealing ("more visitors!"), but visitors who do not buy are just a number on a dashboard.

Step 2: Choose Your Budget Strategy

What: Decide how Meta allocates your daily spend across ad sets within this campaign.

Why: Meta offers two approaches, and picking the wrong one can waste your budget on underperforming audiences.

CBO -- Campaign Budget Optimization:

With CBO, you set one budget at the campaign level, and Meta distributes it across your ad sets automatically. If Ad Set A is performing well and Ad Set B is not, Meta shifts more money to A.

  • Best for: Scaling. When you have multiple ad sets and want Meta to find the best one.
  • Set: daily_budget on the campaign (not on individual ad sets).
  • Think of it as: "Here is 1,000 EGP. Spend it wherever it works best."

ABO -- Ad Set Budget Optimization:

With ABO, you set individual budgets on each ad set. Each ad set gets exactly what you assign it, regardless of performance.

  • Best for: Testing. When you want to give each audience a fair shot with equal spend.
  • Set: daily_budget on each ad set (not on the campaign).
  • Think of it as: "Give 500 EGP to audience A and 500 EGP to audience B. I will compare them myself."

Important budget detail: Meta's API expects budgets in cents (the smallest currency unit). So 500 EGP is sent as 50000. One thousand EGP is 100000. If you send 500, Meta will create a campaign with a budget of 5 EGP -- probably not what you intended.

Step 3: Set Advantage+ Controls

What: Configure Meta's automated optimization features for the campaign.

Why: Meta's v25.0 API introduced Advantage+ controls, which let Meta automatically adjust parts of your campaign (targeting, placements, creative) to improve performance. For most e-commerce campaigns, you want to let Meta handle some of this -- but not all of it.

The key field is bid_strategy. Here are your options:

Strategy API Value What It Does
Lowest Cost LOWEST_COST_WITHOUT_CAP Meta gets you the most results for your budget. No cap on what it pays per result. This is the default and works well for most campaigns.
Cost Cap COST_CAP You set a maximum cost per result (e.g., "do not pay more than 200 EGP per purchase"). Meta stays under that cap. Good when you know your target CPA.
Bid Cap BID_CAP You set a maximum bid for each auction. More aggressive than Cost Cap. For advanced users only.
Minimum ROAS MINIMUM_ROAS You set a minimum return on ad spend (e.g., "I want at least 3x ROAS"). Meta only spends when it expects to hit your target.

Start with LOWEST_COST_WITHOUT_CAP. It gives Meta the most flexibility to find cheap conversions. Switch to COST_CAP once you know your target CPA from real data.

Step 4: Configure Special Ad Categories

What: Tell Meta whether your ads fall into a regulated category.

Why: Certain types of ads -- housing, employment, credit, and social/political issues -- have legal restrictions on targeting. If your ads fall into one of these categories, you must declare it. If they do not (which is the case for most e-commerce stores), you send an empty array.

For a fashion/footwear store: "special_ad_categories": []

If you sell financial products, job listings, or real estate, check Meta's documentation for the correct category value.

Step 5: Create the Campaign via API

What: Send a POST request to Meta's API to create the campaign.

Why: This is the actual creation step. Everything before this was preparation -- choosing the objective, budget strategy, and settings. Now we assemble those choices into a single API call.

Open Claude Code and say:

"Create a Meta Sales campaign called 'Spring Collection - Sales' with a daily budget of 500 EGP. Use lowest cost bidding and set it to PAUSED so I can review before it goes live."

Claude Code will: 1. Read your .env for the ad account ID and access token 2. Call the Meta API with the correct endpoint, objective (OUTCOME_SALES), budget (converted to cents automatically), and bid strategy 3. Report back with the campaign ID and a link to view it in Ads Manager

Note on budgets: Meta's API expects budgets in cents (smallest currency unit). Claude Code handles this conversion automatically — you just say "500 EGP" and it sends 50000 to the API. If you ever create a campaign manually, remember to multiply by 100.

What Claude Code will show you:

It will confirm the campaign name, objective, budget, and status, then give you the Campaign ID and a direct link to Ads Manager where you can review everything before activating.

Troubleshooting

Error Code Cause Fix
"Invalid parameter" 100 Wrong objective value or missing required field Check that objective is one of the five valid values listed in Step 1. Verify special_ad_categories is present (even as empty array).
"Invalid OAuth 2.0 Access Token" 190 Token expired or malformed Generate a new long-lived token. Tokens expire after ~60 days.
"User request limit reached" 17 Too many API calls too quickly Wait 5 minutes and try again. Implement exponential backoff if running batch scripts.
"The daily budget must be at least..." 100 Budget too low for the target country Meta has minimum daily budgets per country. For Egypt, the minimum is roughly 20 EGP (2000 cents). Increase your budget.
"Ad account is disabled" 294 Your ad account has been restricted Check Meta Business Suite for any policy violations. Contact Meta support if needed.
"Bid strategy is not compatible" 100 Mismatch between bid strategy and campaign settings MINIMUM_ROAS requires promoted_object with pixel_id. COST_CAP requires bid_amount. Use LOWEST_COST_WITHOUT_CAP to start.
Campaign budget shows wrong amount -- Forgot to multiply by 100 Pause the campaign immediately. Update the budget: POST /{campaign_id} with corrected daily_budget in cents.

Verification

  1. Check the API response. The response should include an id field with a numeric campaign ID. If you see an error object instead, something went wrong -- check the troubleshooting table.

  2. Open Ads Manager. Navigate to the Ads Manager URL printed in the output. Confirm:

  3. Campaign name matches what you set
  4. Budget shows the correct amount (500 EGP, not 5 EGP)
  5. Status is PAUSED

  6. Objective shows "Sales" (or whichever you chose)

  7. Ask Claude Code to verify. Say: "Show me the details of campaign [campaign ID] — name, objective, status, budget, and bid strategy." Claude Code will query the API and confirm everything matches what you requested.

SOP 2: Ad Set Configuration & Targeting

# Ad Set Configuration & Targeting SOP

Status:       Verified
API Version:  v25.0 (Meta Graph API)
Last Updated: 2026-03-02
Platform:     Meta

Quick Reference

Field Value
Endpoint POST /act_{ad_account_id}/adsets
Method POST
Auth Meta User Access Token
Rate Limit 200 calls/hour per ad account
Depends On SOP 1 (Campaign Creation) -- you need a campaign_id

One-sentence summary: Create an ad set with audience targeting, placements, and optimization settings.

Prerequisites

☐ .env file configured with META_ACCESS_TOKEN, META_AD_ACCOUNT_ID, META_PIXEL_ID
☐ A campaign already exists (from SOP 1) -- you need its campaign_id
☐ You know who your target audience is (age, gender, location, interests)

Step 1: Define Your Audience

What: Decide who should see your ads. This is the "who" of your campaign.

Why: The best ad in the world will fail if it is shown to the wrong people. A men's boots campaign shown to women who do not wear boots is wasted money. Targeting is how you make sure every EGP goes toward reaching someone who might actually buy.

Meta gives you three ways to build an audience:

Approach 1: Interest-Based Targeting

You tell Meta: "Show my ads to people in Egypt who are interested in shoes, fashion, and online shopping." Meta uses its data to find these people.

To find valid interest targeting options, ask Claude Code:

"Search Meta's targeting interests for 'shoes' and show me the available options with their audience sizes."

Claude Code will query Meta's targeting search API and show you a list of matching interests with their names and audience sizes. The audience size tells you how many people match that interest globally — bigger is not always better. For a niche product, a smaller, more specific interest often performs better than a broad one.

Approach 2: Custom Audiences (Retargeting)

Custom Audiences let you target people who have already interacted with your business -- website visitors, email subscribers, app users, or people who engaged with your Facebook page.

To use a Custom Audience, you need its ID. You can create one through the API or in Ads Manager. The most common types for e-commerce:

Custom Audience Type Who It Targets How Long to Keep
Website Visitors People who visited your site (via Pixel) 7-30 days
Add to Cart People who added items to cart but did not buy 7-14 days
Purchasers People who completed a purchase 30-180 days
Email List Your email subscribers, uploaded to Meta Until you update the list
Page Engagers People who liked/commented/shared your page content 30-90 days

Approach 3: Lookalike Audiences

A Lookalike Audience takes one of your Custom Audiences (say, your past purchasers) and asks Meta to find new people who look similar. "Find me more people who behave like my best customers."

Lookalike audiences are specified by a percentage: 1% is the most similar (smallest audience, highest quality), 10% is the broadest (largest audience, lower quality).

For a new campaign, start with interest-based targeting. Once you have enough purchase data (at least 100 purchases tracked by your Pixel), create a Lookalike based on purchasers. This is usually the highest-performing audience for e-commerce.

Step 2: Set Demographics

What: Define the age range, gender, and geographic locations for your audience.

Why: Even within an interest-based audience, you want to narrow down to the people most likely to buy. If your products are men's boots sized 39-45, showing ads to 18-year-olds or women is not efficient.

Parameter API Field Example Notes
Minimum age age_min 25 Meta minimum is 18
Maximum age age_max 45 Meta maximum is 65+
Gender genders [1] for male, [2] for female Omit or use [0] for all genders
Countries geo_locations.countries ["EG"] ISO country codes
Cities geo_locations.cities [{"key": "2343528", "name": "Cairo"}] Optional -- use for local targeting

Finding city keys: Use GET /search?type=adgeolocation&q=Cairo&location_types=city&access_token={TOKEN} to find the correct key for any city.

Step 3: Choose Placements

What: Decide where your ads appear -- Facebook Feed, Instagram Reels, Stories, Audience Network, etc.

Why: Different placements reach different people at different costs. Instagram Reels might have cheaper CPMs (cost per thousand impressions) but lower purchase intent. Facebook Feed might cost more but convert better. Your choice depends on your creative format and your audience's behavior.

Advantage+ Placements (Automatic):

Let Meta decide where to place your ads. This is the recommended option for most campaigns. Meta's algorithm tests all available placements and shifts budget to whichever performs best.

To use Advantage+ placements, simply omit the publisher_platforms, facebook_positions, and instagram_positions fields from your targeting. Meta will automatically use all available placements.

Manual Placements:

If you want control, tell Claude Code which placements to use when creating the ad set:

"Only show ads on Facebook Feed, Stories, and Reels, plus Instagram Feed, Stories, Reels, and Explore. No Audience Network."

Platform Position API Value Best For
Facebook Feed feed Image ads, carousel, video
Facebook Stories story Vertical video/image (9:16)
Facebook Reels reels Short video (< 30s)
Facebook Right Column right_hand_column Retargeting (cheap, low volume)
Instagram Feed stream Image ads, carousel
Instagram Stories story Vertical video/image (9:16)
Instagram Reels reels Short video (< 30s)
Instagram Explore explore Discovery-oriented ads

Start with Advantage+ (automatic) placements. Only switch to manual if you have specific creative that only works in certain formats (e.g., you only have square images, so you exclude Stories and Reels which need vertical).

Step 4: Set Optimization Goal

What: Tell Meta what specific result to optimize for within your campaign objective.

Why: Even within a "Sales" campaign, you can optimize for different things. "Optimize for purchases" tells Meta to find people likely to buy. "Optimize for link clicks" tells Meta to find people likely to click -- which is a very different (and cheaper, and less valuable) audience.

Optimization Goal API Value When to Use
Conversions (purchases) OFFSITE_CONVERSIONS You want purchases. This is the default for OUTCOME_SALES campaigns.
Link Clicks LINK_CLICKS You want traffic. Cheaper but lower intent.
Landing Page Views LANDING_PAGE_VIEWS Like link clicks, but only counts people whose page fully loaded.
Impressions IMPRESSIONS You want maximum reach. No optimization for actions.
Daily Unique Reach REACH Show to as many unique people as possible. Good for awareness.
Value VALUE Optimize for highest purchase value, not just purchase count. Advanced.

For e-commerce, the combination is almost always: - optimization_goal: "OFFSITE_CONVERSIONS" - promoted_object.custom_event_type: "PURCHASE"

This tells Meta: "Find people who are most likely to complete a purchase on my website."

Step 5: Set Bid Strategy

What: Tell Meta how much you are willing to pay for each result.

Why: This controls your cost. The bid strategy you set at the ad set level works within the campaign-level bid strategy. For most e-commerce ad sets optimizing for purchases, use LOWEST_COST_WITHOUT_CAP to let Meta find the cheapest conversions.

If you know your target CPA (cost per acquisition), tell Claude Code:

"Use cost cap bidding with a maximum CPA of 200 EGP."

That tells Meta: "Try to get me purchases for 200 EGP or less."

Step 6: Create the Ad Set via API

What: Assemble all the choices from Steps 1-5 into a single API call.

Why: This is where everything comes together. The ad set is the container that holds your audience, placements, optimization, and budget. A campaign can have multiple ad sets, each targeting a different audience.

Open Claude Code and say:

"Create an ad set called 'Men 25-45 - Shoes Interest - Egypt' under campaign [your campaign ID]. Target men aged 25-45 in Egypt who are interested in Shoes and Shoe shops. Optimize for purchases using my pixel. Set daily budget to 250 EGP and start it PAUSED."

Claude Code will: 1. Read your .env for the ad account ID, access token, and pixel ID 2. Build the correct targeting object with demographics, interests, and geographic location 3. Set optimization for purchases (OFFSITE_CONVERSIONS) with your pixel 4. Create the ad set via the Meta API 5. Report back with the ad set ID

What Claude Code will show you:

It will confirm the ad set name, targeting (age, gender, country, interests), optimization goal, budget, and return the ad set ID.

Troubleshooting

Error Code Cause Fix
"Invalid parameter: targeting" 100 Malformed targeting object -- missing required field or wrong structure Verify that geo_locations has at least one country. Check that interest IDs are valid numbers (use the targeting search API to confirm).
"campaign_id is required" 100 Missing or invalid campaign ID Confirm the campaign was created (SOP 1) and copy the exact ID from the response.
"promoted_object is required" 100 Missing pixel_id or custom_event_type Add promoted_object with your pixel_id and custom_event_type: "PURCHASE".
"Budget too low" 100 daily_budget is under Meta's minimum for the target country For Egypt, use at least 2000 cents (20 EGP). For best results, use at least 5000 cents (50 EGP).
"Invalid interest ID" 100 The interest ID does not exist or was deprecated Use the targeting search API (/targetingsearch?q=...) to find current, valid interest IDs.
"Custom audience not found" 100 The custom audience ID does not exist or you lack permission Verify the audience ID in Ads Manager under Audiences. Make sure your token has the ads_management permission.
Ad set created but shows "Learning Limited" -- Audience too small or budget too low to exit the learning phase Meta needs ~50 conversions per week to exit learning. Increase budget or broaden your audience. This is not an error -- it is a signal to adjust.

Verification

  1. Check the API response. Look for an id field in the response. That is your ad set ID.

  2. Ask Claude Code to verify. Say: "Show me the details of ad set [ad set ID] — targeting, optimization goal, budget, and status." Claude Code will confirm everything matches.

  3. Open Ads Manager. Navigate to your campaign and confirm the ad set appears with the correct audience, placements, and budget.

SOP 3: Ad Creative (Image & Video)

# Ad Creative SOP

Status:       Verified
API Version:  v25.0 (Meta Graph API)
Last Updated: 2026-03-02
Platform:     Meta

Quick Reference

Field Value
Image Upload Endpoint POST /act_{ad_account_id}/adimages
Video Upload Endpoint POST /act_{ad_account_id}/advideos
Creative Endpoint POST /act_{ad_account_id}/adcreatives
Ad Endpoint POST /act_{ad_account_id}/ads
Auth Meta User Access Token
Depends On SOP 1 (Campaign) + SOP 2 (Ad Set)

One-sentence summary: Create ad creative with image or video, ad copy, destination URL, and call-to-action, then link it to an ad set as a live ad.

Prerequisites

☐ .env file configured with META_ACCESS_TOKEN, META_AD_ACCOUNT_ID, META_PAGE_ID, META_INSTAGRAM_ID
☐ A campaign and ad set already exist (from SOPs 1 and 2)
☐ You have an image file (JPG or PNG, 1080x1080 or 1080x1920) or a video file (MP4 or MOV)
☐ You have written your ad copy (primary text, headline, description)

Step 1: Upload an Image

What: Upload your ad image to Meta's servers and receive an image hash that you will use in the creative.

Why: Meta does not accept image URLs directly in the creative object. You must first upload the image to Meta, which returns a unique hash (a string of letters and numbers that identifies your image). Think of it as checking your coat at a restaurant -- you hand over the coat, and they give you a ticket. You use the ticket to retrieve the coat later.

Open Claude Code and say:

"Upload my ad image at ./media/spring-collection-ad.jpg to Meta's ad account and give me back the image hash."

Claude Code will upload the image to Meta's adimages endpoint and return the hash — a unique identifier you will use in the next step to attach the image to your creative.

Supported image specs:

Spec Feed (1:1) Stories/Reels (9:16)
Dimensions 1080 x 1080 1080 x 1920
Format JPG, PNG JPG, PNG
Max size 30 MB 30 MB
Text overlay < 20% of image area < 20% of image area

Step 2: OR Upload a Video

What: Upload a video file to Meta's servers if you are creating a video ad instead of an image ad.

Why: Video ads often outperform image ads for e-commerce, especially in Stories and Reels placements. The upload process is different from images -- videos take time to process on Meta's servers, so you need to check the processing status before using the video in a creative.

Open Claude Code and say:

"Upload the video at ./media/spring-collection-reel.mp4 to Meta as an ad video titled 'Spring Collection Reel'. Wait for processing to complete and give me the video ID."

Claude Code will upload the video, monitor its processing status (videos take time to encode on Meta's servers), and report back when it is ready to use. If processing takes longer than expected, it will give you the video ID so you can check later.

Supported video specs:

Spec Feed Stories/Reels
Dimensions 1080x1080 (1:1) or 1080x1350 (4:5) 1080 x 1920 (9:16)
Format MP4, MOV MP4, MOV
Max size 4 GB 4 GB
Max length 240 minutes 60 seconds (15s optimal for Reels)
Min length 1 second 1 second

Step 3: Create the Ad Creative Object

What: Build the creative object that combines your image (or video) with your ad copy, destination URL, and call-to-action button.

Why: The creative is the thing people actually see. It ties together the visual (image or video), the text (primary text, headline, description), the link (where people go when they click), and the CTA button (the "Shop Now" or "Learn More" button). Without a creative, your campaign is an empty shell.

For image ads:

Open Claude Code and say:

"Create an ad creative called 'Spring Collection - Feed Image' using the image hash from Step 1. Use this copy: - Primary text: 'Step into spring with our new collection. Premium leather, modern designs.' - Headline: 'New Spring Arrivals' - Description: 'Free shipping over 1000 EGP' - Link to my spring collection page with UTM tracking - CTA button: Shop Now"

Claude Code will build the creative object, attach it to your Facebook Page and Instagram account (from .env), automatically add UTM parameters to the URL, and return the creative ID.

For video ads:

Open Claude Code and say:

"Create a video ad creative called 'Spring Collection - Reel' using video ID [from Step 2]. Use this copy: - Primary text: 'Step into spring with our new collection.' - Headline: 'New Spring Arrivals' - Description: 'Free shipping over 1000 EGP' - Link to my spring collection page with UTM tracking - CTA button: Shop Now"

Claude Code will create the video creative, attach it to your Page and Instagram, and return the creative ID.

Step 4: Build the Destination URL with UTM Parameters

What: Add tracking parameters to your destination URL so Google Analytics (or any analytics tool) knows the traffic came from your Meta ads.

Why: Without UTM parameters, visits from your ads show up as "direct" or "social" in analytics -- you cannot tell which specific campaign or ad drove the visit. UTMs solve this by tagging every click with the source, medium, campaign name, and ad name.

Here are the four UTM parameters you should always include:

Parameter Value Purpose
utm_source meta Which platform (Meta/Facebook/Instagram)
utm_medium paid Type of traffic (paid ads, not organic)
utm_campaign {campaign_name} Which campaign this ad belongs to
utm_content {ad_name} Which specific ad or creative variant

Example:

https://your-store.myshopify.com/collections/spring
  ?utm_source=meta
  &utm_medium=paid
  &utm_campaign=spring-collection-2026
  &utm_content=men-feed-image-v1

The code in Step 3 already builds UTMs automatically from the creative name. If you need a custom URL, just pass it as the websiteUrl parameter with your UTMs already included.

Step 5: Choose Your Call-to-Action (CTA)

What: Select the button text that appears on your ad.

Why: The CTA button is the final nudge. "Shop Now" tells people this is an e-commerce ad where they can buy something. "Learn More" is softer and works for content-focused ads. The right CTA sets expectations and improves click quality.

CTA API Value When to Use
Shop Now SHOP_NOW Product pages, collections, any purchase-focused ad
Learn More LEARN_MORE Blog posts, brand stories, educational content
Sign Up SIGN_UP Email list, VIP club, waitlist
Get Offer GET_OFFER Discount codes, special promotions
Order Now ORDER_NOW Similar to Shop Now -- good for limited-time offers
Buy Now BUY_NOW Direct purchase, single product
Contact Us CONTACT_US Customer service, custom orders

For e-commerce, use SHOP_NOW 90% of the time. It is the clearest signal to the viewer that they can buy something when they click.

Step 6: Create the Ad

What: Link the creative to the ad set, creating the actual ad that people will see.

Why: In Meta's structure, an "ad" is the combination of a creative (what it looks like) and an ad set (who sees it). You cannot run a creative without an ad set, and an ad set without an ad does nothing. This step connects the two.

Open Claude Code and say:

"Create an ad called 'Spring Collection - Men Feed - Image v1' linking creative [creative ID] to ad set [ad set ID]. Set it to PAUSED."

Claude Code will create the ad, linking the creative to the ad set, and return the ad ID.

What Claude Code will show you:

It will confirm the ad name, which ad set and creative it is linked to, and return the ad ID.

Dynamic Product Ads (DPA) Creative

If your campaign uses a product catalog (for retargeting people who viewed specific products on your site), the creative uses a different format. Instead of a specific image and copy, you provide a template that Meta fills with the relevant product for each viewer.

Open Claude Code and say:

"Create a DPA creative called 'DPA Template - Still Looking?' with this template: - Message: 'Still thinking about [product name]? It's waiting for you!' - Headline: '[product name] - [product price]' - Description: 'Free shipping over 1000 EGP' - CTA: Shop Now - Use my product catalog's product set"

Claude Code will create the DPA creative using Meta's template placeholders ({{product.name}}, {{product.current_price}}, etc.). Meta automatically replaces these with real product data from your catalog. If someone viewed the "Classic Leather Boot" for 1,299 EGP, they see an ad for that exact boot at that exact price.

Available template placeholders:

Placeholder What It Shows
{{product.name}} Product title
{{product.price}} Original price
{{product.current_price}} Sale price (or regular if no sale)
{{product.description}} Product description
{{product.brand}} Brand name
{{product.url}} Product page URL

Troubleshooting

Error Code Cause Fix
"Invalid image hash" 100 Image hash is wrong or image was not uploaded to this ad account Re-upload the image using Step 1 and use the new hash. Hashes are account-specific.
"page_id is required" 100 Missing page_id in object_story_spec Add your Facebook Page ID. Check .env for META_PAGE_ID.
"Instagram account not connected" 100 The Instagram account ID is wrong or not linked to your page Go to your Facebook Page settings, link your Instagram business account, and use the correct ID.
"Video is still processing" 100 You tried to use a video before processing completed Wait for the status to change to "ready." Check with GET /{video_id}?fields=status.
"template_data required" 100 You used link_data for a DPA creative DPA creatives need template_data, not link_data. Use the DPA template format above.
"product_set_id required" 100 DPA creative missing the product set reference Add product_set_id to the creative body (not inside object_story_spec).
Ad rejected for policy violation -- Image has too much text, misleading claims, or restricted content Review Meta's ad policies. Use the Text Overlay tool (search.facebook.com) to check text percentage. Reduce text below 20%.

Verification

  1. Check the creative. Ask Claude Code: "Show me the details of creative [creative ID] — name, copy, image, and link." It will confirm all fields match what you intended.

  2. Preview the ad. Ask Claude Code: "Generate a preview of creative [creative ID] for desktop feed." It will show you how the ad will look to users before you publish.

  3. Open Ads Manager. Navigate to your campaign > ad set > ad and confirm the creative, copy, URL, and CTA are all correct.

SOP 4: Campaign Monitoring & Reporting

# Campaign Monitoring & Reporting SOP

Status:       Verified
API Version:  v25.0 (Meta Graph API)
Last Updated: 2026-03-02
Platform:     Meta

Quick Reference

Field Value
Endpoint GET /{object_id}/insights
Method GET
Auth Meta User Access Token
Rate Limit 200 calls/hour per ad account
Objects Works on campaigns, ad sets, and individual ads

One-sentence summary: Pull performance data, calculate key metrics, and generate a daily report that tells you exactly what is working and what is not.

Prerequisites

☐ .env file configured with META_ACCESS_TOKEN and META_AD_ACCOUNT_ID
