☐ At least one active campaign with real spend data (paused campaigns have no insights)
☐ The campaign has been running long enough to accumulate data (at least 24 hours)

Step 1: Query Campaign Insights

What: Pull performance data from Meta's API for a specific campaign, ad set, or ad.

Why: Ads Manager shows you charts and tables, but the data behind those charts lives in Meta's API. Pulling it programmatically lets you do things the UI cannot: calculate custom metrics, set alerts, compare across time periods, and generate automated reports.

Tell Claude Code:

"Pull performance insights for campaign [campaign ID] for the last 7 days. Include spend, impressions, reach, clicks, CPC, CPM, CTR, actions, revenue, cost per action, ROAS, and frequency."

Claude Code will query the Meta Insights API, handle pagination and error responses, and return the raw data that subsequent steps use for calculations.

Key fields explained:

Field What It Means
spend How much you spent in the selected period (in your currency, not cents)
impressions How many times your ads were shown
reach How many unique people saw your ads
clicks Total clicks (includes all clicks -- link clicks, likes, comments, etc.)
cpc Cost per click (any click)
cpm Cost per 1,000 impressions
ctr Click-through rate (clicks / impressions as a percentage)
actions Array of all actions taken (purchases, add-to-carts, page views, etc.)
action_values Revenue values for each action type
cost_per_action_type Cost per action for each action type
purchase_roas Return on ad spend for purchases
frequency Average number of times each person saw your ad

Step 2: Set the Date Range

What: Specify which time period you want data for.

Why: "How is my campaign doing?" is meaningless without a time frame. Today's data tells you about today. This week's data tells you about trends. Comparing this week to last week tells you if things are improving or declining.

Common date ranges:

You can specify date ranges naturally:

"Show me campaign performance for today."

"Show me campaign performance for yesterday."

"Show me campaign performance for the last 7 days."

"Show me campaign performance from February 15 to February 28."

Claude Code translates your natural language date into the correct API format.

The time_range parameter in the API accepts this format:

time_range={"since":"2026-03-01","until":"2026-03-01"}

For a single day, since and until are the same date.

Step 3: Add Breakdowns (Optional)

What: Break down the data by dimensions like age, gender, placement, or device.

Why: Aggregate numbers hide important patterns. Your campaign might have a 3x ROAS overall, but when you break it down by age, you discover that 25-34 year olds have a 5x ROAS while 45-54 year olds have a 0.5x ROAS. Without breakdowns, you would never know to shift budget toward the younger group.

Add breakdowns to the API call with the breakdowns parameter:

To break down performance by age and gender, tell Claude Code:

"Show me campaign performance broken down by age and gender for the last 7 days."

Available breakdowns:

Breakdown API Value What You Learn
Age age Which age groups convert best
Gender gender Male vs. female performance
Placement publisher_platform Facebook vs. Instagram performance
Position platform_position Feed vs. Stories vs. Reels performance
Device device_platform Mobile vs. desktop
Country country Performance by country

Do not combine too many breakdowns. Each breakdown multiplies the number of rows in the response. age,gender gives you ~20 rows. age,gender,platform_position gives you 100+ rows. Keep it to one or two breakdowns at a time.

Step 4: Calculate Key Metrics

What: Extract and calculate the five metrics that actually matter for e-commerce campaigns.

Why: Meta returns a lot of data, but most of it is noise. These five metrics tell you everything you need to know about whether a campaign is healthy or needs attention.

You do not need to calculate these yourself. Open Claude Code and say:

"Pull my campaign insights for the last 7 days and calculate ROAS, CPA, CPM, CTR, and frequency for each campaign. Flag anything outside healthy ranges."

Claude Code will pull the raw data from Meta's API, extract purchases and revenue from the actions arrays, and calculate all five metrics automatically.

What each metric tells you:

Metric Target What It Means
ROAS 3x+ for fashion "For every 1 EGP I spend, I get 3 EGP back in revenue." A ROAS of 3x means you tripled your money. Below 1x means you are losing money.
CPA Depends on margins "Each purchase costs me X EGP in ad spend." If your average order is 800 EGP and your margin is 40%, your CPA must be under 320 EGP to be profitable.
CPM Market-dependent "It costs me X EGP to reach 1,000 people." Higher CPMs mean more competition for your audience. In Egypt, fashion CPMs typically range from 20-80 EGP.
CTR > 1% for Feed "X% of people who see my ad click on it." Below 0.5% means your creative is not resonating -- time for new images or copy.
Frequency < 3 cold, < 6 retarget "Each person has seen my ad X times on average." High frequency means ad fatigue -- the same people keep seeing the same ad. Refresh your creative.

Step 5: Check Threshold Alerts

What: Compare your metrics against predefined thresholds and flag anything that needs attention.

Why: You do not want to check every number manually every day. Alerts automate the "is this good or bad?" question. If everything is green, you can move on. If something is red, you investigate.

Open Claude Code and say:

"Check my campaign metrics against these thresholds and flag any alerts: ROAS below 2x is a warning, below 1x is critical. Frequency above 4 is a warning, above 6 is critical. CTR below 0.5% is a warning. Any campaign spending over 200 EGP with zero purchases should be flagged."

Claude Code will compare your metrics against these thresholds and generate a prioritized alert list.

Step 6: Generate the Summary Report

What: Combine everything into a clean, readable report.

Why: Raw numbers are hard to digest. A formatted report lets you scan the health of all your campaigns in under a minute.

Open Claude Code and say:

"Generate a daily performance report for all my active campaigns. Show spend, revenue, purchases, ROAS, CPA, CPM, CTR, frequency, impressions, reach, and clicks for each campaign. Include a summary with totals and overall ROAS. Flag any alerts where metrics fall outside healthy thresholds."

Claude Code will pull insights for every active campaign, calculate all metrics, generate a formatted report, and append threshold-based alerts at the bottom.

Example report output:

============================================================
  DAILY PERFORMANCE REPORT
  Date: 2026-03-02
============================================================

--- Spring Collection - Sales - 2026-03-02 ---
  Spend:       1,250.00 EGP
  Revenue:     4,875.00 EGP
  Purchases:   7
  ROAS:        3.90x
  CPA:         178.57 EGP
  CPM:         45.20 EGP
  CTR:         1.85%
  Frequency:   1.42
  Impressions: 27,655
  Reach:       19,475
  Clicks:      512

--- DPA Retargeting - Cart Abandoners ---
  Spend:       480.00 EGP
  Revenue:     2,340.00 EGP
  Purchases:   4
  ROAS:        4.88x
  CPA:         120.00 EGP
  CPM:         62.10 EGP
  CTR:         2.35%
  Frequency:   3.10
  Impressions: 7,730
  Reach:       2,493
  Clicks:      182

============================================================
  SUMMARY
============================================================
  Total Spend:     1,730.00 EGP
  Total Revenue:   7,215.00 EGP
  Total Purchases: 11
  Overall ROAS:    4.17x

  No alerts. All campaigns within healthy thresholds.

============================================================

Troubleshooting

Error Code Cause Fix
"No data for this date range" -- Campaign was paused or not active during the specified period Verify the campaign was active (not paused) during the date range. Try a broader date range.
"Invalid time_range" 100 Date format is wrong Use YYYY-MM-DD format: "since":"2026-03-01","until":"2026-03-01". Both dates must be valid calendar dates.
"Unsupported get request" 100 Wrong object ID type Make sure you are querying a campaign ID (not an ad set or ad ID) at the /campaigns endpoint, or use the generic /{object_id}/insights which works for any level.
Empty actions array -- Campaign has impressions but no conversions tracked Check that your Pixel is firing correctly. Verify the Pixel is connected to the ad account. Test with the Meta Pixel Helper browser extension.
"purchase_roas" field is empty -- Meta has not calculated ROAS yet ROAS requires both spend and attributed purchases. If the campaign is new or has few conversions, ROAS may not be available yet. Calculate it manually: revenue / spend.
Rate limit reached 17 Too many insight queries in one hour Reduce the number of campaigns you query. Batch your queries. Wait 5 minutes and retry.

Verification

  1. Cross-check with Ads Manager. Open Ads Manager, set the same date range, and compare the numbers. Spend, impressions, and clicks should match exactly. Purchase counts may differ slightly due to attribution windows.

  2. Run the report for yesterday. Yesterday's data is fully finalized (today's data may still be updating). Compare yesterday's report with what Ads Manager shows for the same day.

  3. Check that alerts trigger correctly. Ask Claude Code to run the report with a lower ROAS threshold (e.g., flag anything below 10x) and confirm the alert fires for your campaigns.

SOP 5: Campaign Scaling & Budget Management

# Campaign Scaling & Budget Management SOP

Status:       Verified
API Version:  v25.0 (Meta Graph API)
Last Updated: 2026-03-02
Platform:     Meta

Quick Reference

Field Value
Endpoints GET /{object_id}/insights, POST /{adset_id}, POST /{campaign_id}
Methods GET (read data), POST (update budgets/status)
Auth Meta User Access Token
Rate Limit 200 calls/hour per ad account
Depends On SOP 4 (Monitoring) -- you need performance data before making scaling decisions

One-sentence summary: Scale winning campaigns and pause underperformers using proven rules, so you spend more on what works and stop wasting money on what does not.

Prerequisites

☐ .env file configured with META_ACCESS_TOKEN and META_AD_ACCOUNT_ID
☐ Active campaigns with at least 3 days of performance data
☐ You have run SOP 4 (Monitoring) and know your current ROAS, CPA, and frequency

Step 1: Identify Winning Ad Sets

What: Find the ad sets that are consistently performing above your target metrics.

Why: Scaling is not about increasing budget on everything. It is about finding the winners and giving them more fuel. An ad set that has maintained a 4x ROAS for five days straight is a winner. An ad set that spiked to 4x once but averages 1.5x is not.

The key word is "consistently." One good day can be a fluke. Three or more consecutive good days is a pattern.

Open Claude Code and say:

"Analyze my ad sets in campaign [campaign ID] over the last 7 days. Identify winners (ROAS above 3x for 3+ consecutive days), underperformers (ROAS below 1x for 3+ consecutive days), and ad sets still in the learning phase. For winners, recommend a new budget at +20%. For underperformers, recommend pausing."

Claude Code will pull daily insights for each active ad set, calculate daily ROAS, count consecutive good and bad days, and categorize every ad set as a winner, loser, or still-learning.

Step 2: Vertical Scaling (Increase Budget)

What: Increase the daily budget on winning ad sets -- but by no more than 20% at a time.

Why: This is the most important rule in Meta scaling, and it is the one most people get wrong. When you dramatically increase a budget -- say, from 500 EGP to 2,000 EGP overnight -- Meta resets the ad set's "learning phase." The algorithm has to re-learn who to show the ad to at this new budget level, and performance often tanks during the re-learning period.

The 20% rule avoids this. Small, incremental increases let Meta adjust gradually without resetting.

The 20% rule in practice:

Day 1:  500 EGP (starting budget)
Day 3:  600 EGP (+20%)  -- if ROAS still above target
Day 5:  720 EGP (+20%)  -- if ROAS still above target
Day 7:  864 EGP (+20%)  -- if ROAS still above target
Day 9:  1,037 EGP (+20%)
Day 11: 1,244 EGP (+20%)

In 11 days, you more than doubled your budget -- but each step was gentle enough that the algorithm never had to reset.

Never increase budget by more than 20% in a single day. This is not a soft guideline. It is a hard rule. Violating it is the number one cause of "my campaign was working great and then suddenly stopped" complaints.

Open Claude Code and say:

"Increase the daily budget on ad set [ad set ID] by 20%. Show me the current budget and the new budget before applying."

Claude Code will read the current budget, calculate the 20% increase, confirm the numbers with you, and update the ad set via the Meta API.

Step 3: Horizontal Scaling (Duplicate and Vary)

What: Duplicate a winning ad set with a different audience, keeping the same creative.

Why: Vertical scaling has limits. At some point, increasing the budget on one audience starts to show diminishing returns -- the CPM goes up, the frequency increases, and ROAS declines. Horizontal scaling solves this by finding new audiences to show the same winning creative to.

The idea is simple: if your boots ad works great with men aged 25-35 interested in shoes, it might also work with men aged 25-35 interested in fashion, or with men aged 35-45 interested in shoes. Same ad, different people.

Open Claude Code and say:

"Duplicate ad set [ad set ID] but change the targeting to women aged 25-45 interested in fashion and online shopping in Egypt. Name it 'Women 25-45 - Fashion Interest - Egypt'. Create it paused so I can review before it goes live."

Claude Code will read the original ad set's configuration (campaign, budget, billing, optimization goal), create a new ad set with the same settings but your new targeting, and remind you to copy the winning ad into the new ad set.

Horizontal scaling strategies to test:

Strategy What to Change Example
New interests Keep demographics, change interests Shoes --> Fashion --> Online Shopping
Broader age range Expand the age window 25-35 --> 25-45 --> 18-55
Lookalike audience Replace interests with a lookalike Interest-based --> 1% Lookalike of Purchasers
Different gender Test the opposite gender Men only --> Women only
Different location Test new cities or countries Cairo only --> All Egypt --> Egypt + UAE

Step 4: Pause Underperformers

What: Stop ad sets that are consistently losing money.

Why: Every EGP spent on a losing ad set is an EGP that could have gone to a winner. Pausing underperformers is not failure -- it is smart budget management. You tested, it did not work, and now you are reallocating to what does work.

When to pause:

Condition Action
ROAS < 1x for 3+ consecutive days Pause immediately. You are losing money.
Spend > 2x your target CPA with 0 purchases Pause. The audience is not converting.
Frequency > 5 with declining CTR Pause and refresh creative. The audience has seen the ad too many times.
Learning phase after 7+ days with < 50 events/week Consider pausing. Meta cannot exit learning without enough conversions. Either increase budget or broaden audience.

Open Claude Code and say:

"Pause ad set [ad set ID]. The reason is: ROAS below 1x for 4 consecutive days."

Claude Code will update the ad set status to PAUSED and confirm the change.

Step 5: Budget Reallocation

What: Move budget from paused ad sets to winning ad sets.

Why: Your total campaign budget stays the same, but the allocation shifts toward what works. This is how you improve overall ROAS without spending more money.

With CBO (Campaign Budget Optimization):

If your campaign uses CBO, Meta handles reallocation automatically. When you pause an underperforming ad set, Meta redistributes its budget to the remaining active ad sets. You do not need to do anything except pause the loser.

With ABO (Ad Set Budget Optimization):

If your campaign uses ABO, you need to manually move the budget. Reduce or pause the underperformer, and add that budget to the winner.

Open Claude Code and say:

"Move 200 EGP of daily budget from ad set [loser ID] to ad set [winner ID]. Respect the 20% rule — if the winner's current budget only allows a smaller increase, cap it and tell me how much is left to add over the coming days."

Claude Code will read both ad sets' current budgets, calculate the safe increase (capped at 20% of the winner's budget), apply both changes, and report what happened.

Notice the 20% cap in the code. Even during reallocation, the 20% rule applies. If you are moving 500 EGP from a loser to a winner that currently has a 300 EGP budget, the maximum you can add today is 60 EGP (20% of 300). The remaining 440 EGP needs to be added gradually over the following days, or kept in reserve.

Step 6: Track Scaling Decisions

What: Log every scaling decision you make -- what you changed, why, and what happened.

Why: Scaling is part science, part pattern recognition. If you do not track your decisions, you cannot learn from them. Did the 20% increase on that audience work? Did the horizontal expansion to a new interest perform? You will not remember in two weeks unless you wrote it down.

Open Claude Code and say:

"Log this scaling decision: I scaled up ad set 'Men 25-45 - Shoes Interest - Egypt' from 500 to 600 EGP because ROAS was above 3x for 5 consecutive days. Save it to data/active/scaling-log.json."

Claude Code will append the entry with a timestamp, action type, ad set details, and your reasoning to the log file.

The Scaling Decision Tree

Here is the complete decision-making process as a text-based flowchart:

START: Review ad set performance (SOP 4)
  |
  v
Has the ad set been active for 3+ days?
  |
  NO --> Keep monitoring. Do not make decisions on less than 3 days of data.
  |
  YES
  |
  v
Is ROAS > target (3x) for 3+ consecutive days?
  |
  YES --> WINNER
  |         |
  |         v
  |       Increase budget by 20%
  |         |
  |         v
  |       Monitor for 48 hours
  |         |
  |         v
  |       Still performing? --> Increase another 20%
  |       Declined? --> Revert to previous budget. Try horizontal scaling instead.
  |
  NO
  |
  v
Is ROAS < 1x for 3+ consecutive days?
  |
  YES --> LOSER
  |         |
  |         v
  |       Pause the ad set
  |         |
  |         v
  |       Reallocate budget to winners (respect 20% rule)
  |         |
  |         v
  |       Log the decision
  |
  NO
  |
  v
Is Frequency > 5 and CTR declining?
  |
  YES --> CREATIVE FATIGUE
  |         |
  |         v
  |       Pause the ad set
  |         |
  |         v
  |       Create new creative (SOP 3)
  |         |
  |         v
  |       Duplicate ad set with fresh creative
  |
  NO
  |
  v
MIXED RESULTS -- Keep monitoring. The ad set is not clearly winning or losing.
Check back in 2-3 days.

Troubleshooting

Error Code Cause Fix
"Budget cannot be reduced below minimum" 100 You tried to set a budget below Meta's minimum (roughly 20 EGP for Egypt) Set the budget to at least 2000 cents (20 EGP) or pause the ad set entirely instead of reducing.
"Cannot update ad set in DELETED status" 100 The ad set was already deleted, not just paused Deleted ad sets cannot be reactivated. Create a new one using SOP 2.
Performance dropped after budget increase -- Budget increase was too aggressive, triggering learning phase reset Revert to the previous budget. Wait 48 hours for stabilization. Then increase by only 20%.
"The ad set is in the learning phase" -- Not enough conversions per week (Meta needs ~50) This is informational, not an error. Either increase budget or broaden audience to generate more conversions. Do not make changes during learning phase -- each change restarts it.
Duplicated ad set has no ads -- duplicateAdSet only creates the ad set, not the ads inside it After duplicating, create a new ad inside the new ad set linking to the same creative ID from the winning ad set.
ROAS fluctuates wildly day-to-day -- Small sample size or Meta's attribution window shifting Do not react to single-day swings. Look at 3-day and 7-day trends. The longer your lookback, the more stable the metrics.

Verification

  1. Confirm budget changes. After scaling, query the ad set to verify the new budget:

Open Claude Code and say:

"Show me the current budget and status of ad set [ad set ID] to confirm my changes took effect."

  1. Check Ads Manager. Open the campaign in Ads Manager and confirm the budget and status match what you set via the API.

  2. Review the scaling log. Open data/active/scaling-log.json and confirm your decision was logged with the correct date, action, and reason.

  3. Monitor for 48 hours. After any scaling change, check performance 48 hours later. If ROAS held steady or improved, the scaling was successful. If ROAS dropped significantly, revert and try a different approach.

Skill Downloads

Two skill packs turn these five SOPs into automated commands you can run in Claude Code.

⚡SKILL DOWNLOAD
Media Buying SOP Pack
What it does: Five commands that execute the media buying SOPs automatically with complete Meta API v25.0 code.
Tell Claude Code: "Install the media-buying-sop-pack skill from https://raw.githubusercontent.com/mazinzaki911/agentic-ecom-playbook/main/skills/media-buying-sop-pack.SKILL.md"
Type /create-campaign in Claude Code
  • Create campaigns, ad sets, and ads via single commands
  • Pull performance insights with automated metric calculations
  • Scale winners and pause underperformers with data-driven logic
↓ Download Skill

Here is what each command does:

Command SOP What It Does
/create-campaign SOP 1 Asks you for campaign name, objective, and budget. Creates the campaign via Meta API.
/setup-adset SOP 2 Asks for target audience, demographics, and placements. Creates the ad set linked to a campaign.
/create-ad SOP 3 Uploads image or video, creates the creative with your copy, links it to an ad set.
/check-performance SOP 4 Pulls insights for all active campaigns, calculates metrics, displays alerts.
/scale-campaign SOP 5 Analyzes ad set performance, identifies winners and losers, recommends actions, and optionally applies them.
⚡SKILL DOWNLOAD
Meta Strategy Builder
What it does: Four strategic modes — testing plans, scaling roadmaps, full-funnel structures, and live campaign audits with prioritized fixes.
Tell Claude Code: "Install the meta-strategy-builder skill from https://raw.githubusercontent.com/mazinzaki911/agentic-ecom-playbook/main/skills/meta-strategy-builder.SKILL.md"
Type /meta-strategy test in Claude Code
  • Generates structured A/B testing plans with success criteria
  • Builds complete four-stage funnels (Awareness → Retargeting)
  • Audits live campaigns and generates prioritized fix lists
↓ Download Skill

Here is what each mode does:

Command Mode What It Does
/meta-strategy test Testing Generates a structured A/B testing plan: audiences to test, creatives to compare, budgets to allocate, and success criteria to measure.
/meta-strategy scale Scaling Takes a winning campaign and generates a step-by-step scaling roadmap with daily budget targets, audience expansion ideas, and risk checkpoints.
/meta-strategy funnel Funnel Builds a complete four-stage funnel: Awareness (broad reach), Consideration (engaged visitors), Conversion (purchase-ready), and Retargeting (past visitors and cart abandoners). Generates campaign structure, ad sets, and creative templates for each stage.
/meta-strategy audit Audit Pulls live data from all your active campaigns, compares metrics against benchmarks, and generates a prioritized list of fixes -- from quick wins to structural changes.

What Is Next

KEY TAKEAWAY
Five SOPs cover the entire media buying lifecycle: create → target → build → monitor → scale. Together, they replace hours of manual clicking with single commands.
REAL TALK
You don't need to memorize every SOP parameter. That's what the skills are for — they prompt you for what's needed and fill in the rest. Just understand the flow.
ACHIEVEMENT UNLOCKED
Five Meta advertising SOPs installed and ready. You can create campaigns, build ads, check performance, and scale winners — all through Claude Code commands.

Five media buying SOPs -- done. You can now create a campaign, target an audience, build the creative, monitor performance, and scale winners -- all through documented, repeatable procedures.

In Section 10: Shopify Store Operations SOPs, you get five more SOPs for the other half of your business: product management, order monitoring, inventory tracking, pricing updates, and collection management. Same format, same level of detail, same working code. By the end of that section, you will have a complete library of ten SOPs covering every major task across both platforms.

[End of Section 9]

Section 10: Shopify Job SOPs

Part 4 -- Operations | Estimated reading time: 35 minutes
IN THIS SECTION YOU WILL
  • Get five complete Shopify store SOPs with ready-to-use Claude Code prompts
  • Learn product management, order tracking, inventory monitoring, pricing, and collections
  • Install the Shopify Jobs SOP Pack, Collections Mapper, and Job Runner skills
  • Chain daily and weekly operations into automated job sequences

This section gives you five ready-made SOPs for your most important Shopify store operations. Same format as Section 9. Same template from Section 8. Every SOP includes exact Claude Code prompts you can use right away -- just open Claude Code, say the prompt, and it handles the rest. At the end, three downloadable skills that automate all of it.

Think of Section 9 as the media buying half of your recipe book. This section is the store operations half. Together, they give you ten SOPs covering every major task across both platforms -- from creating ad campaigns to managing products, orders, inventory, pricing, and collections.

Here is what you are getting:

  1. Product Management -- list, filter, and bulk-update products
  2. Order Operations -- fetch orders, calculate revenue, identify top sellers
  3. Inventory Management -- check stock levels, detect low-stock, update quantities
  4. Pricing & Discounts -- set compare-at prices, apply discounts, create discount codes
  5. Collection Management -- create smart and manual collections, add products, sort, publish

Each SOP is self-contained. You can use any one independently. But together, they cover the daily, weekly, and monthly tasks that keep a Shopify store running.

One important note before we start: all Shopify SOPs in this section use the GraphQL Admin API (version 2025-01) and the client credentials grant authentication from Section 3. If you have not set up your Shopify connection yet, go back to Section 3 first. Everything here depends on it.

Let us get into it.

SOP 1: Product Management & Bulk Updates

# Product Management & Bulk Updates SOP

Status:       Verified
API Version:  2025-01 (Shopify GraphQL Admin API)
Last Updated: 2026-03-03
Platform:     Shopify

Quick Reference

Field Value
API Shopify GraphQL Admin API
Version 2025-01
Auth Client Credentials Grant (Section 3)
Rate Limit 2,000 cost points per second (GraphQL)
Base URL https://{shop}.myshopify.com/admin/api/2025-01/graphql.json

One-sentence summary: List, filter, and bulk-update products in your Shopify store using GraphQL queries and mutations.

Prerequisites

☐ .env file configured with SHOPIFY_SHOP, SHOPIFY_CLIENT_ID, SHOPIFY_CLIENT_SECRET
☐ Shopify connection working (Section 3)
☐ Node.js 18+ installed
☐ dotenv package installed (npm install dotenv)

Step 1: Authenticate and Set Up the Client

What: Get a fresh access token from Shopify using the client credentials grant.

Why: Every API call to Shopify requires an access token. The client credentials grant gives you a token that lasts 24 hours. Claude Code requests it automatically at the start of every operation. This is the same authentication pattern from Section 3 -- every Shopify SOP in this section starts with this step.

Open Claude Code and say:

"Test my Shopify connection by authenticating with the client credentials from my .env file and confirming the store is accessible."

Claude Code will read your .env file, request an access token from Shopify using the client credentials grant, and confirm the connection is working. This is the foundation that every subsequent step depends on -- Claude Code handles authentication automatically for all Shopify operations.

Tip: Claude Code manages token refreshes behind the scenes. You never need to worry about token expiration or caching.

Step 2: List Products with Filters

What: Query your Shopify store for products, optionally filtering by status, type, tag, or any other product attribute.

Why: Before you can update products, you need to find them. Shopify's GraphQL API supports a powerful query parameter that lets you filter products without fetching your entire catalog. This is critical when you have thousands of products -- you do not want to pull 4,750 products just to find the 30 winter boots.

The query parameter uses Shopify's search syntax. Here are the filters you will use most often:

Filter Syntax Example
By status status:ACTIVE Only products visible on your storefront
By status status:DRAFT Products not yet published
By product type product_type:Boots All products with type "Boots"
By tag tag:winter All products tagged "winter"
By vendor vendor:YourBrand All products from vendor "YourBrand"
By title title:Chelsea Products with "Chelsea" in the title
Combined status:ACTIVE AND tag:winter Active products tagged "winter"

You can combine filters with AND and OR. The AND is implicit -- status:ACTIVE tag:winter is the same as status:ACTIVE AND tag:winter.

Open Claude Code and say any of the following:

"Show me all my active products in Shopify."

"List all winter boots in my store."

"Show me all draft products that haven't been published yet."

"List all products tagged winter that are type Boots."

Claude Code will query your Shopify store using the GraphQL Admin API, filter by whatever criteria you specify, and display a formatted table with each product's title, type, status, tags, and stock level. If there are more products than fit in one page, it will automatically fetch additional pages.

Expected output:

Found 12 products matching "product_type:Boots AND tag:winter":

────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Title                              Type           Status    Tags                     Stock
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Classic Chelsea Boot - Black       Boots          ACTIVE    winter, men, leather     24
Classic Chelsea Boot - Brown       Boots          ACTIVE    winter, men, leather     18
Women's Ankle Boot - Tan           Boots          ACTIVE    winter, women, suede     7
Montana Hiking Boot                Boots          ACTIVE    winter, men, outdoor     31
...
────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

Step 3: Bulk Tag Updates

What: Add or remove tags from multiple products at once.

Why: Tags are how Shopify organizes products behind the scenes. They power smart collections ("show all products tagged winter"), filter analytics ("how are my sale items performing?"), and drive marketing logic ("tag products as BOGO to include them in buy-one-get-one campaigns"). Being able to add or remove tags in bulk saves hours of clicking through the Shopify admin.

Shopify updates one product at a time behind the scenes, but Claude Code processes them in a batch for you. Shopify's rate limit is generous -- 2,000 cost points per second -- so Claude Code can update dozens of products in quick succession without hitting any limits.

Open Claude Code and say any of the following:

"Add the tag 'spring-2026' to all my active winter boots."

"Remove the 'winter' tag from all products tagged winter and add the 'clearance' tag instead."

"Tag all products of type Boots with 'end-of-season'."

Claude Code will find the matching products, read their current tags, add or remove the tags you specified, and update each product one by one. It will show you a summary of how many products were updated successfully and how many (if any) failed.

Step 4: Variant Management

What: Update individual product variants -- their prices, SKUs, inventory tracking settings, or option values.

Why: In Shopify, the variant is the thing customers actually buy. A product called "Classic Chelsea Boot" might have six variants: sizes 39, 40, 41, 42, 43, 44. Each variant has its own price, SKU, and inventory count. When you need to change a price or update a SKU, you are updating a variant, not the product itself.

Open Claude Code and say:

"Update the price of the Classic Chelsea Boot size 42 variant to 550 EGP and set its SKU to ACH-CHEL-BLK-42."

Claude Code will find the variant, update its price and SKU via the Shopify GraphQL API, and confirm the changes with the new values.

Troubleshooting

Problem Cause Fix
Access denied or 401 response Access token expired or invalid Ask Claude Code to re-authenticate. Client credentials tokens last 24 hours -- Claude Code handles refreshes automatically.
Product not found Product ID format is wrong Claude Code handles ID formatting automatically. If you reference a product by name and it cannot be found, double-check the spelling.
Empty results when filtering Filter syntax error or no matching products Double-check the filter. Tags and product types are case-sensitive -- "winter" and "Winter" are different in Shopify.
Throttled response (429) Too many API calls Shopify gives you 2,000 cost points per second. Claude Code handles rate limiting automatically, but if you see throttling errors, ask Claude Code to slow down the requests.
Tags not updating Tags format issue Claude Code handles tag formatting automatically. If tags are not updating, double-check that the tags you specified are spelled correctly.
userErrors on update Missing required field or validation failure Claude Code will show you the specific error message. Common cause: setting a price to a negative number or leaving a required field empty.

Verification

  1. Check Claude Code's output. Each operation shows a summary of what it did -- products found, tags updated, variants changed.

  2. Open Shopify Admin. Go to Products in your Shopify admin panel. Search for the product you updated. Confirm that tags, prices, and other fields match what Claude Code set.

  3. Re-run the list query. After a bulk update, ask Claude Code to list the same products again. The updated tags should now appear in the output.

SOP 2: Order Operations & Reporting

# Order Operations & Reporting SOP

Status:       Verified
API Version:  2025-01 (Shopify GraphQL Admin API)
Last Updated: 2026-03-03
Platform:     Shopify

Quick Reference

Field Value
API Shopify GraphQL Admin API
Version 2025-01
Auth Client Credentials Grant (Section 3)
Rate Limit 2,000 cost points per second (GraphQL)
Timezone Africa/Cairo (UTC+2)

One-sentence summary: Fetch orders by date range and status, calculate revenue totals, identify top-selling products, and generate daily summaries.

Prerequisites

☐ .env file configured with SHOPIFY_SHOP, SHOPIFY_CLIENT_ID, SHOPIFY_CLIENT_SECRET
☐ Shopify connection working (Section 3)
☐ Node.js 18+ installed
☐ dotenv package installed
☐ Store has at least some orders to query (new stores with zero orders will return empty results)

Step 1: Fetch Orders by Date Range

What: Query Shopify for all orders within a specific date range, including their line items (the individual products in each order).

Why: This is the foundation of all order reporting. Whether you want today's revenue, last week's top sellers, or a monthly breakdown, it starts with fetching the right orders. The date filter is the most important part -- get the timezone wrong and you will either miss orders from early morning or include yesterday's orders in today's report.

Egypt uses the Africa/Cairo timezone, which is UTC+2. The timestamps in the query must include the +02:00 offset to match your store's timezone. If you are in a different timezone, adjust accordingly.

Open Claude Code and say any of the following:

"Show me all orders from today."

"Fetch all orders from February 24 to March 2, 2026."

"Show me only the unfulfilled orders from this week."

Claude Code will connect to your Shopify store, fetch all orders matching your date range and status filters, and display them. If there are more than 50 orders, Claude Code automatically paginates through all pages to collect every order. It uses the Africa/Cairo timezone (UTC+2) to ensure the date boundaries match your store.

Pagination explained: Shopify limits each response to 50 orders. Claude Code automatically fetches additional pages until all orders are collected. You do not need to worry about this -- it handles pagination behind the scenes.

Step 2: Filter by Fulfillment Status

What: Narrow down your orders by whether they have been shipped, partially shipped, or not shipped at all.

Why: Different fulfillment statuses serve different operational needs. Unfulfilled orders are your picking queue -- what needs to go out today. Fulfilled orders are completed sales. Partially fulfilled orders might have backordered items. Knowing which is which lets you run the right report at the right time.

Status Shopify Value What It Means
Unfulfilled unfulfilled Order placed, nothing shipped yet
Partially fulfilled partial Some items shipped, some remaining
Fulfilled fulfilled Everything shipped
Any null (omit the filter) All orders regardless of fulfillment

Filtering by fulfillment status is simple -- just tell Claude Code which status you want:

Open Claude Code and say:

"Show me today's unfulfilled orders -- that's my shipping queue for today."

"Show me all fulfilled orders from March 1 to March 3 for revenue accounting."

Claude Code will fetch orders filtered by the fulfillment status you specify and display them in a clear list.

Step 3: Calculate Revenue Totals

What: Sum up the order totals to get your revenue, average order value, and discount totals for any date range.

Why: These are the numbers you check every day. Total revenue tells you how the business is doing. Average order value tells you how much each customer spends. Discount total tells you how much you gave away in promotions. Together, they paint a complete financial picture.

Open Claude Code and say:

"Calculate my total revenue, average order value, and total discounts given for today's orders."

Claude Code will fetch the orders, sum up total revenue (including taxes and shipping), product subtotals, and discount amounts, then show you the order count and average order value.

Step 4: Identify Top Sellers

What: Aggregate all line items across orders to find which products sold the most units and generated the most revenue.

Why: Your top sellers are the products that deserve more ad budget, more inventory, and more attention. Your bottom sellers might need a discount, a creative refresh, or to be removed from campaigns entirely. This step gives you a ranked list so you can make those decisions with data.

Open Claude Code and say:

"What are my top 10 best-selling products this week by revenue and by units sold?"

Claude Code will look at all the line items across your orders, aggregate them by product, and rank them two ways: by total revenue generated and by total units sold. This tells you which products deserve more ad budget and inventory.

Step 5: Generate Daily Summary

What: Combine everything into a single, clean daily report that you can read at a glance.

Why: This is the finished dish -- the thing you actually consume every morning. The previous steps were preparation. This step assembles the numbers into a formatted summary that tells you exactly how the business did today.

Open Claude Code and say:

"Give me a complete daily order summary for today -- revenue, fulfillment breakdown, and top 5 sellers."

Or for a specific date:

"Give me the daily order summary for March 1, 2026."

Claude Code will fetch all orders for that date, calculate revenue totals (total revenue, subtotals, discounts, average order value), break down fulfillment status (unfulfilled, fulfilled, partially fulfilled), and rank the top 5 products by both revenue and units sold.

Expected output:

============================================================
  DAILY ORDER SUMMARY -- 2026-03-03
============================================================

  REVENUE
  ────────────────────────────────────
  Orders:          42
  Total Revenue:   18,540 EGP
  Subtotal:        19,200 EGP
  Discounts Given: 660 EGP
  Avg Order Value: 441.43 EGP

  FULFILLMENT
  ────────────────────────────────────
  Unfulfilled:     15
  Fulfilled:       24
  Partial:         3

  TOP 5 BY REVENUE
  ────────────────────────────────────
  1. Classic Chelsea Boot - Black      3,450 EGP (6 units)
  2. Montana Hiking Boot               2,800 EGP (4 units)
  3. Women's Ankle Boot - Tan          2,100 EGP (3 units)
  4. Leather Belt - Classic Brown      1,890 EGP (7 units)
  5. Premium Loafer - Navy             1,650 EGP (3 units)

  TOP 5 BY UNITS SOLD
  ────────────────────────────────────
  1. Leather Belt - Classic Brown      7 units (1,890 EGP)
  2. Classic Chelsea Boot - Black      6 units (3,450 EGP)
  3. Montana Hiking Boot               4 units (2,800 EGP)
  4. Women's Ankle Boot - Tan          3 units (2,100 EGP)
  5. Premium Loafer - Navy             3 units (1,650 EGP)

============================================================

Troubleshooting

Problem Cause Fix
Zero orders returned for today Timezone mismatch -- the query uses a different timezone than your store Egypt is UTC+2. Make sure timestamps use +02:00. If your store is set to a different timezone, adjust the offset.
Orders from yesterday showing up Timezone offset is wrong (too far ahead) Check your store's timezone in Shopify Admin > Settings > General. The +02:00 offset must match.
Access denied or 401 Token expired or invalid credentials Re-check SHOPIFY_CLIENT_ID and SHOPIFY_CLIENT_SECRET in your .env file. Regenerate if needed.
Revenue numbers do not match Shopify dashboard Dashboard may include refunds, shipping, or taxes differently The totalPriceSet includes taxes and shipping. The subtotalPriceSet is product totals only. Compare like-for-like.
Pagination stops early Network error during cursor-based pagination Ask Claude Code to try again. If it consistently fails at the same point, ask it to use a smaller page size.
Line items show null for variant Variant was deleted after the order was placed This is normal for old orders. The product data may no longer exist. Claude Code handles this gracefully.
Throttled response Exceeded 2,000 cost points per second Claude Code automatically handles rate limiting. If you see throttling errors, ask Claude Code to add a small delay between requests.

Verification

  1. Compare against Shopify Analytics. Open Shopify Admin > Analytics > Reports > Sales by day. The total for today should be close to what Claude Code reports. Small differences are normal due to timing and refund handling.

  2. Spot-check individual orders. Pick an order from Claude Code's output (e.g., order #1042). Open it in Shopify Admin. Confirm the total and line items match.

  3. Run for a known date. If you know exactly how many orders you had yesterday, ask Claude Code to pull yesterday's date and verify the count matches.

SOP 3: Inventory Management & Alerts

# Inventory Management & Alerts SOP

Status:       Verified
API Version:  2025-01 (Shopify GraphQL Admin API)
Last Updated: 2026-03-03
Platform:     Shopify

Quick Reference

Field Value
API Shopify GraphQL Admin API
Version 2025-01
Auth Client Credentials Grant (Section 3)
Rate Limit 2,000 cost points per second (GraphQL)
Default Low-Stock Threshold 5 units

One-sentence summary: Check stock levels across all products, detect low-stock and out-of-stock items, generate inventory reports, and update quantities when needed.

Prerequisites

☐ .env file configured with SHOPIFY_SHOP, SHOPIFY_CLIENT_ID, SHOPIFY_CLIENT_SECRET
☐ Shopify connection working (Section 3)
☐ Node.js 18+ installed
☐ dotenv package installed
☐ Inventory tracking enabled for your products in Shopify (Settings > Shipping and delivery > Locations)

Step 1: Fetch Inventory Levels via Product Variants

What: Pull inventory levels for all products by querying their variants. Each variant has an inventoryQuantity that tells you how many units are in stock.

Why: Shopify tracks inventory at the variant level, not the product level. A product called "Classic Chelsea Boot" might have 30 units total, but that could mean size 42 has 15 units while size 39 has zero. Variant-level inventory is the only way to get an accurate picture of what you can actually sell.

Open Claude Code and say:

"Fetch inventory levels for all my active products, broken down by variant."

Or to check a specific product type:

"Show me inventory levels for all active Boots."

Claude Code will query every active product in your Shopify store, pull the inventory quantity for each variant (including SKU, price, and whether tracking is enabled), and compile a complete inventory list. It automatically paginates through all products, even if you have thousands.

Step 2: Low-Stock Detection

What: Scan all variants and flag any with inventory below a configurable threshold.

Why: Running out of stock on a best-seller is one of the most expensive mistakes in e-commerce. Not only do you lose the sale, but if you are running ads to that product, you are paying for clicks that cannot convert. Low-stock detection gives you an early warning so you can reorder, pause ads, or shift marketing to products you actually have in stock.

The default threshold is 5 units, but you can set it to whatever makes sense for your business. If a product sells 10 units per day, a threshold of 5 means less than half a day of stock. If it sells 1 unit per week, 5 units is five weeks of runway.

Open Claude Code and say:

"Show me all variants that are low on stock -- anything with 5 or fewer units left."

Or with a custom threshold:

"Flag any variants with less than 10 units in stock."

Claude Code will scan all tracked variants and display a sorted table of low-stock items, showing the product name, variant, SKU, current stock level, and price. The most critically low items appear first.

Step 3: Out-of-Stock Alerts

What: Identify all variants that have zero inventory -- meaning they cannot be purchased.

Why: Out-of-stock items are invisible lost sales. Customers land on the product page, see "Out of Stock," and leave. If the product is in an active ad campaign, every click to that page is wasted ad spend. This step flags those products so you can take immediate action: reorder, remove from ads, or mark as draft.

Open Claude Code and say:

"Show me all variants that are completely out of stock."

Claude Code will identify every tracked variant with zero inventory and list them with product name, variant, and SKU. These are the items that cannot be purchased and may be wasting ad spend if they are in active campaigns.

Step 4: Generate Inventory Report

What: Combine the data from the previous steps into a complete inventory report with totals, breakdowns, and alerts.

Why: A raw list of variants is hard to act on. An inventory report gives you the big picture: total units in stock, total value, how many products are low versus healthy, and which product types are most at risk. This is the report you share with your purchasing team or review weekly.

Open Claude Code and say:

"Generate a full inventory report for all active products with a low-stock threshold of 5 units."

Or more specific:

"Give me an inventory report for just Boots, and flag anything under 10 units."

Claude Code will produce a complete report including: total variants, total units in stock, total inventory value in EGP, a health breakdown (healthy vs. low-stock vs. out-of-stock), a breakdown by product type showing variants/units/value for each, and detailed lists of every low-stock and out-of-stock variant.

Expected output:

============================================================
  INVENTORY REPORT -- 2026-03-03
============================================================

  SUMMARY
  ────────────────────────────────────
  Total Variants:    842
  Total Units:       12,450
  Total Value:       6,225,000 EGP
  Healthy (>5):      720
  Low Stock (1-5):   68
  Out of Stock (0):  54

  BY PRODUCT TYPE
  ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
  Type                Variants    Units     Value (EGP)
  ──────────────────────────────────────────────────
  Boots               340         5,200     3,120,000
  Sneakers            220         3,800     1,520,000
  Belts               150         2,400       960,000
  Loafers             132         1,050       625,000

!!! LOW STOCK ALERT -- 68 variants below 5 units !!!
...

XXX OUT OF STOCK -- 54 variants with zero inventory XXX
...

============================================================

Step 5: Update Inventory Quantities

What: Adjust the inventory quantity for a specific variant at a specific location.

Why: Sometimes you receive new stock and need to add it to the system. Sometimes you find a discrepancy between the physical count and Shopify's number and need to correct it. Claude Code can increase or decrease inventory by a specific amount.

Behind the scenes, Shopify needs three pieces of information to adjust inventory:

  1. Inventory Item ID -- the internal ID for this variant's inventory record (Claude Code finds this automatically)
  2. Location ID -- which warehouse or store location to adjust (Claude Code looks this up for you)
  3. Delta -- how many units to add (positive number) or remove (negative number)

To find your location ID, you run a one-time query:

Open Claude Code and say:

"Show me all my Shopify store locations and their IDs."

Claude Code will list every warehouse and store location configured in your Shopify account, showing which are active and which are inactive. You need a location ID for inventory adjustments (the next step).

Then use the location in the adjustment:

Open Claude Code and say:

"Add 20 units to the Classic Chelsea Boot size 42 at the main store location -- we just received new stock."

"Remove 3 units from the Montana Hiking Boot size 43 -- they were damaged."

"Set the inventory of the Women's Ankle Boot size 39 to exactly 25 units at the main store."

Claude Code will find the correct inventory item and location, apply the adjustment (adding or removing the number of units you specify), and confirm the new quantity. For exact quantity changes, it will calculate the difference between the current stock and your target number automatically.

Important: The delta is a relative change, not an absolute value. If a variant has 10 units and you pass delta: 5, it becomes 15. If you pass delta: -3, it becomes 7. To set an exact quantity, first check the current quantity, calculate the difference, and use that as the delta.

Troubleshooting

Problem Cause Fix
inventoryQuantity is always 0 Inventory tracking is not enabled for the product In Shopify Admin, edit the product, scroll to the variant, and check "Track quantity." Or enable it via the API.
Inventory item not found Wrong inventory item ID Ask Claude Code to look up the correct inventory item ID for the variant. Variant IDs and inventory item IDs are different.
Location not found Wrong location ID or location is inactive Ask Claude Code to show your active store locations. Use the ID from an active location.
Cannot adjust untracked inventory Trying to adjust a variant that does not have inventory tracking Enable tracking first, then adjust. You cannot adjust quantities on untracked items.
Quantities seem wrong after adjustment Adjusted the wrong location in a multi-location store If you have multiple locations, make sure you are adjusting the correct one. Each location has its own stock count.
Access denied on inventory mutation Token lacks inventory write permissions Client credentials should include all admin scopes. If using a custom app, ensure write_inventory scope is granted.
Large inventory fetch is slow Fetching thousands of variants with pagination This is normal. A store with 4,750 products might have 20,000+ variants. Claude Code paginates automatically. For faster results, ask it to check only a specific product type (e.g., "Check inventory for Boots only").

Verification

  1. Check Shopify Admin. After adjusting inventory, open the product in Shopify Admin. Click on the variant and verify the inventory quantity matches the expected new value.

  2. Re-run the inventory report. Ask Claude Code to generate the inventory report again. The adjusted variant should no longer appear in the low-stock or out-of-stock alerts (if you added enough units).

  3. Check the inventory history. In Shopify Admin, go to the variant's inventory page. You should see a "Manual adjustment" entry with the exact delta you applied.

SOP 4: Pricing & Discount Operations

# Pricing & Discount Operations SOP

Status:       Verified
API Version:  2025-01 (Shopify GraphQL Admin API)
Last Updated: 2026-03-03
Platform:     Shopify

Quick Reference

Field Value
API Shopify GraphQL Admin API
Version 2025-01
Auth Client Credentials Grant (Section 3)
Rate Limit 2,000 cost points per second (GraphQL)
Currency EGP (Egyptian Pound)

One-sentence summary: Set compare-at prices, apply percentage discounts, create discount codes, round prices, and revert prices to their original values.

Prerequisites

☐ .env file configured with SHOPIFY_SHOP, SHOPIFY_CLIENT_ID, SHOPIFY_CLIENT_SECRET
☐ Shopify connection working (Section 3)
☐ Node.js 18+ installed
☐ dotenv package installed
☐ You know which products you want to update (have product IDs or a filter ready)

Step 1: Set Compare-At Price

What: Set the "compare at" price on a variant. This is the "was $X" in the "was $X, now $Y" price display that customers see on your storefront.

Why: The compare-at price is Shopify's built-in way to show a discount. When a variant has both a price and a compareAtPrice, the storefront displays the original price crossed out and the current price highlighted -- the classic sale pattern. This is far better than just lowering the price, because customers can see exactly how much they are saving.

Think of it like a price tag at a clothing store. The original price is printed on the tag. When items go on sale, the store does not print a new tag -- they add a red sticker with the sale price. The customer sees both numbers and immediately understands the deal. The compare-at price is the printed tag; the current price is the red sticker.

Open Claude Code and say:

"Set the compare-at price to 700 EGP on the Classic Chelsea Boot size 42 variant. The current price is 490 EGP, so customers will see 'was 700, now 490'."

Claude Code will update the variant's compare-at price via the Shopify GraphQL API and confirm what the customer will see on the storefront.

Critical rule: The compare-at price must ALWAYS be higher than the current price. If you try to set a compare-at price that is lower than or equal to the current price, Shopify will reject the mutation with a validation error. This is the single most common pricing error.

Step 2: Apply Percentage Discount

What: Apply a percentage discount to product variants. Move the current price to compare-at, then set the new price based on the discount percentage.

Why: This is the most common pricing operation during sales. "30% off all winter boots" means: take each variant's current price, move it to the compare-at slot (so customers see what it used to cost), and set the new price to 70% of the original. This two-step process ensures the discount is visible on the storefront.

Open Claude Code and say any of the following:

"Apply a 30% discount to all active products tagged SALEJAN. Move the current price to compare-at and set the new discounted price."

"Apply 25% off to all active winter boots."

"Apply 50% off to all products tagged end-of-season."

Claude Code will find all matching products, move each variant's original price into the compare-at slot (so customers see what it used to cost), calculate the discounted price, and update every variant. It handles products that are already discounted by using the compare-at price as the base. It will show you a progress log and a final summary of how many variants were updated.

Step 3: Create Discount Codes

What: Create a discount code that customers can enter at checkout to get a percentage or fixed-amount discount.

Why: Discount codes are different from compare-at pricing. Compare-at prices show a discount on the product page -- every customer sees it. Discount codes are private -- only customers who have the code get the deal. This makes them perfect for VIP customers, email campaigns, influencer partnerships, and limited-time promotions.

Shopify's GraphQL API uses the discountCodeBasicCreate mutation for simple percentage or fixed-amount discounts.

Open Claude Code and say any of the following:

"Create a discount code called FLASH15 that gives 15% off. It should expire on March 5, 2026, be limited to 100 uses, and only work once per customer."

"Create a discount code VIP200 that gives 200 EGP off orders over 1,000 EGP. One use per customer, no expiration."

"Create an influencer discount code SARAH10 that gives 10% off with unlimited uses and no restrictions."

Claude Code will create the discount code in your Shopify store with all the settings you specified -- discount type (percentage or fixed amount), expiration date, usage limits, minimum order requirements, and per-customer restrictions. It will confirm the code was created and show you all the details.

Step 4: Round Prices to Nearest 10

What: Round all variant prices to the nearest 10 EGP for a cleaner storefront display.

Why: After applying a percentage discount, you often end up with messy prices like 472.50 EGP or 688.75 EGP. These look unpolished. Rounding to the nearest 10 (470 or 690) gives your store a more professional appearance. In Egypt's market, customers are used to seeing round prices -- 450, 500, 550 -- and odd amounts can feel unfamiliar.

Open Claude Code and say:

"Round all my sale product prices to the nearest 10 EGP. Only touch products tagged 'sale'."

Claude Code will query all matching products, round each variant's price to the nearest 10 (e.g., 472.50 becomes 470, 688.75 becomes 690), and update them via the Shopify GraphQL API. It will show a summary of how many variants were updated and any price changes.

Step 5: Price Reversion

What: Restore prices to their original values by moving the compare-at price back to the main price and clearing the compare-at field.

Why: When a sale ends, you need to put prices back. If you applied a 30% discount by moving the original price to compare-at and setting the new price, reverting is the reverse: move compare-at back to price and clear compare-at. This is the "remove the red sticker and let the printed price tag show again" operation.

Open Claude Code and say:

"Revert all end-of-season products back to their original prices. Move the compare-at price back into the regular price and clear the compare-at field."

Claude Code will find all products matching your filter, check each variant for a compare-at price (which holds the original price during a sale), restore it as the regular price, and clear the compare-at field. It will show you how many variants were reverted.

Troubleshooting

Problem Cause Fix
Compare at price can't be lower than the price You set compare-at lower than the current price Compare-at must always be HIGHER than price. If you need to raise the price above compare-at, set the price first, then set compare-at. Or clear compare-at first (null), then set both.
Discount shows as 0% on storefront Price and compare-at price are the same Make sure the new discounted price is actually different from the compare-at. A 0% discount happens when you accidentally set both to the same value.
Discount code says "already exists" A code with the same string already exists Each discount code must be unique. Either delete the old one first or use a different code string (e.g., FLASH15-V2).
Discount code is invalid at checkout Code expired, hit usage limit, or customer already used it Check the discount in Shopify Admin > Discounts. Verify the dates, usage limit, and whether appliesOncePerCustomer is blocking a repeat use.
Prices show extra decimal places Floating point arithmetic Claude Code automatically rounds calculated prices to two decimal places. If you see odd prices, ask Claude Code to round them to the nearest 10 EGP.
Revert does nothing Products never had a compare-at price If prices were lowered directly (without setting compare-at first), there is no "original" to revert to. The compare-at field is your backup. Without it, you need to set prices manually.
Rounding changes compare-at relationship Rounded price now equals or exceeds compare-at After rounding, verify that each price is still lower than its compare-at. If rounding made them equal, either adjust the compare-at upward or accept the small difference.

Verification

  1. Check the storefront. Open your store in a browser. Navigate to a discounted product. You should see the compare-at price crossed out and the current price displayed.

  2. Test the discount code. Add a product to your cart, go to checkout, and enter the discount code. Confirm the discount applies correctly.

  3. Check Shopify Admin. Go to Products, find the updated products, and verify prices match what Claude Code set. Go to Discounts and verify the code exists with the correct settings.

  4. Run a reverse check. After applying a discount, ask Claude Code to list the same products again. The prices should reflect the discounted amounts.

SOP 5: Collection Management

# Collection Management SOP

Status:       Verified
API Version:  2025-01 (Shopify GraphQL Admin API)
Last Updated: 2026-03-03
Platform:     Shopify

Quick Reference

Field Value
API Shopify GraphQL Admin API
Version 2025-01
Auth Client Credentials Grant (Section 3)
Rate Limit 2,000 cost points per second (GraphQL)
Two Types Smart Collections (automatic rules) and Manual Collections (hand-picked products)

One-sentence summary: Create smart and manual collections, add and remove products, set sort order, and publish collections to your sales channels.

Prerequisites

☐ .env file configured with SHOPIFY_SHOP, SHOPIFY_CLIENT_ID, SHOPIFY_CLIENT_SECRET
☐ Shopify connection working (Section 3)
☐ Node.js 18+ installed
☐ dotenv package installed
☐ You know which sales channel IDs to publish to (Online Store, Point of Sale, etc.)

Step 1: Create a Smart Collection

What: Create a collection that automatically includes products based on rules -- like tags, price ranges, or product types.

Why: Smart collections are the backbone of e-commerce organization. Instead of manually adding each product to a "Winter Boots" collection, you set a rule: "include all products where tag equals winter AND product type equals Boots." Shopify then automatically adds matching products, even new ones you add later. It is like setting up a smart playlist in a music app -- you define the criteria once, and the playlist maintains itself.

Shopify calls the rule system a ruleSet. Each rule has a column (what to check), a relation (how to compare), and a condition (what to compare against).

Column What It Checks Example Rule
TAG Product tags tag equals winter
PRODUCT_TYPE Product type field product_type equals Boots
VENDOR Product vendor vendor equals YourBrand
VARIANT_PRICE Variant price price greater_than 500
TITLE Product title title contains Chelsea
VARIANT_COMPARE_AT_PRICE Compare-at price Has a compare-at price set (on sale)
Relation Meaning
EQUALS Exact match
NOT_EQUALS Does not match
GREATER_THAN Numeric comparison
LESS_THAN Numeric comparison
STARTS_WITH String starts with
ENDS_WITH String ends with
CONTAINS String contains
NOT_CONTAINS String does not contain

Open Claude Code and say:

"Create a smart collection called 'Winter Boots' that automatically includes all products tagged 'winter' AND product type equals 'Boots'. Sort by best selling. Use AND logic so products must match ALL rules."

Claude Code will create the smart collection via the Shopify GraphQL API with the rule set you described. It will confirm the collection name, handle, ID, rules, and how many products matched.

AND vs OR: The disjunctive flag controls whether a product must match ALL rules (AND, disjunctive: false) or ANY rule (OR, disjunctive: true). For a "Winter Boots" collection, you want AND -- the product must be tagged winter AND be type Boots. For a "Sale Items" collection, you want OR -- the product can have any one of the sale tags.

Step 2: Create a Manual Collection

What: Create a collection where you hand-pick exactly which products are included.

Why: Not every collection fits neatly into rules. A "Staff Picks" collection, a "Gift Guide," or a "Homepage Featured" section requires human judgment about which products belong. Manual collections give you full control -- nothing gets in without you explicitly adding it.

Open Claude Code and say:

"Create a manual collection called 'Staff Picks - March 2026' with the description 'Our team\'s favorite items this month.' Set it to manual sort order."

Claude Code will create the empty manual collection. You will then add products to it in Step 3.

Step 3: Add and Remove Products

What: Add products to a manual collection or remove them.

Why: Once you have created a manual collection, it is empty. You need to populate it. Claude Code can add products in bulk or remove them. You can add or remove dozens of products in a single command.

Open Claude Code and say:

"Add these three products to my Staff Picks collection: Classic Chelsea Boot - Black, Montana Hiking Boot, and Women's Ankle Boot - Tan."

Or to remove products:

"Remove the Montana Hiking Boot from the Staff Picks collection."

Claude Code will find the product IDs by name, add or remove them from the specified collection, and confirm with a count of how many products are now in the collection.

Smart collections note: You cannot manually add or remove products from a smart collection. The rule set determines membership. If you need to override a smart collection's membership, convert it to a manual collection or adjust the rules.

Step 4: Sort Collection

What: Change the sort order of products within a collection.

Why: The order in which products appear matters. Best-selling items first means customers see your winners immediately. Price low-to-high is great for sale collections where customers are deal-hunting. Manual order lets you control the exact sequence for curated collections like "New Arrivals" or "Staff Picks."

Sort Order API Value What It Does
Best Selling BEST_SELLING Most purchased items first
Alphabetical (A-Z) ALPHA_ASC Sorted by product title
Alphabetical (Z-A) ALPHA_DESC Reverse alphabetical
Price (Low to High) PRICE_ASC Cheapest first
Price (High to Low) PRICE_DESC Most expensive first
Newest First CREATED_DESC Most recently created products first
Oldest First CREATED Oldest products first
Manual MANUAL Custom order you define yourself

Open Claude Code and say:

"Sort my Winter Boots collection by best selling."

Or:

"Sort the Sale Items collection by price, lowest first."

Claude Code will update the collection's sort order via the Shopify GraphQL API and confirm the change.

Step 5: Publish to Sales Channels

What: Make a collection visible on specific sales channels -- your online store, point of sale, or other connected channels.

Why: Creating a collection does not automatically make it visible to customers. By default, new collections are unpublished. You need to explicitly publish them to each sales channel where you want them to appear. Think of it like stocking a shelf: the products exist in your warehouse (Shopify), but they are not on the store floor (your website) until you put them there.

To publish, you need the publication ID for each sales channel. Here is how to find them:

Open Claude Code and say:

"Show me all my Shopify sales channels."

Claude Code will list all your publications (Online Store, Point of Sale, etc.) with their IDs.

Then publish:

"Publish the Winter Boots collection to the Online Store and Point of Sale channels."

Claude Code will publish the collection to the specified sales channels and confirm how many channels it is now available on.

Troubleshooting

Problem Cause Fix
Smart collection has 0 products Rules do not match any products, or rules use wrong values Double-check your rule conditions. TAG is case-sensitive -- winter and Winter are different. Test by searching for the tag in Shopify Admin first.
Cannot add products to a smart collection Smart collections are rule-based, not manual Smart collections are automatic. To include a product, change its tags or type to match the collection's rules. Or ask Claude Code to create a manual collection instead.
Collection not found Wrong collection ID format Shopify GraphQL IDs look like gid://shopify/Collection/450012345678. Do not use just the numeric ID.
Published but not visible on storefront Collection is empty, or theme does not display it A published collection with zero products will not appear in navigation. Also check your theme's navigation settings in Online Store > Navigation.
Sort order reverts to default Updated the wrong field or Shopify reset it Ask Claude Code to sort the collection again. For MANUAL sort, you also need to set the product order.
Publication not found Wrong publication ID or channel not installed Ask Claude Code to show your current sales channels. The IDs change if you reinstall a channel.
Rate limit when creating many collections Too many mutations in quick succession Claude Code handles rate limiting automatically. If you are creating many collections at once, it will space out the requests.

Verification

  1. Check Shopify Admin. Go to Products > Collections. Your new collection should appear in the list. Click on it to verify the title, rules (for smart collections), products (for manual collections), and sort order.

  2. Check the storefront. Open your store in a browser. Navigate to the collection URL: https://your-store.myshopify.com/collections/{handle}. The collection should display with the correct products and sort order.

  3. Verify publication. In Shopify Admin, open the collection and look at the "Sales channels" section on the right sidebar. It should list all the channels you published to.

  4. Test smart collection rules. Add a new product with matching tags. Wait a moment, then refresh the collection in Shopify Admin. The new product should appear automatically.

Skill Downloads

Three skill packs turn these five SOPs into automated commands you can run in Claude Code.

⚡SKILL DOWNLOAD
Shopify Jobs SOP Pack
What it does: Five commands that execute the Shopify job SOPs automatically with complete Shopify GraphQL API 2025-01 code.
Tell Claude Code: "Install the shopify-jobs-sop-pack skill from https://raw.githubusercontent.com/mazinzaki911/agentic-ecom-playbook/main/skills/shopify-jobs-sop-pack.SKILL.md"
Type /list-products in Claude Code
  • List, filter, and bulk-tag products via single commands
  • Check orders, revenue summaries, and inventory alerts
  • Update prices and manage collections in one step
↓ Download Skill

Here is what each command does:

Command SOP What It Does
/list-products SOP 1 Asks for a filter (tags, type, status), lists matching products in a formatted table, and optionally performs bulk tag updates.
/check-orders SOP 2 Asks for a date range and fulfillment status, fetches orders, calculates revenue, and displays a daily summary with top sellers.
/check-inventory SOP 3 Scans all active products, flags low-stock and out-of-stock variants, and generates a complete inventory report by product type.
/update-prices SOP 4 Asks for a filter, discount percentage, and operation type (apply discount, set compare-at, round prices, or revert). Applies the pricing change to all matching variants.
/manage-collections SOP 5 Asks for collection type (smart or manual), rules or product IDs, sort order, and publication channels. Creates and publishes the collection in one step.
⚡SKILL DOWNLOAD
Collections Mapper
What it does: Maps, builds, and audits your Shopify collection hierarchy. Three modes for complete store organization.
Tell Claude Code: "Install the collections-mapper skill from https://raw.githubusercontent.com/mazinzaki911/agentic-ecom-playbook/main/skills/collections-mapper.SKILL.md"
Type /collections-map in Claude Code
  • Maps all existing collections with product counts and rules
  • Builds full category hierarchies from a single command
  • Audits for empty, broken, or duplicate collections
↓ Download Skill

Here is what each mode does:

Command Mode What It Does
/collections-map Map Fetches all existing collections from your store, lists them with their product counts, rules (for smart collections), and publication status. Gives you a complete map of your store's organization.
/collections-build Build Takes a category structure (like "Men > Boots > Winter") and creates the full hierarchy of collections with smart rules, sort orders, and publication settings. Builds an entire store navigation in one command.
/collections-audit Audit Scans all collections for problems: empty collections, collections with broken rules, unpublished collections that should be published, duplicate collections, and collections with low-stock products. Generates a fix-it list.
⚡SKILL DOWNLOAD
Automated Job Runner
What it does: Runs daily, weekly, or custom Shopify job sequences that chain multiple SOPs together into a single command.
Tell Claude Code: "Install the job-runner skill from https://raw.githubusercontent.com/mazinzaki911/agentic-ecom-playbook/main/skills/job-runner.SKILL.md"
Type /shopify-job daily in Claude Code
  • Daily mode: order summary + inventory alerts + pricing checks
  • Weekly mode: full inventory report + revenue comparison + collection audit
  • Custom mode: mix and match any SOPs with custom parameters
↓ Download Skill

Here is what each mode does:

Command Mode What It Does
/shopify-job daily Daily Runs your daily operations checklist: generates today's order summary (SOP 2), checks for low-stock and out-of-stock alerts (SOP 3), and flags any pricing anomalies (SOP 4). One command, three reports.
/shopify-job weekly Weekly Runs your weekly deep-dive: full inventory report by product type (SOP 3), week-over-week revenue comparison (SOP 2), collection health audit (SOP 5), and top/bottom sellers analysis.
/shopify-job custom Custom Run any combination of SOPs with custom parameters. Specify which jobs to run, which filters to apply, and which thresholds to use. Example: /shopify-job custom --inventory --threshold 10 --filter "tag:winter"

What Is Next

Ten SOPs done -- five for media buying, five for Shopify. You can now create campaigns, target audiences, build creatives, monitor performance, scale winners, manage products, track orders, watch inventory, adjust prices, and organize collections. All through documented, repeatable procedures with Claude Code prompts that handle the technical work for you.

KEY TAKEAWAY
Ten SOPs give you complete coverage of both platforms. With five for Meta and five for Shopify, every core operation is documented, coded, and ready to automate.
REAL TALK
You now have more automation coverage than most ecommerce teams with dedicated developers. These SOPs represent weeks of trial-and-error distilled into tested, working procedures.
ACHIEVEMENT UNLOCKED
Ten SOPs done — five for Meta ads, five for Shopify operations. Your complete recipe book covers campaigns, audiences, creatives, performance, scaling, products, orders, inventory, pricing, and collections.

In Section 11: Converting SOPs into Claude Skills, you learn how to take any SOP and convert it into a Claude Skill -- a single slash command that executes the entire procedure automatically. Same steps, same logic, same error handling. Hands-free execution.

REMINDER
Agentic workflows execute multiple steps autonomously. Always run new workflows with --dry-run or against a test environment first. Review every action before enabling fully autonomous mode on production systems.
Part 5: Going Agentic
Sections 11 -- 13

Convert SOPs into one-command Skills, chain them into autonomous workflows, and build a complete media buying strategy with Claude Code.

Section 11: Converting SOPs into Claude Skills

Part 5 -- Going Agentic | Estimated reading time: 20 minutes
IN THIS SECTION YOU WILL
  • Understand what a Claude Skill is and how it differs from an SOP
  • Learn the skill file structure (frontmatter, instructions, arguments)
  • Convert your first SOP into a working skill manually
  • Install the SOP-to-Skill Converter for automated conversion

You now have 10 SOPs. Each one is a document -- a set of instructions that someone (or something) can follow. But right now, to use an SOP, you'd tell Claude Code: "Follow the Campaign Creation SOP and create a campaign with these settings." That works, but there's a better way: converting the SOP into a Claude Skill. A Skill is an SOP that Claude Code can execute with a single command.

Instead of explaining what you want and pointing Claude Code to a document, you type /create-campaign sales 500 and it just does it. Same steps, same logic, same error handling -- but triggered by a single slash command instead of a paragraph of instructions.

11.1 SOP vs Skill -- Document vs Executable

An SOP is a document. It lives in a folder somewhere -- maybe in Media Buying/sops/, maybe in a shared Google Drive, maybe printed on paper. It describes a task step by step, and a person (or Claude Code, when you point it to the file) reads the document and follows the instructions. The SOP does not do anything on its own. It is passive. It waits to be read.

A Skill is a program. Well, not exactly a program in the traditional sense -- you are not writing Python or JavaScript here. A Skill is a structured instruction file that Claude Code recognizes as something it can execute directly. It lives in a specific folder (.claude/skills/), has a specific format (YAML frontmatter plus a body of instructions), and is triggered by a slash command like /create-campaign. The Skill does not wait to be read. It activates when you call it.

Here is the analogy that makes it click. An SOP is like a recipe written on a piece of paper. You read the recipe, gather the ingredients, preheat the oven, and follow each step yourself. The paper does not cook anything. You do the cooking; the paper just tells you how. A Skill is like putting that recipe into a smart oven that follows it automatically. You press a button, tell it what dish you want, and the oven reads the recipe, sets the right temperature, times each step, and alerts you when it is done. Same recipe. Same steps. Hands-free execution.

SOP Skill
Format Markdown document Markdown with YAML frontmatter
How to use Tell Claude Code to follow it Type /command-name
