Lives in Anywhere (docs folder, shared drive) .claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md
Triggered by Describing what you want in words A single slash command
Accepts input You explain parameters in natural language $ARGUMENTS variable captures your input
Can be shared Yes, as a file Yes, same file format
Self-contained Yes Yes

When to keep as SOP: when the task requires human judgment at every step, involves creative decisions (writing ad copy, choosing images), is rarely repeated, or is still in "draft" status -- you have not tested it enough to trust automation.

When to convert to Skill: when the task follows a predictable pattern, the inputs change but the steps stay the same (same recipe, different ingredients), you do the task frequently, and it has a clear success criteria.

Most of the SOPs from Sections 9 and 10 are excellent Skill candidates. Creating a campaign follows the same steps every time -- only the objective, budget, and audience change. Same recipe. Different ingredients. That is the sweet spot for Skills.

11.2 Anatomy of a Skill File

Every Claude Skill file has two parts: the frontmatter (metadata at the top, between --- markers) and the body (instructions that follow).

---
name: command-name
description: What this skill does, in one sentence. Claude uses this to decide when to suggest it.
user-invocable: true
allowed-tools: Bash, Read, Write, Grep, WebFetch
argument-hint: [optional-hint-shown-to-user]
---

# Skill Name

Instructions that Claude Code follows when this skill is triggered.
These are written in natural language, like you're explaining to a smart assistant.

The Frontmatter Fields

The frontmatter uses YAML -- just key-value pairs, one per line. Here is what each field does:

name -- The command name after the slash. If you set name: create-campaign, you type /create-campaign to trigger it. Keep it short, lowercase, and hyphenated. Good names read like verbs: create-campaign, check-revenue, upload-feed.

description -- One sentence Claude reads to understand when this skill is relevant. Claude Code actually reads this to decide whether to suggest the skill when you describe a task. Be specific. Bad: "Does campaign stuff." Good: "Create a Meta ad campaign with objective, budget, and Advantage+ settings via Meta API v25.0."

user-invocable -- Set to true if the user can trigger it directly with a slash command (most of the time). Set to false for helper skills that are only called by other skills -- sub-recipes that a main recipe references internally.

allowed-tools -- Which tools the skill can use. This is a safety mechanism -- you only grant the permissions the skill actually needs.

Tool What It Does Include When...
Bash Run terminal commands, execute scripts The skill runs any script or CLI command
Read Read file contents The skill reads config files or data files
Write Create or overwrite files The skill generates output files
Edit Modify existing files The skill updates parts of existing files
Grep Search file contents by pattern The skill searches across your codebase
Glob Find files by name pattern The skill locates files matching a pattern
WebFetch Fetch content from a web page The skill pulls data from a URL
WebSearch Search the web The skill needs to look something up online

argument-hint -- Shown to the user after the command name. Think of it like placeholder text in a form field. [objective budget-amount] shows as /create-campaign [objective budget-amount].

The Body

Everything below the frontmatter is what Claude Code actually follows. It uses three building blocks:

$ARGUMENTS -- whatever the user typed after the command name. /create-campaign winter boots 500 makes $ARGUMENTS = "winter boots 500". You parse this inside the skill to extract objectives, budgets, names, and other parameters.

Natural language instructions -- write as if explaining to a smart assistant. Be explicit. Bad: "Create the campaign." Good: "Send a POST request to https://graph.facebook.com/v25.0/act_{ad_account_id}/campaigns with the objective, name, status, and special_ad_categories fields."

Decision trees -- handle varying inputs with plain-English logic:

If $ARGUMENTS contains "CBO":
  Configure Campaign Budget Optimization
If $ARGUMENTS contains "ABO":
  Budget will be set at the ad set level instead
If neither is specified:
  Default to CBO and mention this default to the user

11.3 Step-by-Step Conversion -- Campaign Creation SOP to /create-campaign Skill

Let us take the Campaign Creation SOP from Section 9 and convert it into a working Skill.

The Original SOP (Abbreviated)

# SOP: Campaign Creation
**Status:** Verified | **API Version:** v25.0 (Meta)

## Prerequisites
<div class="checklist-item" style="margin-left:0px"><span class="check-icon">&#9744;</span> .env configured with META_ACCESS_TOKEN and AD_ACCOUNT_ID</div>
<div class="checklist-item" style="margin-left:0px"><span class="check-icon">&#9744;</span> Valid (non-expired) Meta access token</div>

## Steps
1. Choose objective (OUTCOME_SALES, OUTCOME_TRAFFIC, etc.)
2. Choose budget strategy (CBO vs ABO)
3. Set Advantage+ controls and special_ad_categories
4. Create campaign via POST /act_{id}/campaigns
5. Verify by querying campaign back via GET

## Troubleshooting
| Error | Cause | Fix |
|-------|-------|-----|
| Code 190 | Token expired | Generate new long-lived token |
| Code 100 | Invalid objective | Check spelling of objective value |
| Code 17 | Rate limit | Wait 5 minutes and retry |

Step 1: Create the File Structure

Open Claude Code and say:

"Install the create-campaign skill by creating the folder and SKILL.md file at .claude/skills/create-campaign/"

The file goes to .claude/skills/create-campaign.SKILL.md. Claude Code scans .claude/skills/ at startup and registers every SKILL.md it finds.

Step 2: Write the Frontmatter

---
name: create-campaign
description: Create a Meta ad campaign with objective, budget, and Advantage+ settings via Meta API v25.0.
user-invocable: true
allowed-tools: Bash, Read, Write
argument-hint: [objective budget-amount]
---

Step 3: Convert SOP Steps to Skill Instructions

The key differences from an SOP:

  1. Remove "What" and "Why" explanations -- Claude Code needs to know what to do, not why.
  2. Add input parsing -- The SOP assumes a human already decided the objective. The Skill extracts it from $ARGUMENTS.
  3. Add smart defaults -- The SOP lists options. The Skill assumes sensible defaults and only asks when ambiguous.
  4. Add confirmation -- Before irreversible actions, show a summary and wait for approval.

Here is the converted skill body:

# Create Meta Ad Campaign

## Parse Input
Read $ARGUMENTS and extract:
- **Objective**: "sales"→OUTCOME_SALES, "traffic"→OUTCOME_TRAFFIC,
  "leads"→OUTCOME_LEADS, "awareness"→OUTCOME_AWARENESS,
  "engagement"→OUTCOME_ENGAGEMENT. If none found, ask the user.
- **Budget**: First number found = daily budget in local currency.
  Meta API expects CENTS: multiply by 100. If none found, ask.
- **Budget Strategy**: "CBO" or "ABO". Default to CBO if unspecified.
- **Campaign Name**: Quoted string in $ARGUMENTS, or generate as
  "[Objective] - [Date] - CBO/ABO"

## Check Prerequisites
1. Read `.env` and verify META_ACCESS_TOKEN and AD_ACCOUNT_ID exist
2. Test token: GET https://graph.facebook.com/v25.0/me?access_token={token}
3. If anything fails, stop and show a clear error with fix instructions

## Show Confirmation
Display summary and wait for user approval:
  Name / Objective / Budget / Bid Strategy / Status: PAUSED
Always create in PAUSED status for review.

## Create the Campaign
POST https://graph.facebook.com/v25.0/act_{AD_ACCOUNT_ID}/campaigns
  - name, objective, status: PAUSED, special_ad_categories: []
  - If CBO: daily_budget in cents
  - bid_strategy: LOWEST_COST_WITHOUT_CAP

## Verify the Result
1. GET the campaign back and confirm all fields match
2. Display campaign ID, settings, and Ads Manager review link

## Error Handling
- Code 190: Tell user to regenerate token
- Code 100: Show rejected parameter and valid values
- Code 17: Wait 60 seconds and retry once
- Any other: Show full error, suggest /handle-error
- After resolving: save the lesson to memory

Step 4: Handle the $ARGUMENTS Variable

This is what makes a Skill flexible. The same skill handles all of these:

/create-campaign sales 500
/create-campaign awareness 1000 ABO
/create-campaign traffic 300 "Spring Collection Launch"
/create-campaign

That last one -- with nothing after it -- is important. A good skill handles missing input gracefully. Instead of crashing, it asks:

What type of campaign do you want to create?
  1. Sales/Conversions  2. Traffic  3. Leads  4. Awareness  5. Engagement

This is the difference between brittle automation and a robust skill that guides you.

Step 5: Add Verification

The Skill automatically queries the campaign back after creating it, compares settings against what was requested, and reports any discrepancies. It also builds the Ads Manager URL so the user can click through and see the campaign in the familiar Meta interface.

This is a pattern for every skill: act, then verify. Never assume the API did what you asked.

11.4 Testing & Iterating

Testing a Skill is like testing a recipe -- cook the dish, taste it, adjust, cook again.

Install it: Place SKILL.md in .claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md and start a new Claude Code conversation (skills load at session start).

Test 1 -- Basic input: /create-campaign sales 500. Should parse inputs, check credentials, show confirmation, create campaign (PAUSED), verify, and show Ads Manager link.

Test 2 -- Minimal input: /create-campaign with nothing else. Should ask for missing info, not crash or guess.

Test 3 -- Full input: /create-campaign sales 500 CBO "Winter Boots Launch". Should use all provided values without asking follow-up questions.

Test 4 -- Error handling: Change META_ACCESS_TOKEN in .env to something invalid. The skill should catch this during prerequisite checks and show a helpful error -- not crash halfway through.

Improving the Skill

  • If a step confuses Claude Code: add more detail. "Look for the first number in $ARGUMENTS and treat it as the daily budget in EGP" beats "parse the input."
  • If an error keeps happening: add it to the Error Handling section. After five rounds, you will cover 90% of real issues.
  • If users always provide the same input: make it a default. If every campaign is OUTCOME_SALES, default to that instead of asking.
  • If a step takes too long: break it into smaller pieces with separate verification.

Every improvement is permanent. The SKILL.md file persists on disk. Tomorrow, next month -- every /create-campaign uses the latest, most refined version.

Tip: After testing a skill, tell Claude Code: "Remember that the create-campaign skill needs to check for Meta connection first." This saves to your memory system (Section 6), so the improvement persists across sessions.

11.5 Converting Your Other SOPs

Here is the quick-reference checklist for converting any SOP:

Step Action
1 Install the skill: Tell Claude Code "Install the [skill-name] skill"
2 Write the frontmatter: name, description, user-invocable, allowed-tools, argument-hint
3 Add input parsing: define how $ARGUMENTS maps to the SOP's parameters
4 Convert SOP steps: rewrite as execution instructions (drop "what" and "why")
5 Add prerequisite checks: make them active checks, not a passive checklist
6 Add confirmation: show summary before irreversible actions
7 Add verification: query results back and confirm they match
8 Add error handling: convert the troubleshooting table to if/then instructions
9 Test: happy path, minimal input, full input, broken credentials
10 Iterate: fix issues, add defaults, refine instructions

Mapping the 10 SOPs to Skills

SOP (from Ch. 9-10) Command Notes
Campaign Creation /create-campaign Converted in this section
DPA Retargeting /create-retargeting Add product set selection logic
Check Performance /check-performance Add date range parsing from $ARGUMENTS
Upload Catalog Feed /upload-feed Add file path detection from $ARGUMENTS
Manage Ad Status /manage-ads Add pause/resume/archive as sub-commands
Check Revenue /check-revenue Simplest conversion -- minimal parsing needed
Update Prices /update-prices Add collection and discount % from $ARGUMENTS
Manage Inventory /manage-inventory Add threshold parameters
Collection Management /manage-collections Add sort-by option from $ARGUMENTS
Product Tagging /tag-products Add tag name and collection from $ARGUMENTS

Start with the ones you use most frequently. The campaign creator and revenue checker are good first candidates. Work through the list over a few days, testing each one before moving to the next.

Skill Download: SOP-to-Skill Converter

⚡SKILL DOWNLOAD
SOP-to-Skill Converter
What it does: Reads any SOP markdown file, extracts the structure, and generates a complete SKILL.md with proper frontmatter and instructions. No manual conversion needed.
Tell Claude Code: "Install the sop-to-skill-converter skill from https://raw.githubusercontent.com/mazinzaki911/agentic-ecom-playbook/main/skills/sop-to-skill-converter.SKILL.md"
Type /sop-to-skill [path-to-sop] in Claude Code
  • Extracts title, prerequisites, steps, and troubleshooting from any SOP
  • Generates properly formatted skill with argument parsing
  • Converts 20 minutes of manual work into a 2-minute automation
↓ Download Skill

When you type /sop-to-skill Media Buying/sops/DPA-RETARGETING.md, the converter:

  1. Reads the SOP file and extracts the structure (title, prerequisites, steps, troubleshooting)
  2. Generates a skill name from the SOP title ("DPA Retargeting" becomes create-retargeting)
  3. Writes the frontmatter with appropriate name, description, tools, and argument hint
  4. Converts each SOP step into an execution instruction
  5. Transforms prerequisites into active checks and troubleshooting into error handling
  6. Adds $ARGUMENTS parsing based on the SOP's parameters
  7. Saves the result to .claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md for your review

What would take 20 minutes of manual conversion takes about 2 minutes with the converter.

What Claude Code will show you:

You:     /sop-to-skill Media Buying/sops/CAMPAIGN-CREATION.md

Claude:  Reading SOP: "Campaign Creation"
         Platform: Meta (v25.0)
         Steps found: 5
         Errors documented: 3

         Generated skill:
           Name: create-campaign
           Command: /create-campaign
           Tools: Bash, Read, Write
           Arguments: [objective budget-amount]

         Saved to: .claude/skills/create-campaign.SKILL.md
         Would you like to test it now?

Section Recap

What Why It Matters
SOP vs Skill distinction Know when to keep a document vs. convert to automation
Skill file anatomy Read, write, and modify any Claude Skill
Frontmatter fields explained Understand what each setting controls
$ARGUMENTS and input parsing Skills handle flexible user input
Full conversion walkthrough Converted a real SOP into a working Skill
Testing methodology (4 test types) Verify skills work before relying on them
Iteration process Skills get better every time you use them
SOP-to-Skill Converter Automate the conversion for future SOPs

The most important takeaway: a Skill is just an SOP that Claude Code can run with a single command. The knowledge is the same. The steps are the same. The format is different -- frontmatter for metadata, $ARGUMENTS for input, execution-oriented instructions instead of explanatory ones -- but the substance is identical. If you can write an SOP, you can write a Skill.

KEY TAKEAWAY
A Skill is just an SOP in a different format. Same knowledge, same steps — just structured for machine execution instead of human reading.
REAL TALK
The SOP-to-Skill Converter handles 80% of the work. For the remaining 20%, you'll want to add edge case handling and test with real data. Perfection comes from iteration, not from getting it right the first time.
ACHIEVEMENT UNLOCKED
You can now convert any SOP into a one-command Claude Skill. Your ten SOPs can become ten skills — each executable with a single slash command.

What Is Next

You can now convert any SOP into a Claude Skill. But individual skills handle individual tasks. What if you want Claude Code to run a chain of tasks -- check inventory, then pause ads for out-of-stock products, then send you a summary? That is not one skill. That is multiple skills working together in sequence, with the output of one feeding into the input of the next.

That is an agentic workflow, and it is what Section 12 is all about. You will learn how to chain skills together, pass data between them, handle failures mid-chain, and build fully autonomous multi-step operations that run from a single command. This is where the system stops being a collection of tools and starts being an intelligent operator.

[End of Section 11]

Section 12: Agentic Workflows

Part 5 -- Going Agentic | Estimated reading time: 20 minutes
IN THIS SECTION YOU WILL
  • Understand the three levels of automation (Manual → Assisted → Agentic)
  • Build three working workflows that chain skills across platforms
  • Learn checkpoint patterns, error recovery, and data passing between steps
  • Install the Workflow Orchestrator skill

Up until now, every task has been one step at a time. You type a command, Claude Code does the thing, you type the next command. That's useful, but it's not agentic. An agentic workflow is when you give Claude Code a high-level goal, and it figures out the sequence of steps to achieve it — calling multiple skills, making decisions along the way, handling errors, and reporting back when it's done.

In Section 11, you learned how to build individual skills — reusable commands like /create-campaign or /check-inventory that each handle one task. This section is about chaining those skills together into workflows that accomplish real business objectives without you holding the wheel at every turn.

By the end of this section, you will have three working workflows: one that launches a product across Shopify and Meta, one that runs your daily operations check, and one that automatically protects your ad spend when inventory runs low.

12.1 What Is an Agentic Workflow?

In plain English, an agentic workflow is a multi-step task that runs on autopilot.

Here is the analogy. Imagine you own a restaurant and you tell your assistant: "Get the restaurant ready for a Friday night rush." A good assistant does not come back and ask you ten separate questions. They know the steps: check the reservations, brief the kitchen staff, verify ingredient stock, set up the tables, update the specials board, and confirm the evening playlist is loaded. They do it all. They make small decisions on the fly — if a table is wobbly, they fix it without asking. If the kitchen is low on salmon, they update the specials to feature something else. They come back when everything is done and tell you: "We're ready. Here's what I did, here's what I changed, and here's one thing you should know about."

That is exactly what an agentic workflow does for your store.

Three Levels of Automation

Not everything needs to be agentic. There are three levels, and understanding them helps you decide how much to automate.

Level What It Looks Like Example
Manual You do every step by hand. Clicking through Ads Manager, editing products one by one in Shopify Admin, copying numbers into spreadsheets. Opening 20 product pages to update prices one at a time.
Assisted You tell Claude Code each step one at a time. You type /create-campaign, wait for it to finish, then type /update-prices, wait, then type /manage-collections. You are still the conductor — deciding what happens next and when. Running individual skills in sequence, checking each result before moving to the next.
Agentic You tell Claude Code the goal, and it chains the steps together automatically. "Launch the new winter boots collection across Shopify and Meta." It figures out the sequence, runs each step, checks for errors, adapts if something goes wrong, and reports back when done. One instruction triggers seven or eight operations across two platforms.

Most of the playbook so far has lived at Level 2 — Assisted. You have powerful skills, but you are driving. This section takes you to Level 3.

What Makes It "Agentic" vs Just "Automated"

This is a distinction worth understanding. A basic automation script does the same thing every time — like a washing machine following its cycle. An agentic workflow is more like a human assistant following a general plan. The difference comes down to four qualities:

Decision-making. An agentic workflow does not just follow a script blindly. It checks conditions and adapts. If you say "launch this product" and the product does not exist in Shopify yet, an automated script crashes. An agentic workflow notices the product is missing and either creates it or asks you what to do.

Error recovery. When a step fails, an agentic workflow tries to fix the problem before escalating to you. If the Meta API returns a rate limit error, it waits and retries. If a collection creation fails because the name already exists, it uses the existing collection instead. Only when the problem is truly beyond its ability — a wrong password, a missing permission — does it stop and ask.

Cross-platform coordination. The most valuable workflows span multiple systems. They update Shopify AND create Meta campaigns in the same flow. They check inventory in one system and pause ads in another. No single tool handles both — the agentic workflow is the glue that connects them.

Self-verification. After completing the work, an agentic workflow checks its own results. It does not just say "I called the API and got a 200 response." It opens your storefront with Playwright and confirms the product page looks right. It queries Meta's API to verify the campaign was created with the correct settings. It proves its own work.

12.2 Workflow 1: Launch a New Product

This is the most powerful workflow in the playbook. It takes a product from zero to live ads in a single command.

Think about what launching a new product actually involves today. You verify the product listing in Shopify. You set the pricing — maybe a launch discount with a compare-at price. You add it to the right collections so it shows up in the right navigation on your site. You create a Meta campaign with the right objective and targeting. You set up the ad creative with product images and copy. You check your storefront to make sure everything looks right. And then you pull together a summary of everything you did.

That is at least an hour of work across two platforms, six browser tabs, and dozens of clicks. With an agentic workflow, it is one command.

The Flow

Here is what happens when you say "Launch the new winter boots collection":

"Launch winter boots collection"
          │
          ▼
┌─────────────────────┐
│ Step 1: SHOPIFY     │
│ Create/verify the   │
│ product listing     │
│ Checkpoint: ✓       │
└─────────┬───────────┘
          ▼
┌─────────────────────┐
│ Step 2: PRICING     │
│ Set compare-at and  │
│ sale price          │
│ Checkpoint: ✓       │
└─────────┬───────────┘
          ▼
┌─────────────────────┐
│ Step 3: COLLECTIONS │
│ Add to relevant     │
│ collections         │
│ Checkpoint: ✓       │
└─────────┬───────────┘
          ▼
┌─────────────────────┐
│ Step 4: META        │
│ Create campaign     │
│ with targeting      │
│ Checkpoint: ✓       │
└─────────┬───────────┘
          ▼
┌─────────────────────┐
│ Step 5: CREATIVE    │
│ Set up ad creative  │
│ with product images │
│ Checkpoint: ✓       │
└─────────┬───────────┘
          ▼
┌─────────────────────┐
│ Step 6: VERIFY      │
│ Screenshot store    │
│ and Ads Manager     │
│ Checkpoint: ✓       │
└─────────┬───────────┘
          ▼
┌─────────────────────┐
│ Step 7: REPORT      │
│ Summary of          │
│ everything created  │
└─────────────────────┘

Notice the word "Checkpoint" after every step. That is the key concept that makes this agentic instead of just automated.

Checkpoints: The Safety Net

A checkpoint is a verification step that runs after every major operation. The workflow pauses, checks whether the step actually succeeded, and only moves forward if the answer is yes.

Think of it like a pilot's pre-flight checklist. A pilot does not just flip switches and hope everything works. After each action, they verify the result. Flaps set? Check. Fuel pressure normal? Check. Instruments reading correctly? Check. If any check fails, they stop and investigate before moving on.

Your workflows work the same way. After creating a campaign on Meta, the workflow does not blindly proceed to create the ad creative. It first queries the campaign back from Meta's API and confirms it exists, has the right status, has the correct objective, and has the expected budget. Only then does it move to the next step.

If a checkpoint fails, three things happen:

  1. The workflow stops at that step
  2. It reports exactly what went wrong ("Campaign created but budget was set to 0 instead of 500 EGP")
  3. It offers to retry from that step or let you fix the issue manually and continue

This means a workflow can never silently fail halfway through and leave you with a half-launched product — some pieces in Shopify, no campaign in Meta, and no idea what made it or what did not.

Walking Through Each Step

Let us go deeper into what each step actually does.

Step 1: Shopify Product Check

The workflow starts by verifying the product exists and is ready to be advertised. It queries the Shopify GraphQL API for the product by title (or handle, or SKU — whatever you provided). It checks five things:

  • Does the product exist?
  • Is its status set to ACTIVE (not draft or archived)?
  • Does it have at least one image?
  • Is the price set and greater than zero?
  • Is there inventory in stock?

If the product does not exist, the workflow tells you: "Product 'Winter Ankle Boot' not found in Shopify. Would you like me to create it?" If it exists but is in draft status, it asks whether to activate it. If it has no images, it flags that as a blocker — you cannot run ads without images.

This is decision-making in action. A simple script would crash on a missing product. The workflow adapts.

Step 2: Pricing

If you are running a promotion, this step applies the sale pricing. It reads the product's current price, calculates the discounted price based on your rules (say, 25% off for winter items), sets the compare-at price to the original, and rounds to the nearest 10 EGP — because 742.50 EGP looks sloppy on a product page and 740 EGP looks intentional.

The checkpoint here queries the product back from Shopify and confirms the price was actually updated. This catches a subtle bug that has burned many store owners: Shopify's API sometimes returns a success response even when a variant price update fails due to a validation rule. The checkpoint catches that.

Step 3: Collections

The workflow adds the product to the appropriate collection(s). If you said "winter boots," it looks for existing collections that match — "Winter Collection," "Men's Boots," "New Arrivals," and so on. If you specified a particular collection, it uses that one.

If the collection does not exist, the workflow creates it. It sets the sort order, the description, and publishes it to your online store. This is cross-platform coordination at work — a Shopify operation that sets the stage for the Meta operation that follows.

Step 4: Meta Campaign

Now the workflow switches platforms. It creates a campaign on Meta using the Marketing API. It sets:

  • Campaign objective: Conversions (optimized for purchases)
  • Budget: Either the amount you specified or a sensible default based on your account's typical spend
  • Status: PAUSED — so you can review before it goes live

The campaign is created with the naming convention your account uses (from the rules in your CLAUDE.md), so it appears correctly organized in Ads Manager alongside your other campaigns.

Step 5: Ad Creative

This step creates the ad set and the ad within the campaign. It sets up:

  • Targeting: Based on the product category — age range, gender, location (Egypt), interests
  • Placements: Automatic placements (letting Meta's algorithm decide where to show the ads)
  • Creative: The product images from Shopify, ad copy that highlights the product and any active promotion, and a destination URL with UTM parameters for tracking

The UTM parameters follow a consistent pattern (utm_source=meta&utm_medium=paid&utm_campaign=winter-boots) so every sale from this campaign shows up correctly in your analytics.

Step 6: Playwright Verification

This is the step that separates an agentic workflow from a script. The workflow opens a real browser using Playwright and does two things:

First, it navigates to the product page on your live storefront. It takes a screenshot and checks that the product title, price (with compare-at crossed out if on sale), images, and "Add to Cart" button all appear correctly. If the price is wrong on the site despite the API confirming it, you will see it in the screenshot.

Second, it navigates to Meta Ads Manager (if you are logged in) and screenshots the newly created campaign, confirming it appears in the dashboard with the correct status and settings.

These screenshots are saved to your project folder as timestamped files, giving you a visual record of every launch.

Step 7: Summary Report

Finally, the workflow generates a plain-text report with everything it did.

What Claude Code will show you:

PRODUCT LAUNCH REPORT
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Product:     Winter Ankle Boot - Brown Leather
Shopify ID:  gid://shopify/Product/8234567890
Status:      ACTIVE

Pricing:
  Original:    980 EGP
  Sale price:  740 EGP (25% off)
  Compare-at:  980 EGP

Collections:
  Added to:    Winter Collection, Men's Boots, New Arrivals

Meta Campaign:
  Campaign:    "Winter Ankle Boot - Conversions" (ID: 120215678901234)
  Ad Set:      "Men 25-45 Egypt - Interests" (ID: 120215678905678)
  Ad:          "Winter Ankle Boot - 25% Off" (ID: 120215678909012)
  Budget:      500 EGP/day
  Status:      PAUSED (review and activate when ready)

Verification:
  Storefront:  ✓ Product page renders correctly
  Screenshot:  screenshots/launch-winter-ankle-boot-2026-03-02.png
  Ads Manager: ✓ Campaign visible in dashboard

Links:
  Product page:   https://your-store.myshopify.com/products/winter-ankle-boot
  Ads Manager:    https://adsmanager.facebook.com/adsmanager/manage/campaigns?act=YOUR_AD_ACCOUNT_ID

Warnings:
  None

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

That is the entire launch — from product verification to live campaign — documented, verified, and ready for your review.

12.3 Workflow 2: Daily Operations Check

If the product launch workflow is the most powerful, the daily operations check is the most frequently used. This is the "morning briefing" workflow — the thing you run every day when you sit down with your coffee.

What It Does

The daily operations workflow runs five checks in sequence and combines them into a single report.

Check 1: Shopify Sales

The workflow queries Shopify for yesterday's orders (not today's — you want complete data, not a half-day). It calculates total revenue, order count, average order value, and identifies the top-selling products. It also flags any products that had returns or cancellations.

Check 2: Meta Ad Performance

Next, it queries every active campaign in your Meta ad account for yesterday's performance metrics. For each campaign, it pulls: amount spent, number of purchases, ROAS (return on ad spend), CPA (cost per acquisition), CPM (cost per thousand impressions), CTR (click-through rate), and frequency.

If you are new to these metrics, here is the cheat sheet:

Metric What It Means Good Benchmark (Fashion/Footwear)
ROAS Revenue divided by ad spend. A ROAS of 5x means you made 5 EGP for every 1 EGP you spent. 3x or higher
CPA How much you paid per purchase. Below your average profit margin per order
CPM How much you paid per 1,000 people who saw your ad. 30-80 EGP in the Egyptian market
CTR Percentage of people who clicked your ad after seeing it. Above 1% for feed ads
Frequency Average number of times one person saw your ad. Below 3 for prospecting, below 6 for retargeting

Check 3: Cross-Platform Analysis

This is where the workflow earns its keep. It takes the Shopify revenue and the Meta spend and calculates the blended ROAS — your overall return across all ad spend. This number is the real health metric of your business. Individual campaign ROAS can be misleading (a retargeting campaign always looks great because it targets people who were already about to buy), but blended ROAS tells you the whole truth.

The workflow also compares yesterday's numbers to the previous seven-day average. If revenue dropped 30% but ad spend stayed the same, that is a problem. If revenue spiked but you did not increase spend, something organic happened that you should understand.

Check 4: Issue Detection

The workflow scans for problems that need attention:

  • Low-stock best sellers: Products that are selling well but running out of inventory. If your top seller has 3 units left and it sold 5 units yesterday, you have less than a day before it is out of stock — while you are still paying to advertise it.
  • High-frequency campaigns: If a campaign's frequency exceeds 3 (for prospecting) or 6 (for retargeting), the same people are seeing the ad too many times. Performance degrades quickly after these thresholds. Creative fatigue sets in and CPAs climb.
  • Unprofitable ad sets: Any ad set with a ROAS below 1x for three consecutive days is losing money. One bad day could be noise. Three bad days is a pattern.
  • Budget pacing: If a campaign has spent significantly more or less than its daily budget, something is off — either the audience is too narrow (underspending) or the algorithm is aggressively spending on low-quality placements (overspending).

Check 5: Recommendations

Based on everything it found, the workflow generates actionable recommendations. Not vague suggestions — specific actions with specific numbers.

Example Output

What Claude Code will show you:

DAILY OPERATIONS REPORT — March 2, 2026
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

SHOPIFY (Yesterday)
  Revenue:       24,750 EGP (42 orders)
  Average Order: 589 EGP
  Top Seller:    Classic Chelsea Boot (12 units)
  Returns:       1 order (580 EGP)
  vs 7-day avg:  +8% revenue, +5% orders

META ADS (Yesterday)
  Total Spend:   3,200 EGP
  Purchases:     28
  Blended ROAS:  7.73x
  Best Campaign: Men 25-35 Interest — ROAS 9.2x, 14 purchases
  Worst Campaign: Women Broad — ROAS 1.1x, 2 purchases

  Campaign Breakdown:
  ┌──────────────────────────┬────────┬──────┬───────┬──────┐
  │ Campaign                 │ Spend  │ ROAS │ Purch │ Freq │
  ├──────────────────────────┼────────┼──────┼───────┼──────┤
  │ Men 25-35 Interest       │ 1,200  │ 9.2x │  14   │ 1.8  │
  │ Retargeting - All        │   800  │ 12.1x│   8   │ 3.2  │
  │ Women 25-40 Lookalike    │   600  │ 4.5x │   4   │ 2.1  │
  │ Women Broad              │   600  │ 1.1x │   2   │ 4.7  │
  └──────────────────────────┴────────┴──────┴───────┴──────┘

ISSUES DETECTED
  ⚠️  Low Stock: Winter Ankle Boot — 3 units left (sold 5 yesterday)
  ⚠️  High Frequency: Women Broad — 4.7 (threshold: 3.0)
  ⚠️  Underperformer: Women Broad — ROAS 1.1x for 3 consecutive days

RECOMMENDATIONS
  1. Increase Men 25-35 Interest budget by 20%
     Current: 600 EGP/day → Suggested: 720 EGP/day
     Reason: Consistent ROAS above 8x for 5 days

  2. Pause Women Broad ad set
     Current spend: 600 EGP/day → Saved: 600 EGP/day
     Reason: ROAS 1.1x for 3 days, frequency 4.7

  3. Restock Winter Ankle Boot urgently
     Current stock: 3 units, daily sell rate: ~5 units
     Estimated stockout: today

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

That report would take you 30 to 45 minutes to assemble by hand — logging into Shopify, exporting data, switching to Meta, checking each campaign, doing the math, comparing to last week, spotting the issues, and writing up recommendations. The workflow does it in under two minutes.

And because it runs the same checks every time, it never forgets to look at frequency. It never skips the stock check because it was in a hurry. It never miscalculates the blended ROAS because it forgot to include one campaign. Consistency is a superpower.

12.4 Workflow 3: React to Low Stock

This is a protection workflow. Its job is to stop you from wasting money advertising products that customers cannot buy.

The Problem

Here is a scenario that happens to every store owner who runs ads:

Your Winter Ankle Boot is selling well. You have a campaign driving traffic to it, spending 400 EGP per day. The boot has been converting at a great ROAS. Life is good.

But you did not notice that inventory dropped to 2 units. Today, 10 people click your ad. Two of them buy — great. The other 8 land on the product page, see their size is sold out, and leave. You paid for those 8 clicks and got nothing in return. Worse, those 8 people now have a slightly negative impression of your store — they came expecting to buy and left disappointed.

By tomorrow, the boot is completely sold out. But your campaign is still running. Every click is now pure waste. And Meta's algorithm does not know the product is out of stock — it keeps spending your budget because the ad is still active.

This happens silently. Unless you check inventory every day and cross-reference it with your active ads (manually, across two platforms), you will not catch it until you notice your ROAS has tanked.

The low-stock protection workflow solves this automatically.

How It Works

Step 1: Inventory Scan

The workflow queries Shopify for all active products and their current inventory levels. It flags any product with stock below your threshold. The default threshold is 5 units, but you can set it to whatever makes sense for your business — if you sell high-ticket items that move 1 per day, a threshold of 3 might be right. If you sell fast-moving accessories, maybe 10.

Step 2: Cross-Reference with Meta

For each low-stock product, the workflow searches your active Meta campaigns for any ads that promote that product. It checks:

  • Direct product ads (where the product URL is the destination)
  • Catalog ads (DPA campaigns that include the product in their product set)
  • Collection ads that feature the product

This cross-referencing is the hard part if you are doing it manually. You would need to open each campaign, check each ad's destination URL or product set, and match it against your low-stock list. The workflow does this in seconds by querying the Meta API programmatically.

Step 3: Pause Affected Ads

For any ads that promote low-stock products, the workflow pauses them. It does not delete them — pausing means they can be turned back on instantly once you restock. And it pauses at the most granular level possible: if only one ad in an ad set targets the low-stock product, it pauses that ad, not the entire ad set.

Step 4: Notify

The workflow generates a summary showing exactly what it did.

What Claude Code will show you:

LOW STOCK PROTECTION REPORT — March 2, 2026
═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Products Below Threshold (5 units):

┌─────────────────────────┬───────┬────────────┬───────────┐
│ Product                 │ Stock │ Active Ads │ Action    │
├─────────────────────────┼───────┼────────────┼───────────┤
│ Winter Ankle Boot       │   3   │ 2 ad sets  │ PAUSED    │
│ Classic Chelsea (Brown) │   1   │ 1 ad set   │ PAUSED    │
│ Leather Belt (Black, L) │   4   │ 0 ads      │ No action │
└─────────────────────────┴───────┴────────────┴───────────┘

Paused Details:
  Winter Ankle Boot:
    - Ad Set "Men 25-35 Interest" → Ad "Ankle Boot 25% Off" [PAUSED]
    - Ad Set "Retargeting - All" → includes in product set [PAUSED]

  Classic Chelsea (Brown):
    - Ad Set "Men Boots Collection" → Ad "Chelsea Boot" [PAUSED]

Daily spend saved: ~450 EGP
Paused ads will auto-resume when you restock and run this workflow again.

Products with no active ads (monitor only):
  Leather Belt (Black, L) — 4 units, no advertising exposure

═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Step 5: Monitor for Restock

The next time you run the workflow (which you should include in your daily operations check), it scans again. If a product that was previously low-stock now has inventory above the threshold — because you restocked — the workflow suggests re-enabling the paused ads. It does not do this automatically (you might have paused them for a different reason), but it tells you: "Winter Ankle Boot is back to 25 units. Two ads were paused on March 2. Would you like to resume them?"

This creates a feedback loop:

Product sells well → Stock drops → Ads pause → You restock → Ads resume

No wasted spend. No out-of-stock frustration for customers. No manual monitoring across two platforms.

12.5 Chaining Skills via CLAUDE.md

You now understand what workflows can do. The question is: how do you set them up so they run consistently, every time, without you having to explain the steps from scratch?

The answer is your CLAUDE.md file.

You have been using CLAUDE.md since Section 2. It is the file that Claude Code reads at the start of every session to understand your project — your account IDs, your API configuration, your platform context. But it is also where you define workflow rules.

When you add a workflow definition to CLAUDE.md, you are giving Claude Code a permanent instruction. It reads these rules every time it starts. So instead of explaining a complex multi-step process each session, you explain it once, save it, and then trigger it with a short phrase.

Defining Workflow Rules

Here is what workflow rules look like in your CLAUDE.md:

## Workflow Rules

### Daily Operations
When I say "daily check" or "morning report", run these steps in order:
1. Query Shopify for yesterday's orders — total revenue, order count,
   average order value, top 3 sellers
2. Query Meta for all active campaigns — yesterday's spend, purchases,
   ROAS, CPA, frequency for each
3. Calculate blended ROAS (total Shopify revenue / total Meta spend)
4. Compare all numbers to the 7-day average
5. Check inventory for all products — flag anything below 5 units
6. For any low-stock products, check if Meta ads are running for them
7. Generate a combined report with all findings
8. Add recommendations: budget changes, pauses, restocks

### Low Stock Protection
When I say "stock check" or "protect stock", run these steps:
1. Query all active products from Shopify with inventory levels
2. Flag any product with stock below 5 units
3. For each flagged product, search active Meta ads that promote it
4. Pause any ads promoting low-stock products
5. Report what was paused and estimated daily spend saved
6. Note any previously-paused ads where stock has been replenished

### Product Launch
When I say "launch [product]", run these steps:
1. Search Shopify for [product] — verify it exists, is active, has images
2. Check pricing — apply sale pricing if a promotion is active
3. Add to relevant collections based on product type and tags
4. Create a Meta conversion campaign for [product] with standard targeting
5. Create ad creative using product images from Shopify
6. Take a screenshot of the product page on the storefront
7. Generate a summary report with all IDs and links

That is it. Those rules, added to your CLAUDE.md, turn three complex multi-platform workflows into three simple triggers.

Why This Works

The power of this approach is that CLAUDE.md is read at the start of every session. You do not need to load anything, install anything, or remember anything. You open Claude Code, type "morning report," and the workflow runs exactly as defined.

It also means the workflow is version-controlled. If you change a step — say, you want the daily check to include a competitor price comparison — you edit one file and the change applies to every future session. You have a single source of truth for how your workflows operate.

And because the rules are written in plain English, anyone on your team can read them and understand what each workflow does. There is no code to decipher, no configuration syntax to learn. If your assistant needs to know what "daily check" does, they read the rules. If they want to modify it, they edit the text.

Combining Workflows

Once you have individual workflows defined, you can combine them. Add a rule like:

### Full Morning Routine
When I say "morning" or "good morning", run these workflows in order:
1. Run the Daily Operations workflow
2. Run the Low Stock Protection workflow
3. If any recommendations suggest pausing ads, ask before executing
4. Save the combined report to reports/daily/YYYY-MM-DD.txt

Now your entire morning operations routine — sales check, ad performance, inventory protection, recommendations — runs from two words: "good morning."

Tips for Writing Good Workflow Rules

Be specific about triggers. List exactly which phrases activate the workflow. "When I say 'daily check' or 'morning report'" is better than "When I ask about daily operations." The more specific your triggers, the less chance of Claude Code running the wrong workflow.

Number your steps. Ordered lists make the sequence clear. Claude Code follows them in order and uses the numbers to report progress ("Step 3 of 7: Adding to collections...").

Include decision points. If a step should only run under certain conditions, say so: "If the product has a compare-at price, apply the sale discount. Otherwise, skip pricing." This gives the workflow the flexibility to adapt without defaulting to the wrong behavior.

Specify what to do on failure. Add a note like: "If any step fails, stop and report which step failed and what the error was. Do not continue to the next step." This prevents cascading failures where a broken Step 3 causes Steps 4 through 7 to run with bad data.

Keep each workflow focused. A workflow that tries to do twenty things is fragile and hard to debug. If your "morning routine" grows to 15 steps, break it into smaller workflows and chain them with a master workflow.

Skill Download: Workflow Orchestrator

⚡SKILL DOWNLOAD
Workflow Orchestrator
What it does: Chains multiple operations across Shopify and Meta into autonomous workflows with checkpoints, error recovery, and resumable execution.
Tell Claude Code: "Install the workflow-orchestrator skill from https://raw.githubusercontent.com/mazinzaki911/agentic-ecom-playbook/main/skills/workflow-orchestrator.SKILL.md"
Type /workflow daily-ops in Claude Code
  • Launch products across Shopify and Meta with verification screenshots
  • Run morning ops: sales + performance + inventory in one command
  • Auto-pause Meta ads for low-stock products and report savings
↓ Download Skill

Putting It All Together

Let us zoom out and see what you have built across Sections 11 and 12.

In Section 11, you built individual skills — single-purpose commands that handle one task well. /create-campaign creates a campaign. /check-inventory checks inventory. /update-prices updates prices. Each one is a reliable, tested, reusable tool.

In this section, you built workflows — multi-step sequences that chain those skills together to accomplish real business objectives. "Launch a product" is not a single task; it is seven tasks across two platforms, tied together with checkpoints and verification. "Daily operations check" is not one query; it is five checks combined into an actionable report.

And the glue that holds everything together is your CLAUDE.md file — the plain-English rulebook that tells Claude Code what to do when you say "morning report" or "launch winter boots" or "protect stock."

Here is where you stand now:

What You Have What It Does
10 SOPs (Sections 9-10) Documented step-by-step procedures for your most important tasks
Claude Skills (Section 11) Reusable commands that execute any SOP on demand
Agentic Workflows (This section) Multi-step sequences that chain skills into complete business operations
CLAUDE.md Rules (This section) Persistent workflow definitions that run the same way every session
Checkpoints (This section) Safety nets that verify each step before proceeding
Cross-platform coordination (This section) Shopify and Meta operations combined in a single flow

You have gone from clicking through two admin panels to typing two words in a terminal. The system does not just do what you tell it — it makes decisions, recovers from errors, verifies its own work, and reports back with evidence.

KEY TAKEAWAY
The difference between "assisted" and "agentic" is decision-making. Assisted automation follows your step-by-step commands. Agentic automation follows your goals and figures out the steps itself.
REAL TALK
Start with the daily-ops workflow. It's the safest — read-only checks that don't modify anything. Once you trust the pattern, graduate to product launches and stock protection workflows.
ACHIEVEMENT UNLOCKED
Three agentic workflows running. You can launch products across platforms, run daily operations checks, and automatically protect ad spend when inventory drops — all from single commands.

What Is Next

You now have individual skills, combined workflows, and a system that improves itself. The final section ties it all together with a media buying strategy framework — how to match creatives to destination links, build a testing matrix for audiences and ad formats, allocate budgets across campaigns using real performance data, and use Claude Code to analyze results and recommend your next moves. Section 13 is where the system stops being a tool and starts being a strategic partner.

[End of Section 12]

Section 13: Media Buying Strategy with Claude Code

Time required: 25-35 minutes Difficulty: Intermediate -- assumes you have completed Sections 1 through 12 What you will have at the end: The ability to build your own media buying strategy using Claude Code -- creative-to-link mapping, testing plans, budget allocation, and a continuous analysis loop tailored to your specific business
IN THIS SECTION YOU WILL
  • See practical examples of using Claude Code to build your own media buying strategy
  • Learn how to combine SOPs and skills into a complete campaign workflow
  • Install the Campaign Strategy Builder skill for interactive strategic planning
  • Understand how the analysis loop keeps your strategy evolving

This is where everything comes together. You have the tools (Claude Code + APIs), the procedures (SOPs), the automation (Skills), and the workflows (Agentic chains). This final section shows you how to combine them into a complete media buying operation -- not by telling you what strategy to use, but by showing you how to build your own.

Every business is different. Your budget, your products, your market, your customers -- they are all unique. Instead of prescribing a one-size-fits-all strategy, this section gives you examples of how to use the skills and SOPs you already have to create a strategy that fits your specific situation. You provide the context. Claude Code provides the execution.

13.1 Creative-to-Link Matching

The foundation of any ad campaign is two things: the creative (what the person sees in their feed) and the destination (where they land when they click). Get this pairing wrong, and everything else -- your budget, your targeting, your copy -- is wasted.

Think of it like a restaurant. The creative is the storefront and the menu in the window. The destination is what happens when the customer walks through the door. If the menu says "Italian fine dining" but the customer walks into a fast food joint, they leave. If the ad shows winter boots but the link goes to your homepage, the customer has to hunt for the product. Most of them will not bother.

URL Structure

Every ad needs a destination URL -- the page the user lands on when they click. This should be a specific, relevant page. Not your homepage (unless brand awareness is your explicit goal).

Here is the hierarchy, from best to worst:

Destination Type URL Example When to Use
Specific product page https://yourstore.com/products/winter-boots-classic Single product ads, UGC reviews
Collection page https://yourstore.com/collections/winter-sale Carousel ads, collection ads
Landing page https://yourstore.com/pages/winter-sale Seasonal campaigns with custom design
Homepage https://yourstore.com Brand awareness only

The rule is simple: the ad and the landing page should tell the same story. If your ad shows a specific boot, the link goes to that boot's product page. If your ad shows a collection of winter styles, the link goes to the winter collection. No surprises. No friction.

UTM Parameters

UTM parameters are tags you add to the end of your URL so you can track exactly where your traffic comes from in analytics tools like Google Analytics or Shopify's built-in reports. UTM stands for Urchin Tracking Module -- a name inherited from an old Google analytics product. The name is not important. What matters is that these tags tell you which ad, on which platform, in which campaign, drove each visit and each sale.

The format looks like this:

https://yourstore.com/products/winter-boots?utm_source=meta&utm_medium=paid&utm_campaign=winter-boots-men&utm_content=carousel-lifestyle

Everything after the ? is the tracking information. The actual page the customer sees is the same -- they still land on the winter boots product page. But now your analytics tools know exactly how they got there.

Here is what each parameter means:

Parameter Value Purpose
utm_source meta Which platform sent the traffic (Meta, Google, TikTok, email)
utm_medium paid What type of traffic it is (paid, organic, email, referral)
utm_campaign winter-boots-men Which campaign this belongs to
utm_content carousel-lifestyle Which specific ad creative the person clicked
utm_term interest-shoes Which targeting or audience (optional, but useful for testing)

Why this matters: When you pull analytics next week and see that you made 15 sales from Meta ads, UTMs tell you the full story. Five sales came from the carousel creative in the men's winter campaign. Seven came from the UGC video in the Chelsea boots campaign. Three came from a retargeting ad showing products they viewed. Without UTMs, all you know is "15 sales from Facebook." With UTMs, you know exactly which creative and which campaign drove each one -- and you can make smart decisions about where to put your money next.

The Creative-to-Link Mapping Table

Before you create a single ad, build a mapping table. This is a simple spreadsheet (or even a markdown table in your project) that connects every creative to its destination, its UTM parameters, and its purpose.

Here is an example:

Creative Type Product / Collection Landing Page UTM Campaign UTM Content
Single image - product shot Winter Boots Classic /products/winter-boots-classic winter-boots-classic single-product-shot
Carousel - 3 products Winter Boots Collection /collections/winter-boots winter-collection carousel-3-products
Video - lifestyle All Winter /collections/winter-sale winter-sale video-lifestyle
UGC video - review Classic Chelsea /products/classic-chelsea classic-chelsea ugc-review
DPA - dynamic catalog Various (auto-matched) /products/{product_handle} dpa-retargeting dynamic-catalog

When you ask Claude Code to create a campaign, it uses this mapping to set the destination URL and append the correct UTM parameters automatically. No copy-pasting, no typos, no forgetting the utm_content tag on the third variation.

You can store this mapping table as a JSON file in your project (for example, data/active/creative-link-map.json). Claude Code reads this file when creating campaigns, applies the mapping, and builds the full URL with all tracking parameters. Consistent, error-free, every single time.

13.2 Building a Testing Plan

Here is the hard truth about advertising: nobody knows what is going to work. Not you. Not Meta. Not the most experienced media buyer in the world. The best advertisers are not the ones who guess right -- they are the ones who test systematically and find winners faster than their competition.

Instead of giving you a testing formula to follow, here is how to use Claude Code to build a testing plan that fits your specific products, budget, and market.

Example: Ask Claude Code to Build Your Testing Plan

"I sell premium leather boots in Egypt. My monthly ad budget is 30,000 EGP. My pixel has about 200 purchases tracked so far. Build me a testing plan for my new spring collection -- tell me what audiences to test, what creatives to try, how to split the budget, and how long to run the test before deciding what's working."

Claude Code will ask follow-up questions about your product range, target demographics, and what creative assets you have available. Then it will generate a complete testing plan with:

  • Which audiences to test and why
  • How many creative variations to run
  • How to split budget across variations
  • How long to run before reading results
  • What metrics to watch and what thresholds to use

Example: Different Business, Different Plan

The testing plan for a luxury shoe store is completely different from a discount accessories brand. Here are examples of how the same prompt produces different strategies based on your context:

"I run a budget-friendly accessories store. Monthly ad budget is 5,000 EGP. New pixel with only 20 purchases. What testing approach should I use?"

vs.

"I run a premium women's fashion brand. Monthly ad budget is 100,000 EGP. Mature pixel with 3,000+ purchases. I want to test a new product line launch."

Claude Code tailors the plan to your constraints -- smaller budgets get fewer variations with longer test periods, new pixels get different optimization objectives, and mature accounts get more aggressive testing structures.

Using the Campaign Strategy Builder Skill

For an even more structured approach, use the Campaign Strategy Builder skill:

"/campaign-strategy test"

This skill runs an interactive session that walks you through your product category, budget, pixel maturity, current winners, and goals. Based on your answers, it generates a complete testing plan with the exact Claude Code commands to set everything up.

The point is: you decide what to test based on your business knowledge. Claude Code handles the structure, math, and execution.

13.3 Budget Allocation

Every store has a different budget, different margins, different goals. Instead of following a generic formula, ask Claude Code to build a budget plan for your specific situation.

Example: Ask Claude Code to Allocate Your Budget

"My monthly Meta ad budget is 30,000 EGP. I have 3 active campaigns that are performing well and I want to test a new product line. Help me allocate this budget across testing new campaigns, scaling existing winners, and retargeting. Consider that my best campaign has a 4x ROAS and my pixel has 500+ purchases."

Claude Code will analyze your current performance data, consider your constraints, and generate a budget allocation plan with daily breakdowns. It factors in your pixel maturity, current winners, and goals to produce a plan that makes sense for your business -- not a generic template.

Example: Different Budget, Different Plan

A store spending 5,000 EGP/month gets a very different allocation than one spending 200,000 EGP/month. Try prompts like:

"I only have 8,000 EGP per month for ads. I'm just starting out with a new pixel. How should I allocate this to maximize learning without wasting money?"

vs.

"I have 150,000 EGP per month. Mature pixel, proven campaigns, launching into a new product category. Build me a budget plan."

Example: Ask Claude Code to Rebalance

As campaigns run and performance changes, your allocation should shift too:

"Check my current campaign performance and tell me if my budget allocation still makes sense. Am I spending too much on underperformers? Should I shift more budget to my winners?"

Claude Code will pull your live data, compare performance across campaigns, and recommend specific budget moves -- including which campaigns to increase, decrease, or pause.

The key principle: your budget allocation should be driven by your data, not by a fixed formula. Use Claude Code to make data-driven allocation decisions as your campaigns evolve.

13.4 Analysis and Recommendations

This is where Claude Code earns its keep. Creating campaigns is valuable. But the real edge comes from closing the loop: analyzing what happened, understanding why, and deciding what to do next. Most store owners skip this step -- or do it poorly -- because it requires pulling data from multiple sources, doing math, comparing time periods, and synthesizing it all into clear decisions.

Claude Code does all of that in seconds.

Weekly Analysis Prompt

Here is the prompt you would give Claude Code for a weekly review:

"Analyze my Meta ad performance for the last 7 days. For each campaign, show ROAS, CPA, spend, and purchases. Compare against last week. Flag any campaign where ROAS dropped more than 20%. Recommend which to scale, pause, or refresh creatives for."

What Claude Code Does

When you give that prompt, here is the sequence of events:

  1. Pulls insights from Meta API for all active campaigns over the last 7 days. It fetches spend, revenue, purchases, impressions, clicks, CTR, CPM, and frequency for each campaign and ad set.

  2. Pulls the previous 7 days for comparison. Same metrics, previous period.

  3. Calculates week-over-week changes. ROAS this week vs last week. Spend change. Purchase volume change. Frequency change.

  4. Applies your scaling rules from the SOPs you built in Section 9. These rules are already in your CLAUDE.md and memory:

  5. ROAS above target for 5+ days? Recommend scaling.
  6. ROAS below 1x for 5+ days? Recommend pausing.
  7. Frequency above 3 for cold audiences? Recommend creative refresh.

  8. Budget increased more than 20% recently? Flag for monitoring.

  9. Generates specific, actionable recommendations. Not vague advice -- concrete next steps with numbers:

SCALE:
  - "Men 25-35 Interest" -- ROAS 5.2x (up 12% vs last week)
    Action: Increase daily budget from 600 to 720 EGP

  - "Women Lookalike 1%" -- ROAS 4.1x (stable)
    Action: Create horizontal scale with Broad targeting

PAUSE:
  - "Women Broad" -- ROAS 0.8x for 5 consecutive days
    Action: Pause immediately. Test new creative before reactivating.

REFRESH:
  - "Men Lookalike 2%" -- Frequency 4.2, CTR declining (2.1% -> 1.4%)
    Action: New creative needed. Current creative has been running 18 days.

MONITOR:
  - "Retargeting - Cart Abandoners" -- ROAS 7.8x but spend is only 85 EGP/day
    Action: Audience may be too small. Consider expanding window from 7 to 14 days.

The Continuous Improvement Loop

Zoom out and look at the big picture. Everything you have built across 13 sections forms a single continuous loop:

Test --> Measure --> Analyze --> Decide --> Execute --> Test again
  |                                                        |
  +--------------------------------------------------------+

Here is who does what:

Step Who Does It How
Test You Choose what to test based on strategy and intuition
Measure Claude Code Pull metrics from Meta API automatically
Analyze Claude Code Calculate changes, compare periods, flag anomalies
Decide You Approve, modify, or reject recommendations
Execute Claude Code Scale budgets, pause campaigns, create new ad sets
Test again You + Claude Code You choose the next hypothesis, Claude Code sets it up

You handle the strategic decisions -- what to test, whether to approve a recommendation, when to try something completely new. Claude Code handles the operational work -- pulling data, crunching numbers, making API calls, and documenting everything.

This division of labor is important. You are not being replaced. You are being amplified. A media buyer who can analyze performance and execute changes in 5 minutes instead of 2 hours can run more campaigns, test more hypotheses, and find winners faster than one who is buried in spreadsheets.

How Memory Makes This Smarter Over Time

Remember the memory system from Section 6? Here is where it pays off in a big way.

Every time you run the analysis loop, Claude Code is not starting from scratch. It has access to everything you have learned so far. Your memory might contain entries like:

  • "Interest-based audiences work best for boots -- lookalikes underperform in this category"
  • "Lifestyle creatives outperform product shots 2:1 for women's collections"
  • "Budget increases over 15% trigger learning phase reset -- stick to 12-15% for stable scaling"
  • "Retargeting with a 7-day window has 3x the ROAS of a 30-day window"
  • "UGC videos from male reviewers outperform female reviewers for men's products"
  • "Thursday and Friday have the highest conversion rates -- avoid launching new tests on Monday"

This knowledge feeds into every recommendation. When Claude Code suggests scaling a men's boots campaign with interest-based targeting, it is not just looking at the last 7 days of data. It is drawing on months of accumulated pattern recognition from your specific store, your specific market, and your specific products.

After each analysis cycle, new lessons get saved. The week you discovered that carousel ads work better than single images for collections? That goes into memory. The month you learned that your customers respond to free shipping offers more than percentage discounts? Saved. The time a broad campaign outperformed a carefully targeted one? Documented.

Six months in, your system has a depth of strategic knowledge that would take a new media buyer years to accumulate. And unlike a human media buyer's memory, none of it fades or gets misremembered. It is all there, searchable, and automatically applied.

A Typical Week

With this system running, your week looks like this: Monday, review the testing pipeline with /campaign-strategy. Tuesday through Thursday, campaigns run while you check in briefly each morning for a quick performance snapshot. Friday, run the weekly analysis -- Claude Code generates the report, you approve or modify its recommendations, and it executes the changes. Saturday, new tests launch if needed. Sunday, rest. Total hands-on time: 2-3 hours per week. The rest is automated.
